segments.s677-g9: The exclamation هه opens the line; غرقه is a participial/adjectival predicate meaning “immersed/drenched,” followed by a prepositional phrase introduced by در listing materials (آهن, (و) سیم, و زر). There is no copula — a common poetic verbless construction — so the clause reads literally “Ha! (he) drenched in iron and silver and gold.”
segments.s677-g10: The negative correlative نه ... نه connects two verbless predicates: یاقوت پیدا (lit. ‘ruby visible’) and زرین کمر (‘golden girdle’). پیدا functions as an adjectival/predicative element meaning ‘visible/seen,’ so the phrase means ‘neither any ruby could be seen, nor any golden belt.’
segments.s677-g11: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning “when.” Word order is Subject (بهرام) + Object (رویِ شهنشاه) + Verb (دید), i.e. “when Bahram saw the emperor’s face.” روی here = ‘face.’
segments.s677-g12: شد is the simple past/copular verb ‘became’ (here introducing a change of state). از خشم gives the cause ‘from/with anger,’ and رنگ رخش (‘the color of his countenance’) is the subject whose state changes; ناپدید is the predicate ‘vanished/was gone.’ The clause is inverted (verb before predicate) but reads “the color of his face became vanished from anger.”
segments.s677-g13: ازان پس چِنین is a formula meaning “thereupon/afterward thus”; گفت is the verb ‘said/spoke’ and با introduces the indirect object (با سرکشان = ‘to/with the unruly ones’). سرکشان is a plural agent (the -ان plural) meaning ‘the rebellious/unruly people’ (poetic/archaic).
segments.s677-g14: که introduces a subordinate clause (‘that/who’). این روسپی‌زاده is a compound description ‘this bawd-born’ (روسپی modifying زاده); بدنشان is بدن with the enclitic -شان ‘their,’ here ‘their bodies/persons.’ The clause is the beginning of a disparaging relative/explanatory remark about those addressed.
segments.s677-g15: ز (poetic form of از) marks cause/origin ‘from.’ پستی و کندی are two abstract nouns linked by و (‘baseness/low status and slowness/dullness’). به مردی رسید literally ‘reached to manhood’ — رسید is used in the sense ‘attained’ and مردی here denotes ‘manhood/manliness.’
segments.s677-g16: توانگر شد is a simple past clause ‘became wealthy’ (توانگر as adjective/substantive ‘wealthy’ + شد ‘became’). The conjunction و links the next clause, where رزمگه (battlefield) precedes the verb برکشید. برکشید is a transitive past verb ‘lifted/raised’ (here idiomatically ‘took to/raised the field’ → ‘took the battlefield’).
segments.s677-g441: نبیره is a noun 'scion/descendant' placed first; it takes the following epithets جهانجوی گرگین in apposition (world‑seeking + the proper name Gorgin). منم is the emphatic copula 'I am' placed at the end, giving the whole phrase the meaning 'I am the scion...'.
segments.s677-g442: هم is the particle 'also/too' linking this clause to the previous; آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. آتش (noun) + تیز (adjective) follows normal Persian order (noun then adjective) 'a keen/sharp fire', and برزین is a proper name in apposition to that phrase; منم again is the emphatic 'I am' at the end.
segments.s677-g443: به ایران is a directional phrase 'into/toward Iran'. بران is the fused phrase بر + آن = 'by/with that' and modifies رای بد 'evil counsel/plan'; ساوه‌شاه is the agent 'the king of Saveh'. The clause is arranged for poetic word order and leads into the result introduced by the next segment.
segments.s677-g444: که introduces the result clause 'so that/that'. The construction نه ... نه ... is correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'; تخت is 'throne' and ماند is the verb 'remained/lasted' (applies to both coordinated nouns). مهر here is an emblematic object (sense 'seal/signet' rather than 'love'), joined by و 'and' to کلاه 'hat'.
segments.s677-g445: کند is the narrative/third‑person verb 'he makes/does' (poetic present used narratively). با + زمین 'with/by the earth' together with راست ('straight/level') describes making the آتشکده 'fire‑temple' level with the ground — i.e., razing it to the earth.
segments.s677-g446: The negative correlative نه ... نه ... again: 'neither Nowruz remained nor the Sadeh festival.' ماند is 'remained/lasted' in the sense of 'endure', applying to both جشن items.
segments.s677-g447: Poetic inversion places the predicate 'همه بنده بودند' ('all were servants') before the explicit subject ایرانیان; بودند is the past plural 'they were'. The line means 'all the Iranians were subjugated/servile.'
segments.s677-g448: برین is the fused prepositional phrase بر + این = 'on this' (i.e. on this land); بوم is 'land/homeland'. تا introduces the temporal/result clause 'until'; من is the first‑person subject and ببستم is the poetically expressed first‑person action 'I closed/put an end to' (here glossed by the available sense 'press/close'). میان is 'the middle/the gap' — 'until I closed the middle' meaning until I intervened/filled the gap.
segments.s677-g89: مرا ز آشتی سودمندی بود functions as a topical/oblique phrase plus a predicate: مرا (مَرا, “to/for me”) marks the experiencer, ز آشتی (“from/of reconciliation”) gives the source or cause, سودمندی بود is the main clause with بود as the past/existential verb ‘was’ and سودمندی (‘usefulness/benefit’) as the subject. Word order is cause/beneficiary before the verb, typical of classical Persian: “For me, from reconciliation there was benefit.”
segments.s677-g90: خرد بی‌گمان تاج بندی بود is a nominal sentence where خرد (‘wisdom’) is the subject, بی‌گمان (‘undoubtedly’) is an adverbial modifier, تاج بندی is a compound (تاج + بندی) meaning ‘the binding/cord/fastening of the crown’ or figuratively ‘that which binds the crown’, and بود is the past copula ‘was’. The phrase asserts that wisdom was (indeed) the binding of the crown.
segments.s677-g91: چو بازارگانی کند پادشا: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction (‘when’/‘if’), بازارگانی is a verbal-noun or adverbial notion ‘mercantile activity/commerce’, کند is the present/past habitual verb ‘does/engages in’, and پادشا is the subject ‘the king’. The literal order is ‘When the king does merchanting…’.
segments.s677-g92: ازو شاد باشد دل پارسا: ازو (‘on account of him’/‘because of him’) introduces the beneficiary/cause, شاد is predicate adjective ‘glad’, باشد is the present-subjunctive/stative copula here meaning ‘is’/‘may be’ (classical Persian often uses باشد as a present/final copula), and دل پارسا (‘the pious heart’) is the subject phrase. Word order places the reason before the predicate and subject.
segments.s677-g93: بدو گفت گستهم کای شهریار: بدو is the prepositional pronoun ‘to him/unto him’, گفت here functions as the speech verb ‘said’ (classical texts sometimes compress forms), گستهم is a poetic form (contextually ‘I said’ or a first‑person comment; the manuscript form is irregular but functions as the speaker’s verb phrase), and کای شهریار is the vocative ‘O king’. The whole segment sets up direct speech addressed to the king.
segments.s677-g94: انوشه بدی تا بود روزگار: انوشه is the name being addressed, بدی here denotes wrongdoing/evil (the speaker charges him with evil), تا بود is a temporal clause marker ‘as long as/while there was’, and روزگار means ‘time/the world/age’. Grammatically this is a verbless accusation: ‘Anoushe — evil, as long as there was time,’ i.e. ‘you have done evil for as long as time has existed.’
segments.s677-g95: همی گوهر افشانی اندر سخن: همی is a frequentative/adverb meaning ‘continually/still’, گوهر افشانی is a compound verbal-noun ‘scattering/strewing of jewels’ (i.e. saying brilliant things), اندر سخن places the action ‘in speech’. The segment describes habitual rhetorical brilliance: ‘you keep showering gems in your speech.’
segments.s677-g96: تو داناتری هرچ باید بکن: تو (‘you’) is the subject, داناتری is comparative ‘more knowing/wiser’ (an assertion of superior knowledge), هرچ (‘whatever’) is a free relative, باید is a modal ‘must/ought’, and بکن is the simple verb ‘do’. The clause instructs: ‘You are wiser — do whatever ought to be done.’
segments.s677-g497: This is a vocative phrase addressing someone with three modifiers. ایا (here an archaic form of ای) calls attention to the addressee; مرد is the head noun 'man/person'; بدبخت and بیدادگر are postpositive adjectives describing him ('unfortunate/wretched' and 'oppressive/cruel'). The word order is typical Persian: vocative + noun + adjectives.
segments.s677-g498: همه روزگارت is a possessive phrase meaning 'your whole days/fortune' (همه + روزگارت). به کژی is a prepositional phrase 'into/to crookedness/wrongdoing' (به + noun). مبر is the negative imperative 'do not take/do not make' governing the clause; together the clause means 'do not turn all your days toward crookedness.'
segments.s677-g499: ز خشنودی ایزد is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'for/from the approval of God' (ز + noun + possessor). اندیشه کن is an imperative formed with the verbal noun اندیشه + the light verb کن, meaning 'think/reflect' or 'be mindful of.' The imperative tone directs the addressee to act for God's approval.
segments.s677-g500: This is a coordinated imperative: two objects connected by و followed by the same light verb کن. خردمندی و راستی پیشه is 'wisdom and uprightness as a practice/profession' (پیشه here = 'practice/adopt as a craft'), and کن is the imperative 'do/adopt.' The structure means 'make wisdom and righteousness your practice.'
segments.s677-g501: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/for' explaining the reason. این بر من و تو همی بگذرد is '[this] will pass over/upon me and you': این = 'this/the present matter', بر = directional/prepositional 'upon/over', من و تو = 'me and you', همی + بگذرد gives a classical continuous/future sense 'will pass.' بگذرد is the present/subjunctive form meaning 'to pass.'
segments.s677-g502: زمانه دم ما همی بشمرد is a compact clause: زمانه (Time/Fortune) is the subject, دم ما = 'our breath/moment' is the object, همی + بشمرد is the verb phrase 'will count/number.' The sense is proverbial: Time will reckon our breaths (i.e., our brief lives are being counted).
segments.s677-g503: که here introduces reported speech or a proverb-like remark. گوید in the original context is a third-person present 'people say / (one) says,' but the provided senses list does not include the verb sense, so the matching sense cannot be selected from the given options. کژی به از راستی is a comparative phrase 'crookedness is better than honesty/rightness' where به از functions as 'better than' in classical usage; word order places the contrasted terms next to one another.
segments.s677-g504: This is a rhetorical question: به کژی 'toward/into crookedness' (به + noun) modifies دل بیاراستی 'you have set/adorned the heart.' چرا introduces 'why' and directs the reproach: literally 'why have you set your heart on crookedness?' The verbal form بیاراستی here is a past/preterite or perfective sense 'you have made/arranged' (the provided senses for بیاراستی do not include this verbal meaning, so no listed sense matches).
segments.s677-g513: The clause begins with the relative/indefinite particle 'که' introducing 'هرکس' (whoever). The second 'که' links the noun to the verb phrase 'برگردد' (turns back/away) — an archetypal Classical Persian subordinate clause. Word order is subject (هرکس) + verb (برگردد) + prepositional phrase (از دین پاک).
segments.s677-g514: 'ز یزدان ندارد به دل بیم و باک' is a verbless/result clause: 'ندارد' is the present-tense verb 'does not have' with the pronominal complement understood from context. 'ز یزدان' (from God) is the source, 'به دل' marks the internal location ('in the heart'), and 'بیم و باک' are two synonymous nouns 'fear and dread/concern' coordinated by 'و'.
segments.s677-g515: 'به سالی' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'each year/annually.' 'همی' is the archaic habitual/progressive marker (classical Persian) attached to the verb 'داد' (gives). 'بایدش' is an impersonal modal ('it is necessary' / 'one ought'), with the clitic '-ش' = 'him'; 'پند' is the object 'advice/counsel.' Word order here is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: literal order 'بایدش پند به سالی همی داد' → 'one ought to give him advice yearly.'
segments.s677-g516: 'چو' here functions conditional ('if'). 'پندش' = 'his counsel' (پند + clitic -ش). 'نباشد' is the classical negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'is not' or 'does not exist/is not present' (not an imperative here). 'ورا' = 'to him' (alternative dative form), and 'سودمند' is a predicate/adjective 'profitable/useful.' The clause thus reads 'if his counsel is not useful to him.'
segments.s677-g517: 'ببایدش' is the impersonal/modal expression 'it must be' / 'he must,' where the clitic '-ش' picks out 'him.' It is followed by the verbal noun/infinitive 'کشتن' ('to kill') functioning as the complement. 'به فرمان شاه' is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by the command of the king' (here 'به' = 'by').
segments.s677-g518: This is a continuation: 'فکندن' is an infinitive/noun form 'to throw/cast'; 'تن' = 'body' (used for the person); 'پرگناهش' = 'his sinful' (پرگناه + clitic -ش). 'به راه' is a locative phrase 'onto the road' ('به' = toward/onto). Together: 'to cast his sinful body onto the road.'
segments.s677-g519: 'چو' is the temporal/conditional 'when/if.' The preposition 'بر' here marks the target of suspicion ('بر شاه' = toward the king). 'گیتی' (the world/people) is the grammatical subject, 'شود' is the present of 'to become,' and 'بدگمان' is the predicate adjective 'suspicious/distrustful.' The clause means 'when the world becomes suspicious of/toward the king.'
segments.s677-g520: Repeating the earlier modal construction: 'ببایدش' ('he must'), followed by the infinitive 'کشتن' ('to kill'). 'هم' = 'also/likewise,' and 'اندر زمان' = 'at that time' / 'immediately then' (an adverbial phrase). The whole means 'he also must be killed at that time.'
segments.s677-g577: ورا (vora) is an enclitic/object pronoun phrase meaning “to him” placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense reporting verb “said/told” and خسرو is the subject (Khosrow). که introduces reported content (“that …”). دارا (Dārā) here is a proper name; بمرد is an older/poetic past/subjunctive form meaning ‘died’ or ‘had died’ used to report the fact. The whole clause is a reporting clause: “Khosrow said to him that Dārā had died.”
segments.s677-g578: نه ... سپرد is a negated verbal clause: نه here negates the action. تاج بزرگی = “the great crown” (تاج ‘crown’; بزرگی qualifying it as great/grand). به ساسان سپرد is a prepositional phrase with سپرد (past of سپردن) ‘entrusted/handed over’ and به marking the recipient (to Sasan). The clause says he did not hand the great crown to Sasan.
segments.s677-g579: This is a conditional clause: اگر = ‘if’; بخت = ‘fortune/fate’; گم شد is a simple past construction ‘was lost’ (گم ‘lost’ + شد). کجا شد is a rhetorical question ‘where did/does it become/go’; نژاد = ‘lineage/race’. The clause asks rhetorically what will become of the lineage if fortune disappears.
segments.s677-g580: نیاید is a present/future-negative form ‘will not come/appear’. ز + گفتار = ‘from/from the speech/utterance’. بیداد داد is a compound where بیداد = ‘injustice/oppression’ and داد is the verb ‘gave’ (i.e. ‘give injustice’). The clause asserts that no lineage will result from words that dispense injustice.
segments.s677-g581: بدین هوش is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘with/by this intelligence’ (بدین = archaic/poetic ‘with this’ = به + این). The list continues with و این رای ‘and this counsel/plan’ and و این فرهی ‘and this nobility/virtue’. The three noun phrases are coordinated and act as the circumstance/means for the following verb.
segments.s677-g582: بجویی همی is a present/continuous second-person form ‘you seek/still seek’ (بجویی = جستن/جوییدن). تخت شاهنشهی = ‘the imperial throne’ (تخت ‘throne’; شاهنشهی ‘king-of-kingship/imperial rule’). The verb follows its adverbial means, giving the meaning ‘you seek the imperial throne by/with these qualities.’
segments.s677-g583: This is a sequence of simple past actions: بگفت ‘he said/told’, و بخندید ‘and laughed’, و برگشت زوی ‘and turned away from him/from there’ — verbs joined by و. برگشت is intransitive ‘returned/turned’, and زوی (‘from him/from there’) marks the source/direction of the turning.
segments.s677-g584: سوی لشکر خویش = ‘toward his army’ (سوی = toward; لشکر = army; خویش = his/one’s own, here split into خوی + ش in the word list). بنهاد روی is a poetic expression ‘put/laid (his) face’ i.e. ‘turned his face’ (بنهاد = put/placed, روی = face). The clause describes his directing his face toward his forces.
segments.s677-g137: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning "when" (temporal). The clause has inverted poetic word order: مهمان (guest) is the logical subject, به خوان تو آید = "comes to your table" (به = toward/into, خوان = banquet/table, تو = your), and ز دور is a postposed adverbial "from afar." آید is the present/habitual/gnomic form "comes."
segments.s677-g138: تو is the emphatic subject "you." دشنام سازی is a verbal phrase formed from the noun دشنام plus سازی ("making/performing"), so here it means "you utter/perform insults." به هنگام is a temporal prepositional phrase "at the time of," and سور is "feast/banquet." The whole gives the present/habitual action: "you utter insults at feast-time."
segments.s677-g139: نه ... بود is the negation plus past copula: "was not." آیین شاهان = "the custom/practice of kings." زین نشان is a compact, somewhat poetic phrase; in literal syntax it would read "of/from this sign/token," but here it functions to point to "this manner/mark" (poetic usage). Word order places the predicate before the emphasized referent.
segments.s677-g140: نه introduces the second member of the correlative negation: "nor those riders..." آن سواران = "those horsemen/riders." گردنکشان is an adjectival/epithetic plural meaning "proud/rebellious ones," describing those riders; the verb is omitted by ellipsis (copula understood from the previous clause).
segments.s677-g141: نه ... کرد و نه ... is a double negative construction: "neither ... did ... nor ..." تازی (Tāzī) refers to Arabs; چنین کرد = "did thus/so." The second noun پارسی (Persian) is coordinated by و/نه and lacks an explicit verb (ellipsis), understood from the first clause.
segments.s677-g142: اگر introduces a hypothetical "if." بشمری is 2nd-person subjunctive/imparative sense of شمردن "you were to count/reckon" (poetic optative). سال = "years" (object of counting). صدبار سی is a numeric phrase (literally "a hundred times thirty"); the clause means "even if you counted the years a hundred times thirty," i.e. an emphatic hyperbole. The tense/mood is hypothetical/conditional.
segments.s677-g143: ازین (from this) + ننگ دارد = "has shame about this" or "considers this a disgrace." The phrase places the prepositional phrase first for emphasis; خردمند مرد (wise man) is the subject, here postposed for poetic effect: literally, "From this shame has the wise man."
segments.s677-g144: به گرد در = a set of adverbials meaning "about/around in" and ناسپاسی = "ingratitude/ungratefulness." مگرد is a prohibitive/exceptional particle here (poetic imperative) meaning roughly "do not (be)" or "avoid" — the line issues a negative injunction: "do not go about in ingratitude." Word order places the admonition after the general statement.
segments.s677-g249: نبوده‌ست is an archaic/perfect negative phrase meaning 'there has not been' (past perfective sense). جز functions as 'except / nothing but' limiting the statement. جادوی پرفریب is a noun phrase 'deceptive sorcery' (جادوی = 'magic, sorcery' in construct; پرفریب = adjective 'deceptive'), so the whole segment says 'there has been nothing but deceptive sorcery'.
segments.s677-g250: که is the relative/connector 'that'. اندر is the older preposition 'in / on / within'. بلندی here is the noun 'height, high place' (note: the supplied lemma/senses do not match the common meaning, so use sense index -1). نمودت is the verb/verb-stem نمود with the enclitic object -ت ('you'), i.e. 'made (it) appear / showed (to) you' (past). نشیب is a noun 'descent' (the thing that is shown). Together: 'that in high places made your descent appear.'
segments.s677-g251: به + noun phrase (به شاخی) marks location/target: 'on/at a little branch / on a twig' (classical usage of به is flexible). همی is the archaic presentive particle indicating continued/ongoing action (similar to modern می-), so همی یازی is an older imperfective/present sense 'still plays / toys'. امروز is temporal 'today'. دست is literal 'hand' (here the agent's hand 'your hand'). Word order places the locative phrase before the verb, typical of Persian poetic diction.
segments.s677-g252: که introduces a subordinate clause 'for/which'. برگش and بارش are nouns with the possessive enclitic -ش ('its leaf', 'its fruit/burden'); the supplied senses for برگش/bar do not match this morphological reading, so I mark the sense indices accordingly (-1 where senses don't fit). بود is the past copula 'was'. زهر is 'poison'. و is 'and'. کبست is an obscure/archaic lexical item here used as a predicate for the fruit; treated as an opaque term in the translation. Overall: 'which had its leaf of poison and whose burden/fruit was کبست.'
segments.s677-g253: نجسته‌ست is an archaic perfect/passive-form meaning 'has been defiled / besmirched' (here negated by the following هرگز 'never'). هرگز 'never' negates the verb. تبار تو این places 'your lineage' followed by the demonstrative 'this' (این) as the object/topic: 'your lineage has never been defiled by this.' Word order is topic (تبار تو) + comment (نجسته‌ست هرگز این).
segments.s677-g254: نباشد here is an optative/subjunctive negative meaning roughly 'may it not be / let there not be'. به introduces the beneficiary/experiencer 'to the seeker' (جوینده). بر آفرین = 'cause for praise / acclamation' (بر = 'for/upon', آفرین = 'praise, acclamation'). The clause as a whole is a wish: 'may the seeker have no reason for praise (on that account)'.
segments.s677-g255: تو را places 'you' in the accusative/topic position with the object marker را. ایزد is the subject 'God / the deity'. این فر و برزت is the compound object: این فر = 'this farr (divine royal glory)', برزت = 'your exaltation/high station' (برز + possessive -ت). نداد is the past verb 'did not give' (negated by the preceding structure/context). The clause literally: 'God did not give you this farr and your exaltation.'
segments.s677-g256: نیاری is used as 'companion / helper' here (نیار + possessive sense), introduced by ز 'from / by' to mark source/agent: 'from/through a companion'. گرگین is an adjective 'wolf-like / wolf-born' (not a proper-name reading here). میلاد = 'birth', and یاد = 'memory / remembrance' (here functioning as a final noun modifying the idea). The phrase means roughly 'not given because of / through a companion of wolfish birth's memory' — i.e. the gift was not due to such a low/ignoble origin.
segments.s677-g25: همان is a demonstrative emphasiser here ('that, the same'). زخم is the noun 'wound' and is linked to the following proper name گوپال (appositive or genitive relation). وباران appears here as a fused form representing 'و باران' ('and a shower/rain'); تیر ('arrow') completes that noun phrase. The phrase strings two coordinated objects: 'that wound of Gopāl' and 'the rain/shower of arrows'.
segments.s677-g26: خروش ('roar/uproar') is the subject noun and یلان ('heroes') qualifies whose cry it is (genitive relationship implied). بر functions as a preposition 'on/against/upon' linking the cry to its targets. The sequence ده و دار و گیر lists the targets (villages/houses/strongholds) coordinated by و; each word is a noun in series receiving the action of the preceding phrase.
segments.s677-g27: ندارد is the third-person singular negative of داشتن ('does not have/there is not'); here it introduces existence/lack. به آوردگه is a prepositional phrase ('at/to the gathering-place'—آوردگه 'place of coming/assembly'). پیل پای literally 'elephant foot' is the nominal object describing what is lacking; Persian places modifiers and compounds in this order.
segments.s677-g28: چو ('when') introduces a temporal clause. من با سپاه is the agent phrase ('I with an army') and اندر آیم ز جای is the verbal core: آیم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I come/advance', اندر marks location 'into/among', and ز جای ('from my place') gives origin; the verb comes at the end as typical in Persian subordinate clauses.
segments.s677-g29: ز here means 'because of' or 'from' (poetic). آواز من ('my voice/shout') is the cause; کوه ریزان is a compound nominal phrase meaning 'mountain-scattering' (i.e. mountains are scattered/collapse). شود is the third-person singular present/subjunctive/passive 'become/are made' completing the result clause.
segments.s677-g30: هژبر is a proper name; دلاور is an epithet 'valiant/brave' modifying the name. گریزان شود is a verbal phrase: گریزان is a participial/adjectival form 'fleeing/put to flight' and شود is the auxiliary 'becomes/is made', so together 'Hežbar the brave takes flight' or 'is put to flight.'
segments.s677-g31: The repeated به marks instrumental or directional phrases ('with/unto'): به خنجر 'with the dagger', به دریا 'toward/on the sea'. بر before افسون gives the sense 'upon/against' the object of the following verb. افسون کنیم is first-person plural subjunctive/intentional 'we will enchant/perform a charm' (کردن + noun use), so the clause expresses an intended violent or magical action with instruments and direction.
segments.s677-g32: بیابان is the direct object ('the wilderness/desert'). سراسر 'throughout/entire' and پر از خون 'full of blood' form a predicative phrase describing the intended result. کند/کنیم at the end ('we will make') is the verb of causation placing the whole result clause as what the speaker vows to do; object-before-verb order is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s677-g97: تو پردادی و بنده بیدادگر: literal word order is subject (تو) + verb (پردادی) followed by coordinating و and a noun phrase. پردادی is the past tense/preterite (gave; here with the intensive sense “gave generously/bestowed”). بنده is a humble self-reference (“your servant”/“I, the servant”) and بیدادگر is an adjectival/nominal description “unjust/cruel,” describing that servant. The clause contrasts the addressed person’s action (you bestowed) with the state of the (humble) speaker.
segments.s677-g98: تو پرمغزی و او پر از باد سر: again two contrasted coordinated halves with تو (you) as subject of the first predicate and او (he) of the second. پرمغزی is a compound adjective “full of marrow/understanding” (i.e. full of intellect). پر + از functions as the adjective phrase “full of …” (پر از X = “full of X”), so پر از باد سر = “full of wind of the head,” an idiom meaning puffed-up or vain. The coordinating و links the two characterizations.
segments.s677-g99: چو بشنید خسرو بپیمود راه: چو + past verb (بشنید) gives a temporal subordinate clause “when he heard.” The proper subject follows the verb (typical older/poetic word order): بشنید (he heard) then خسرو (Khosrow) — Khosrow is the one who heard. بپیمود راه is the main action after hearing: بپیمود is a past form meaning ‘traversed/undertook’ and راه is its object/goal “the road / set out.”
segments.s677-g100: خرامان بیامد به پیش سپاه: خرامان is an adverbial participial/adjective meaning ‘with a stately/proud gait’ and modifies the verbal predicate بیامد (came). به پیش + noun = ‘into the presence of / before’ the following noun سپاه (the army/host). The clause describes Khosrow’s entrance — he came, stately, before the army.
segments.s677-g101: بپرسید بهرام یل را ز دور: بپرسید is past (‘he asked’). The direct object construction بهرام یل را places بهرام یل (Bahram, the hero) as the thing asked about, and the enclitic را marks it as definite/object. ز دور (‘from afar’) is an ablative/locative phrase indicating the question came from a distance or that Bahram was enquired about from afar.
segments.s677-g102: همی جست هنگامه رزم سور: همی is a frequentative/continuous/emphatic particle (here marking a vivid, immediate action), followed by the past jussive/preterite-like verb جست ‘he leapt/sprung.’ هنگامه رزم is a genitive/construct-like sequence meaning ‘the tumult/moment of battle.’ سور here follows and functions adjectivally/intensifyingly with رزم in the rhythm of the verse; the whole phrase describes an energetic springing into the clamor of combat.
segments.s677-g103: ببه هرام گفت ای سرافراز مرد: ببه is an interjection/exclamation (“Bravo!” or “Well done!”). The following token here is the speaker’s name (intended as بهرام, Bahram) followed by گفت ‘said,’ which introduces direct speech. ای سرافراز مرد is a vocative: ای + adjective + noun = “O exalted/proud man.” Note: word order places the exclamation and speaker name before گفت, then the vocative addresses the addressee.
segments.s677-g104: چگونه‌ست کارت به دشت نبرد: چگونه‌ست is a fused interrogative/copula meaning ‘how is it’ or ‘how goes.’ کارت is the possessed noun ‘your work/deed’ (here ‘your fighting/action’). به دشت = ‘in the plain/field’ (locative), and نبرد is the noun ‘battle/contest,’ specifying the sphere of the action. Together this forms a direct question: “How fares your action in the field of battle?”
segments.s677-g337: This clause begins with the preposition 'ز' (from) introducing origin/place. 'شهری که ویران شد' is a relative clause: 'شهری' (city/urban) is modified by 'که ویران شد' (that was ruined). 'شد' here marks the change/state (‘became/was ruined’). 'اندر جهان' ('in the world') is a locative phrase placed after the verb, typical of Persian poetic word order.
segments.s677-g338: 'به جایی' is a direction/target phrase ('to a place'). The relative clause 'که درویش باشد نهان' follows: 'که' = 'where/that', 'درویش' = 'a dervish', 'باشد' is the (optative/subjunctive/present) third‑person form of 'to be' used poetically ('be / may be / is'), and 'نهان' is the predicate adjective 'hidden'. Together they give 'to a place where a dervish is hidden.'
segments.s677-g339: This clause uses the verb phrase 'توانگر کنم' ('I will make [someone] wealthy') with 'کنم' as the first‑person singular present/subjunctive of 'to do/make'. 'مرد درویش را' is the definite object (marked by 'را') meaning 'the dervish man' — object follows the verb here because of poetic inversion but 'کنم' governs it: 'I will make the dervish man rich.'
segments.s677-g340: The second coordination omits a repeated verb; 'پراگنده' (scattered) functions as the predicate participial/adjectival concept governed by the earlier 'کنم' (i.e. 'I will make/leave scattered'), joined by 'و' to add another object 'مردم خویش را' ('my/one's own people', object marked by 'را'). So the sense is 'and (I will) scatter/disperse my people.'
segments.s677-g341: 'همه خارستانها' (all the thorny thickets/wastelands) is the object phrase; 'کنم' ('I will make') is the verb. 'چون بهشت' is a simile/adverbial phrase ('as/like paradise') modifying the result. The order puts object + verb + simile (poetic inversion) and means 'I will turn all the thorn‑thickets into something like paradise.'
segments.s677-g342: 'پر از' is a fixed combination meaning 'full of'; here it takes a coordinated list 'مردم و چارپایان و کشت' ('people and quadrupeds and crops'). The list after 'پر از' names what fills that paradise‑like place. 'کشت' is used here in the sense of cultivation/fields rather than killing.
segments.s677-g343: 'بمانم' is first‑person singular of 'ماندن' used optatively/subjunctively here ('may I remain' / 'let there remain'). 'یکی خوبی' is the direct object: 'one goodness' (i.e., one good deed or one good thing). 'اندر جهان' is the locative 'in the world.' Together: 'Let one good thing remain in the world.'
segments.s677-g344: This is a subordinate clause introduced by 'که' ('so that/that'). 'نامم‌پس از مرگ' expresses 'my name after death' (classical ordering: 'نامم‌پس' + 'از مرگ' = 'after my death'). 'نبود نهان' is a negative past/state construction meaning 'was not hidden' or 'is not hidden' — together: 'that my name will not be hidden after death.'
segments.s677-g409: "چه" is the interrogative 'what' placed at the start; "گفت" is the past verb 'said' (here introducing reported speech); the following noun phrase "آن خردمند شیرین سخن" (that + wise + sweet‑speaking) is the person who spoke. Word order is verb + nominal subject/description, typical of older Persian poetry.
segments.s677-g410: "که‌گر" = که اگر = 'that if' (a fused conditional marker). "بی‑بنان" (lit. 'without fingers') here means someone powerless/without means; the enclitic "را" marks that phrase as a definite/affected noun (object/recipient). "نشانی" = 'a sign/indication', and "ببن" is an archaic poetic form of ببین/ببین ('see' / 'show') used as the verb in the conditional clause. Together: 'that if you (one) show a sign to the powerless...'.
segments.s677-g411: The prepositional phrase "به فرجام کار" ('at/to the end/outcome of the affair') is fronted; "آیدت" = آید + ت (verb 'comes' with enclitic 'to you') means 'will come/befall you'; "رنج و درد" are the things that befall (suffering and pain). The order places circumstance first, then verb + clitic, then the sufferers as the logical object/experiencers.
segments.s677-g412: "به گرد در" functions idiomatically as 'around/among' (به گرد + در = among/about in); "ناسپاسان" is the plural/agentive form 'the ungrateful/thankless ones'; "مگرد" here is a prohibitive 'do not go/turn' (classical usage 'مگرد' = do not go/turn away/visit), so the clause warns 'do not frequent/associate with the ungrateful.'
segments.s677-g413: "دلاور شدی" = 'you became valiant' (دلاور noun + شدی 'became'); the following adjectives "تیز و برتر منش" modify the subject: تیز 'keen/sharp', و 'and', برتر منش 'of superior disposition/character' (برتر qualifying منش). In older Persian the copula شدی follows the predicate noun and is completed by adjectival complements.
segments.s677-g414: "ز" here is the poetic preposition 'from'; "بد گوهر" = 'bad/evil essence' (a compound noun meaning bad stock/nature); "آمد" is the past 'came'; "تو را" marks the one affected ('to you' with the object marker); "بدکنش" is a compound meaning 'bad‑doer' or 'evil disposition/trait' — syntactically the subject that 'came' to you. Word order is idiomatic older Persian: 'From bad essence came to you a bad-doer (i.e. an evil trait).'
segments.s677-g415: "تو را" is the clitic-marked object 'you'; "کرد" is the verb 'made' (causative/make); "سالار گردنکشان" is the title given — سالار 'commander' with the genitive/descriptor گردنکشان 'of the neck‑breakers/proud ones' (i.e. rebellious or arrogant people). The phrase means 'he made you commander of the proud/rebels.'
segments.s677-g416: "شدی" = 'you became'; "مهتر" = 'chief/headman'; "اندر زمین" = 'in/throughout the land'; "کشان" (a participial/attributive form from کشیدن) literally 'dragging/leading' used here as 'roaming/leading across' — so the clause describes the subject as having become a foremost leader roaming the land. Word order: verb 'شدی' + predicate 'مهتر' followed by the adverbial phrase "اندر زمین کشان."
segments.s677-g241: This is a nominal phrase describing the agent. ستمگاره is a compound noun 'one who practices oppression' (subject/agent). دیویست is diو + -ست (the old copula 'is' fused) giving 'is a demon' or functioning adjectivally 'demonic'; با خشم و زور is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with anger and strength' modifying the agent. Word order (noun + adjective/copula + with-phrase) is typical of classical Persian descriptive lines.
segments.s677-g242: کزین گونه is a prepositional phrase ('کز' = from, here 'in this way') meaning 'thus/in this manner'. چشم تو را is the definite object (چشم = sight/eye, تو = 'your', را = object marker) placed before the verb as is common in Persian; کرد کور is a compound where کرد (past 'made/caused') takes the adjective کور 'blind' as resultative complement: 'made (you) blind.'
segments.s677-g243: به جای خرد is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in place of wisdom' (به جای = 'instead of / in place of', خرد = 'wisdom'). خشم و کین are the nouns 'anger and hatred/vengeance' that serve as the direct objects of the verb; یافتی is the second-person past 'you found/obtained' (یافتن) and closes the clause.
segments.s677-g244: ز دیوان کنون is another prepositional adverbial: ز = 'from', دیوان here refers to 'the devils' (classical plural of دیو) — note the provided lemma senses do not list this reading, see senses field. کنون = 'now'. آفرین is a noun 'praise/acclamation' placed before the verb, and یافتی repeats the past 'you obtained/received'. The clause structure is object/adverbial + verb, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s677-g245: تو را is the indirect/direct object 'for you' with را marking affectedness. خارستان شارستانی is a compound description meaning literally 'a thorny (خارستان) [of] the town (شارستانی)' — here it conveys 'turned your town into thickets/ a thorn-waste' with شارستانی qualifying the place; نمود is the past verb 'made/caused' (نمودن). The noun phrase precedes the verb, as usual.
segments.s677-g246: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (introducing an item), دوزخی modifies بوستانی to give 'a hellish garden' (دوزخی = 'hellish, of hell'; بوستانی = 'garden-like / a garden'). نمود again is the past verb 'made/created', completing the clause: 'he made a certain hellish garden.'
segments.s677-g247: چراغ خرد is a noun phrase 'the lamp of wisdom' (چراغ = lamp/light, خرد = wisdom). پیش چشمت is a locative phrase 'before/in front of your eyes' (پیش = before, چشمت = 'your eye' with the possessive suffix). بمرد is an archaic/poetic past-form meaning 'died' (here third-person past): 'the lamp of wisdom died before your eyes.'
segments.s677-g248: ز جان و دلت روشنایی ببرد: ز = 'from'; جان و دل‑ت = 'your life and your heart' (دل + ت as the 2nd-person suffix; the list separates ت). روشنایی = 'light/illumination' is the object; ببرد is the verbal form of بردن 'to take/carry away' (here perfective/past narrative sense 'took away'). The construction means 'it took the light away from your life and heart.'
segments.s677-g569: بدو گفت is the light dative + verb order: بدو (“to him/unto him”) precedes گفت (past 3sg “said”), so “he said to him.” خسرو is the speaker (subject) appearing after the verb, a common Persian order. که‌ای is an archaic/exclamatory addressing particle (که ای → “O/you who are”), and بدکنش is a vocative noun ‘ill‑doer’ addressing the addressee.
segments.s677-g570: نه introduces negation “not.” از تخم ساسان is a genitive/origination phrase “of the seed/stock of Sasan” (از = “of,” تخم = “seed/lineage,” ساسان = the name Sasan). شدی is the past 2nd‑person form of شدن (“you became” / copular “you are” in sense), and بر منش is a prepositional phrase meaning “with respect to character” (بر + منش), so the clause says the addressee does not belong to Sasan’s stock in character.
segments.s677-g571: دروغست is a fixed predicate meaning ‘it is a lie’ placed before the phrase it qualifies. گفتار تو is the noun phrase ‘your speech’ (گفتار = speech, تو = you/your). سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning ‘completely’ or ‘through and through’ and functions adverbially to intensify the preceding predicate.
segments.s677-g572: This clause states a general judgment. سخن گفتن is a verbal noun phrase ‘to speak/speaking’; کژ (literally ‘crooked’ or ‘wrong’) qualifies the manner of speaking; نباشد is a classical present/subjunctive/negative copula meaning ‘is not’ or ‘ought not to be’ here used to negate the predicate; هنر (‘art/skill’) is the predicate: ‘speaking crookedly is not skill.’
segments.s677-g573: This is a past description of origin and condition. تو is the subject ‘you’; از introduces origin ‘from/among’; بدتنان (lit. ‘bad‑bodied/ill‑favored ones’) is a plural or collective noun meaning ‘ill‑favored people’ or ‘base folk’; بودی is the second‑person singular past ‘you were’; و introduces the coordinate predicate بی‌بنان (‘without means’ or ‘deprived’), forming ‘you were of ill‑favored people and lacked means.’
segments.s677-g574: This negative clause states lineage and lack of entitlement. نه negates the following clause; از تخم ساسان ‘from Sasan’s seed’ expresses descent/lineage (تخم = seed/origin); رسیدی is the second‑person past ‘you reached/attained/come’ used here of descent or attainment; به نان literally ‘to bread’ means ‘to (earn) livelihood/bread.’ Together: ‘you did not come from Sasan’s seed to earn bread,’ i.e. you are not of Sasanian stock entitled to sustenance.
segments.s677-g575: Again an introductory reporting clause. بدو ‘to him’ is dative, گفت is the reporting verb ‘said’ (here treated as past tense), and بهرام names the speaker. کاندر is an archaic fused form of که اندر (‘that in/wherein’) used to introduce the content/location clause; جهان = ‘the world’ or ‘this life’ specifies the domain: ‘that in the world…’
segments.s677-g576: This is the content clause. شبانی ‘shepherding’ (or ‘being a shepherd’) is the subject; ز (poetic form of از) marks source ‘from Sasan’; نگردد is a classical negative present/subjunctive/impersonal form of گشتن ‘to become’ meaning ‘does not become / will not be’ (here ‘will not be made’); نهان ‘hidden’ is the predicative adjective placed after the verb: ‘shepherding from Sasan will not be hidden.’
segments.s677-g489: نوشتی is a 2nd-person singular past form 'you wrote'; Persian places verb after object phrases, so 'همان نام من بر درم' is the direct object/adjunct that the verb comments on. همان ("that very/the same") emphasizes which name; نام من is a genitival noun phrase "my name"; بر + درم means "on my dirham" (بر = on, درم here = dirham/small coin).
segments.s677-g490: زگیتی (from/of the world) begins an adverbial prepositional phrase. مرا is split in the provided tokens as م + را; together they form the accusative clitic 'me' (مرا) marking the object of کرد. خواستی is the simple past 'you wanted/wished'; کرد is a light verb 'made/caused' — together خواستی کرد = 'you wished to make/cause.' کم is an adjective/adverb meaning 'small/little' and here describes what the speaker was made into.
segments.s677-g491: بدی را places 'evil' as a topical/accusative phrase (را marks it); تو is the pronoun 'you' (subject) and اندر جهان 'in the world' is a locative phrase. مایه‌ای is a nominal predicate meaning 'a source/causal element' (مایه + the indefinite -ای). Word order is topicalized: object/topic (بدی را) before subject and predicate for emphasis.
segments.s677-g492: هم here adds emphasis ('even/also'); از introduces origin/reason 'from/of'; بی‌رهان is an adjective 'unrestrained/without restraint'; برترین is a superlative adjective 'foremost/most eminent'; پایه‌ای is a predicate noun 'a basis/foundation.' The phrase means that, by reason of unrestrainedness, the addressee stands as the principal basis.
segments.s677-g493: هران (lit. 'each of those/every one') modifies خون ('blood') to mean 'every drop/each blood.' که is the relative/conjunction 'that/which'; شد functions as an auxiliary/past marker here ('was'); در جهان ریخته is a participial clause 'poured/spilled in the world' describing the blood. The clause thus is a relative clause: 'every blood that was spilled in the world.'
segments.s677-g494: توباشی is an optative/second-person form roughly 'may you be' or 'you should be' (a wish/curse). بران = بر + آن ('upon/on that'), where بران is a fused form meaning 'on/unto that'; گیتی 'world' is the noun, and آویخته is a participle/adjective 'hung/suspended.' The phrase is therefore a wished state: 'may you be hung upon that world.'
segments.s677-g495: نیابی is a negative subjunctive/optative form of 'to find' here meaning 'you do not (or will not) find' and carries admonitory force. شب تیره 'dark/ominous night' is the object (آن را marks 'that'); به خواب 'in sleep' is the locative/means phrase. Word order places the negated verb at the start for emphasis.
segments.s677-g496: که here introduces an explanatory clause 'for/since/that'; جویی (from جست/جوی) is a present-participial sense 'you seek/pursue'; همی is an adverb 'always/still' intensifying the verb; روز 'day' is the object and در آفتاب 'in the sunlight' is the locative phrase. Together the clause gives the reason: 'for you are always seeking the day in the sun.'
segments.s677-g465: ندانست is a past negative form (ن + دانست) meaning ‘did not know’ and here comes before the subject (a common poetic/old order). کس functions as an indefinite negative reading ‘no one’ (not ‘someone’). نام او is a genitive phrase ‘his name’ and در جهان is a locative prepositional phrase ‘in the world’, placed after it for emphasis.
segments.s677-g466: فرومایه بد is a compound adjectival description (‘base/lowly’ + ‘bad/evil’) modifying the (implied) person; در میان is a prepositional phrase ‘among/in the midst of’ and مهان (‘guests/visitors’ here reading as the prominent company) completes the phrase. The whole phrase characterizes how he was regarded among that group.
segments.s677-g467: بیامد is a simple past ‘came’. گرانمایه is an epithet ‘esteemed/precious’ modifying مهران, a proper name. ستاد is a past verb ‘praised’ (from ستودن); the clause is compact — ‘the esteemed Mehrān came, (and) praised’.
segments.s677-g468: به + شاه زمانه is a prepositional phrase ‘to/for the king of the age’ (شاه = king, زمانه = the age/era). نشان تو داد is a verb phrase with نشان ‘a sign/token/mark’ as object, تو ‘you’ marked as the possessed of that token, and داد ‘gave’ as the final verb — standard Persian SOV order: object(s) before the verb.
segments.s677-g469: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’. خاک سیاهت is a noun + possessive suffix (‘your black earth/soil’), where سیاهت is the adjective/possessive compound meaning ‘your blackness/dark soil’. چنان ‘so/thus’ intensifies the following verb. برکشید is the past transitive ‘lifted up’ (بر + کشیدن). Together they express ‘(he) thus lifted up from your dark soil’.
segments.s677-g470: شد is an auxiliary/past marker here (’became’/’was’), making the prior action/result into an accomplished state. آن روز ‘that day’ is a temporal phrase; بر چشم تو lit. ‘upon your eye’ functions idiomatically as ‘before your eyes/in your sight’. ناپدید is the predicate adjective ‘vanished/invisible’. Word order places the time and locative phrase before the predicate.
segments.s677-g471: تو را uses تو ‘you’ plus the postpositive object marker را to mark you as the definite object. داد is the past verb ‘gave’. The coordinated nouns گنج و سلیح و سپاه (‘treasure and weapons and army’) are the direct objects listed before the verb in typical Persian SOV order.
segments.s677-g472: درفش تهمتن is a noun phrase ‘the standard/banner of T(ah)mātan’ (an epithet/name). درفشان here is used adjectivally ‘shining, glittering’. چو is a simile marker ‘like/as’ and ماه ‘the moon’ completes the simile. The phrase describes the appearance of the banner.
segments.s677-g521: بریزند is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive form used here as a predictive 'they will spill/they spill'; the explicit subject is omitted (typical in Persian poetry). هم and بی‌گمان are adverbial modifiers following the verb for emphasis ('also / indeed' and 'undoubtedly'). خون تو places the object 'your blood' after the adverbials; تو is a possessive/you referring to the addressee.
segments.s677-g522: همین is an emphatic demonstrative 'this very / just this'. جستن is used as a verbal noun/infinitive 'seeking, pursuit' (nominalized here). تخت وارون تو is a noun phrase: تخت 'throne' modified by the adjective وارون 'overturned/turned upside down', followed by the possessor تو ('your'); word order (noun + adjective + possessor) is poetic but means 'your overturned throne'.
segments.s677-g523: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now / at present'. زندگانیت is a possessive nominal (here 'your life/being' or 'your living'), where the stem indicates the state of being alive and the enclitic ت marks 'your'. ناخوش بود is a predicative phrase: ناخوش 'unwell / ill' + بود (past-form of 'to be') used here to state the condition ('is/was unwell') — Persian often uses بود in narrative contexts where English uses present.
segments.s677-g524: وگر is a conditional 'and if / but if'. بگذری is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of گذشتن used here as 'you continue / you pass on / you live on'. جایت is the possessive noun 'your place/seat' (جا + -ت). آتش بود is a nominal predicate 'was/would be fire' (آتش = 'fire', بود = 'be' used to state the outcome).
segments.s677-g525: وگر introduces a conditional 'if'. دیر مانی here reads as 'دیر' ('long / a long time') + مانی; مانی functions as a 2nd-person verb-form 'you remain / you linger' (classical/poetic present). برین هم نشان is a clause meaning 'this too (will be) a sign' where برین is an emphatic/poetic 'on/this' (بر + این) and هم نشان 'also a sign/mark'. The syntactic pattern is conditional ('if you linger') followed by the consequent introduced in the remainder of the line.
segments.s677-g526: This short clause gives the consequence: سر از شاه ... کشان is a compressed poetic expression. سر از شاه literally 'head from (the) king' (سر + از + شاه) — here idiomatically 'your head will be taken/removed (in relation to the king)'. داد یزدان is a genitive phrase 'the justice/judgment of God' (داد = 'justice', یزدان = 'God'); کشان is a present-participial/agentive form 'those who draw/drag' or 'wielders' attached to that idea (i.e. 'those who enact God's justice'). The clause thus says that your head will be taken by those who carry out God's justice. Note: وز is an archaic/poetic particle here linking elements; its exact function is conjunctive/grammatical in older usage.
segments.s677-g527: پشیمانی آیدت is a verb phrase where پشیمانی 'regret' is the subject and آیدت ('comes to you') is the verb + enclitic object (آید + ت). زین کار خویش is a prepositional/agentive phrase 'from this deed of your own' (زین = 'from this' in poetic contraction, کار = 'deed/act', خویش + ش = 'of your own' with the possessive clitic ش marking 'his/her/its/your' depending on context). The order is topic (regret) + verb + source (of regret).
segments.s677-g528: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'. گفتار ناخوب و کردار خویش lists the causes: 'your bad speech and your own conduct' — گفتار 'speech', ناخوب 'bad/ill', و 'and', کردار 'deed/conduct', خویش ش is again the possessive 'your own'. Together they explain why regret will come.
segments.s677-g593: همی‌راند is the archaic/poetic imperfective past (همی- prefix indicating continuous action) meaning 'was riding / rode on'. Here it functions as the main verb and the subject is omitted (typical in poetry). پرخاشجوی و دژم are predicate adjectives following the verb describing the rider — 'aggressive and sullen' — joined by و.
segments.s677-g594: This is a sequence of noun phrases specifying equipment and position. کمندی is the snare/lasso (object), ببازو is a fused prepositional phrase (به + بازو) meaning 'on/around the arm', و links the two phrases, and درون شست خم is a noun phrase 'in the bend/hollow of the thumb' (درون = 'inside', شست = 'thumb', خم = 'bend/curve'). The phrase describes how the snare's loop lies.
segments.s677-g595: چو here functions as 'when'. نزدیکتر گشت is an intransitive perfective/ephemeral phrase 'became nearer / drew closer' (گشت = 'became/turned'). با introduces accompaniment or a characteristic; خنگ عاج is an adjectival phrase modifying an object (literally 'dazed/dull ivory'), a poetic epithet applied to the ivory piece.
segments.s677-g596: همی‌بود is the continuous past 'was (being)'. یازان is a present-participial/participial form functioning as 'opening/widening' (verbal adjective); together همی‌بود یازان = 'was opening'. به + پرمایه تاج is a prepositional phrase 'toward/towards the substantial (heavy, rich) crown' indicating direction or target of the opening.
segments.s677-g597: بینداخت is a past simple 'he threw/cast'. The object is آن تاب‌داده کمند: آن ('that') + تاب‌داده (past participle 'swung, swung-around' modifying کمند) — 'that swinging lasso'. The past participle follows the demonstrative as a modifier, a common classical/poetic order.
segments.s677-g598: سر تاج شاه = 'the top/summit of the king's crown' (a genitive/possessive group). اندرآمد here is an archaic/periphrastic past sense 'entered / came upon' (classical compound اندر آمد = 'came into/onto') — this particular sense is archaic and not listed among the provided senses, so marked -1. ببند is an imperative 'bind/fasten/close (it)!' addressed as a command to secure the crown/snare.
segments.s677-g599: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain / someone'. تیغ = 'sword'. گستهم is an irregular or archaic form here that does not match the provided sense list (no clear match), so its sense is marked -1; in context it likely modifies the action (adverbial or adjectival) but is unclear. زد is the past 'struck'. برکمند would normally mean 'at/on the snare' (بر + کمند), but the provided sense options don't fit this prepositional/locative use, so it is marked -1.
segments.s677-g600: سرشاه را is a marked noun phrase with را; سرشاه means 'the sovereign' or 'the king' here as the person referred to. زان = 'from that / thereby'. نیامد گزند is an idiomatic negated past construction 'no harm came' (نیامد = 'did not come', گزند = 'harm'), i.e. 'the sovereign did not suffer harm from that.' The subject is omitted (impersonal construction), and را marks the affected person.
segments.s677-g145: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. مهمانت is the noun مهمان + enclitic -ت 'your guest' (object/possessive suffix). آواز فرخ is a noun + adjective phrase 'a joyful/glad cry' (فرخ here = 'happy/fortunate' modifying آواز). دهد is third-person singular present ('gives/utters') placed at the clause end in classical Persian.
segments.s677-g146: برین گونه is a frozen adverbial phrase 'in this manner' formed from بر + ین (an archaic/orthographic form of این) + گونه ('manner'). بر دیو پاسخ دهد is a prepositional phrase 'to/against the demon' (بر = 'toward/to') followed by پاسخ 'answer' and the verb دهد 'gives/returns' at the end; the whole clause means '(he) gives an answer to the demon.'
segments.s677-g147: بترسم is first-person present 'I fear' (classical/polite form). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. روز بد آیدت پیش literally 'a bad day comes upon you' — آیدت is آید + enclitic -ت 'to you' (befall you) and پیش here means 'upon/before' (i.e., 'befall').
segments.s677-g148: The second که introduces a consequence/result clause 'that ...'. سرگشته is a predicative adjective 'bewildered' placed before the verb (poetic order). بینمت is بینم + enclitic -ت 'I see you' (I shall find you/see you). بر رای خویش is a prepositional phrase 'concerning your own counsel/opinion' (بر = 'on/upon', رای = 'counsel/opinion', خوی + ش = 'own/his' with the possessive suffix ش).
segments.s677-g149: تو را uses تو + the object/topic marker را to mean 'for you' or 'as for you'. چاره is 'remedy/solution' and is the thing being located. بر دست is a prepositional phrase literally 'on/in the hand' where بر = 'on/upon' and دست has the idiomatic sense 'power/control; hand (authority)'. آن پادشاست 'that king' names whose hand it is (پادشاست = پادشاه است, 'is king' / 'the king').
segments.s677-g150: که here is a relative/conjunction 'who/that' introducing attributes of the king. زنده‌ست is 'is alive/existent' (present copula). جاوید is an adjective 'eternal', and و...فرانرواست is a poetic compound meaning 'and is ever‑ruling/ever‑prosperous' (و = 'and' fused with the following archaic predicative form). Together they describe the king as living and eternal in his reign.
segments.s677-g151: This coordination lists moral charges: گنهکار 'sinful/sinner' qualified by یزدانی 'toward the divine/God' (an adjectival/genitive relation), joined by و 'and' to ناسپاس 'ungrateful'. The phrase characterizes a person as both sinful before God and ungrateful.
segments.s677-g152: Parallel nominative phrases with an implied copula: تن اندر نکوهش 'the body is in reproach/under censure' (اندر = 'in/within'), دل اندر هراس 'the heart is in dread/terror'. The structure omits an explicit verb (است) as is common in poetic Persian; both phrases describe the physical and emotional condition.
segments.s677-g401: This clause opens with the prepositional pronoun 'بدو' (to him), then the past verb 'گفت' (said) and the subject 'بهرام' — an inverted order common in Persian poetry: 'To him said Bahram'. 'جنگی' is an adjectival/noun label 'warlike / warrior' modifying the following predicate 'منم' (I am). 'منم' is the present copula with the enclitic -م marking first person: 'I am.'
segments.s677-g402: The particle 'که' introduces the content of what he said ('that / who'). The object phrase 'بیخ کیان را' ('the root/foundation of the Kians') takes the definite object marker 'را'. The preposition 'ز' (from) + 'بن' (root/foundation) emphasizes uprooting 'from the very base', and 'برکنم' is a first-person present/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'I uproot / I will uproot' (expressing intent or future action). Word order places object phrases before the verb, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s677-g403: 'چنین' (thus) is an adverb modifying the speech verb. Again 'گفت' functions as 'said' (the provided sense list lacks the verbal sense, see note). 'خسرو' is the speaker's name. The following 'که' begins a subordinate clause, and 'آن داستان' ('that tale') is the object/phrase being introduced.
segments.s677-g404: Here 'که' is a relative/introducing particle ('which / that'). 'داننده' literally 'the one who knows' (a knowledgeable person) is the agent. The collocation 'یاد آرد' uses the noun 'یاد' (remembrance) with the verb 'آرد' (brings) to mean 'brings to mind / recalls'. Finally 'از باستان' is a prepositional phrase 'from antiquity / ancient times.' The verb 'آرد' is present-tense, giving a general or timeless sense.
segments.s677-g405: The clause begins with 'که' (that / for), introducing a general statement. 'هرگز' (never) is an adverb of negation placed before the predicate. The preposition 'به' ('to / for') marks the indirect object, followed by a coordinated list 'نادان و بی‌راه و خرد' — adjectives/nouns describing the person (ignorant, unjust/wayward, and lacking wisdom). The coordination 'و' links these descriptors.
segments.s677-g406: 'سلیح بزرگی' is the object phrase meaning 'a great weapon' (noun + attribute). The modal/negative 'نباید' expresses prohibition ('should not / must not'), and 'سپرد' is the verb 'to entrust / hand over.' Together they form an impersonal/modal construction: 'one should not entrust (it).' Persian often uses this agentless pattern to state general advice.
segments.s677-g407: 'که' again introduces the explanatory clause. 'چون' is temporal ('when'), and 'بازخواهی' is a verbal expression meaning 'you will seek/claim back' (compound of 'باز' + 'خواهی'). 'نیاید' is the negative present/subjunctive third-person form 'does not come / will not come,' and 'به دست' ('into the hand') completes the idiom 'به دست نیاید' meaning 'does not come into one's possession.'
segments.s677-g408: Here 'که' functions causally ('because'). 'دارنده' is the agent 'the holder/possessor.' The phrase 'زان چیز' ('by/with that thing') marks the cause or instrument, and 'گشته‌ست' is the compound perfect 'has become.' 'مست' is a predicate adjective 'drunk/intoxicated,' used figuratively to mean 'besotted' or 'consumed by it.' The construction states the reason why recovery is impossible: the possessor has been made intoxicated by the thing.
segments.s677-g385: میان را is the direct object marked by the enclitic را (definite object marker), 'the middle/center'. ببستند is past plural 'they closed/tied' (here literal or idiomatic 'closed off'); the accompaniment phrase با رومیان ('with the Romans') follows the verb, showing who acted together with them.
segments.s677-g386: گرفتند is past plural 'they seized/took'. ناگاه is an adverb 'suddenly' (placed after the verb for poetic compression). تخت کیان ('the throne of the Kiyan/royal house') is the object phrase: تخت (throne) modified by کیان (the royal house/kings).
segments.s677-g387: ز ری is a prepositional phrase 'from Rey' (ز here is the poetic form of 'from'). بود is the past copula 'was'. ناپاکدل ماهیار is a name with an epithet: ناپاکدل 'impure‑hearted' modifying ماهیار (a proper name).
segments.s677-g388: کزو is a compound relative/particle 'from whom' (linking back to ماهیار). تیره شد is a predicative phrase: تیره 'dark/ominous' plus شد 'became' (past of شدن). تخم اسفندیار ('the seed/line of Esfandiyar') is the noun phrase whose state changed—تخم here means 'seed' in the sense of descent/offspring.
segments.s677-g389: ازان پس 'after that/thereupon' is a temporal phrase. ببستند is past plural 'they bound/tied' (here idiomatically 'they girded [their loins]'). ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians'.
segments.s677-g390: به کینه 'with hatred' gives the motive/reason (به + noun). یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' modifying the action. کمر بر میان is a compact phrase: کمر here 'girdle/waist' (i.e., girded their loins), بر indicates direction/against, and میان 'the middle' is the target/location of their action.
segments.s677-g391: نیامد here is the negative past 'did not come', used in the idiom پسند آمدن 'to be pleasing'; so نیامد ... پسند means 'did not please/was not approved by'. جهان‌آفرین را is marked with را: the Creator is the one to whom approval would be due (the experiencer). پسند is the noun 'approval/liking'.
segments.s677-g392: ازیشان is 'from them' (از + ایشان). به ایران رسید is the verb phrase 'reached (to) Iran' with رسید past 'arrived/reached'. آن گزند 'that harm' is the affected thing and appears at the end for emphasis/topic ('that harm reached Iran from them').
segments.s677-g49: چنین گفت is a fixed classical phrase meaning “thus said.” کان is an archaic/demonstrative particle meaning “that/the one” (introducing the person being described). دودگون دراز are two adjectival attributes (دودگون = two‑coloured/twofold, دراز = long/tall) that describe that omitted noun/person; in classical Persian such adjectives follow or cluster after the demonstrative.
segments.s677-g50: نشسته is a participial/predicative adjective meaning “seated” (literally ‘having sat’). بر introduces a prepositional phrase and takes آن (demonstrative “that”) as its object; ابلق (piebald) names the (implied) mount and سرفراز is an epithet/attribute (“proud, high‑headed/victorious”) modifying it — so نشسته بر آن ابلق سرفراز = “seated upon that proud piebald.”
segments.s677-g51: بدو is the prepositional pronoun ‘to him/unto him’ and precedes گفت, so بدو گفت = “he said to him.” گردوی is an epithet/nickname ‘walnut‑coloured (one)’; که here introduces the content/clause that follows (like English ‘that’). آری is an emphatic interjection ‘indeed/yes’ and همان points to ‘that very one.’
segments.s677-g52: نبرده‌ست is a perfect/compound negative meaning ‘has not (taken/won/held)’ and governs the following noun گمان (‘opinion/thought’); هرگز (‘never’) is an adverb of negation that typically follows or precedes the compound verb in classical style. به نیکی is a prepositional phrase literally ‘in/with goodness’ — together the clause says ‘has never (held) a good opinion’ (i.e. never thought well/intended well).
segments.s677-g53: چنین گفت again = ‘thus said.’ کز is a fused form of که + ز meaning ‘who/which/from which’ (here functioning like ‘if/when’ or a relative introducing the following conditional idea). پهلو means ‘side’ (i.e. aside, privately) and کوژپشت is the noun ‘hunchback’ (the person to be questioned).
segments.s677-g54: بپرسی is a present/subjunctive form meaning ‘if you ask/should you ask’ (classical Persian uses this form in conditional subordinate clauses). سخن is the object ‘speech/word/question,’ پاسخ is ‘answer/reply,’ and آرد is a present/predictive verb ‘brings/gives.’ درشت is an adjectival modifier ‘coarse/rough’ describing the reply. Together: ‘if you ask (him) a question aside, he will give a coarse answer.’
segments.s677-g55: This is a verbless descriptive clause listing physical features. همان ‘that very one’ introduces the portrait. خوک بینی is a compound‑like description ‘pig‑nosed’ (خوک = pig, بینی = nose), and خوابیده چشم literally ‘sleeping‑eyed’ = ‘half‑closed/sleepy eyes.’ Classical Persian often strings such nouns/adjectives without a copula to produce terse pictorial descriptions.
segments.s677-g56: دل آگنده دارد is a typical Persian word order where the possessed noun (دل = heart) + adjective (آگنده = filled) comes before the verb دارد ‘has’ — literally ‘a heart full he has’ = ‘his heart is full.’ تو گویی is an idiomatic simile (‘it is as if’/‘one would say’) — تو here is the poetic second‑person element used in comparatives and گویی = ‘it seems/suppose’ (classically used to introduce a comparison). به خشم = ‘with anger’ (prepositional phrase showing the content of that fullness).
segments.s677-g393: A noun phrase: کلاه کیی (the Kayanian hat/crown) followed by a locative phrase بر سر اردشیر (on Ardashir's head). Persian permits this order (noun + modifier + prepositional phrase). Here کلاه is the concrete object (a crown), کیی marks its Kayanian/royal character, and بر سر + name gives the placement.
segments.s677-g394: A short verb clause where نهاد is the simple past (he/it placed). The logical subject is داور دستگیر (the Helper‑Judge/God) which follows the time phrase آن زمان ('at that time'). دستگیر is an epithet meaning 'succouring, helper' modifying داور. The object (the crown) is understood from the previous segment.
segments.s677-g395: An adjectival predicate: به تاج کیان ('for the crown of the Kians') is a prepositional/object phrase, او is the pronoun 'he', and سزاوار بود is the predicate 'was worthy/fit'. بود is the past copula, so the whole means 'he was worthy of the Kayanian crown.'
segments.s677-g396: اگر چند here is a concessive formula meaning 'although/even though' (literally 'if though'). بی‌گنج و دینار بود is a past predicate 'was without treasure and dinars' — بی‌- marks lack. بود again is the past copula linking the concessive clause to the previous statement.
segments.s677-g397: A simple past clause: کنون ('now') sets the temporal frame, نام آن نامداران is the subject phrase meaning 'the name/reputation of those famous ones', and گذشت is the past verb 'passed' (i.e., their fame has passed or is past).
segments.s677-g398: سخن گفتن is a verbal noun phrase 'the speaking/telling', ما is the subject, and همه باد گشت is an idiomatic optative/result clause: باد here functions like an optative particle ('may/become') and گشت is the past of شدن used in this periphrastic way to express 'has turned into' or 'would be'. Together: 'all our speech has turned into mere wind' (i.e. is worthless/vanished).
segments.s677-g399: A question: کنون ('now') opens it, مهتری را ('preeminence/leadership' with the object marker را) is the thing considered, سزاوار is the predicate 'deserving', and کیست is the fused interrogative 'who is'. The structure literally asks 'Now, who is deserving of the chief place?'
segments.s677-g400: جهان را بنوی uses را to mark the world as the scope/recipient ('write for the world' / 'let it be written in the world'); بنوی is the imperative/jussive 'write / let it be written'. جهاندار کیست is a subordinate question 'who is the sovereign' (کیست = 'who is'). The whole is an injunction to record for the world who the ruler is.
segments.s677-g1: رسیدند is a 3rd-person plural past verb meaning “arrived/reached.” In this classical word order the finite verb appears before its coordinated subjects (بهرام و خسرو). به هم is a reciprocal directional phrase (“towards one another”/“to each other”), so the whole clause means “Bahram and Khosrow reached/approached one another.”
segments.s677-g2: This is an elliptic predicative phrase describing the two men in parallel. گشاده modifies روی (gشاده‌رو = open-faced/cheerful) and precedes the pronoun-like یکی (“one [of them]”); the copula is omitted (typical in poetic Persian). و دیگر دژم parallels the first half: “and the other (دیگر) [was] sullen (دژم).”
segments.s677-g3: نشسته is a stative past participle/predicate meaning “seated” and appears before its noun, a common poetic order. جهاندار is the noun ‘world‑holder/sovereign’ (the subject). بر is the preposition “on,” and خنگ عاج is a compound/phrase referring to an ivory seat or object made of ivory; together the clause says the sovereign was seated upon that ivory (object).
segments.s677-g4: فریدون is the proper name (here in apposition). یل is an epithet ‘hero/valiant’ attached to the name. بود is the past copula “was.” با introduces the accompaniment/instrumental phrase “with,” and فر و تاج are the two accompanying nouns “royal glory (فر)” and “crown,” giving “Fereydun the hero was with glory and crown.”
segments.s677-g5: ز is the poetic preposition meaning “of/from” and introduces the material/description of the garment. دیبای زربفت چینی is a packed modifier chain describing قبای: ‘a robe (قبای) of brocade/dībā, gold‑woven (زربفت), and Chinese (چینی)’ — classical Persian permits several modifiers in sequence before the head noun.
segments.s677-g6: چو is a simile marker “like/as.” گردوی here describes the colour/appearance (‘walnut‑coloured’). پیش اندرون رهنمای is a locative/identifying phrase: پیش = “before/in the presence of,” اندرون = “the inner (court/quarters),” and رهنمای = “the guide.” In natural English the clause is rendered as the inner guide appearing/wearing a walnut‑coloured Chinese gold‑brocade robe.
segments.s677-g7: چو is the comparative “like/as.” بندوی is a noun ‘bondman/servant.’ The following واو connects to گستهم; گستهم is an archaic/poetic first‑person past form (“I wandered”) that here occurs in classical lines as a fixed phrase or interjectional remark and is positioned before the locative phrase بر دست شاه (‘at/on the king’s hand,’ i.e. in the king’s service). The whole fragment uses poetic ordering and elliptic constructions rather than a modern full clause.
segments.s677-g8: چو again introduces a simile “like.” خراد can be read as an adjective/epithet meaning ‘wise’ (or as part of a name/title) and برزین is here a proper name (Barzin); together they form the name/epithet “Kharad‑Barzin.” زرین کلاه is an adjectival + noun phrase ‘golden cap’ (زرین = golden, کلاه = cap/hat). The phrase describes someone like Kharad‑Barzin wearing a golden cap; the copula is omitted as is common in poetry.
segments.s677-g265: سخنها (plural) + شنیدیم is a simple past clause: 'we heard speeches/words.' چندی here means 'some/several' (a small number) and درشت is an attributive adjective meaning 'coarse/harsh' modifying سخنها; word order is noun – verb – modifiers in a compact poetic line.
segments.s677-g266: به پیروزگر is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward the victor.' باز هشتیم پشت is a tersely ordered poetic expression meaning 'we turned our backs' (باز + پشت = 'back/again/backwards'); هشتیم is a poetic/archaic 1st‑person plural verbal form (here producing the sense 'we [turned/stood]') and پشت is the noun 'back(s)' — together they convey 'we showed/turned our backs.'
segments.s677-g267: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' من is the subject 'I.' سزاوار is a predicate adjective 'worthy/deserving (of),' and شاهی is the abstract noun 'kingship' (the thing one might be worthy of). نیم is an archaic/poetic first‑person copular/ending here and must be read as part of the predicate ('I am' / in context effectively 'I am not' by sense of the whole line); it is an archaic form rather than the modern هستم/نیستم.
segments.s677-g268: مبادا is an optative/negative wish 'may it not be / God forbid.' که introduces the subordinate clause. در + زیر + دستی is a small locative/idiomatic phrase 'in/under the hand' meaning 'under someone's authority or power'; دستی here functions as 'hand/authority' (poetic usage). زیم is an archaic clitic/ending whose exact form is poetic and indicates relation to the speaker; together the clause expresses 'may it never be that I am under someone's hand.'
segments.s677-g269: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' پاسخش is the object 'his answer' (پاسخ + possessive clitic -ش), and داد is the simple past 'he gave' used idiomatically for 'he replied/uttered.' بهرام is the subject/name. باز is an adverb 'again' modifying the act of replying; poetic word order places these elements compactly.
segments.s677-g270: که‌ای here introduces the reported speech; the sequence combines که (introducing what follows) with the vocative ای ('O') addressing someone. بی‌خرد is an epithet 'foolish/unwise,' ریمن is a proper name, and دیوساز is a compound insult 'demon‑maker' (literally 'one who makes demons') — the whole is direct speech: 'O foolish Rīman, demon‑maker.'
segments.s677-g271: پدرت is 'your father' (پدر + 2nd‑person suffix -ت). آن is a demonstrative 'that/the,' and جهاندار is an honorific title 'world‑ruler/sovereign.' دین دوست is a two‑word epithet 'friend/lover of the religion' (i.e., pious), and مرد here is the noun 'man/person' (often used of a notable or heroic man). The order strings titles and attributes after the possessor.
segments.s677-g272: که is a relative 'who.' هرگز is 'never.' نزد means 'in the presence of' or 'before' (i.e., before other people). بر کسی is literally 'upon anyone' ('بر' + 'کسی'), and باد here is the optative particle 'may it be' (wishing/hope), so the clause expresses a wish: 'may it never be (that this happens) before anyone.' سرد literally 'cold' is used figuratively here (poetically) to indicate 'coldness, aloofness, or disgrace' — the whole line thus prays that he never suffer humiliation or shame before anyone.
segments.s677-g41: چنین گفت خسرو is a short declarative clause: چنین ‘thus’ (fronted adverb) + گفت ‘said’ (past tense) + خسرو (subject). The following که‌ای سرکشان is a vocative address; که‌ای here is an archaic/poetic vocative particle (= که ای / کِی), and سرکشان is a plural noun addressing the proud/rebellious ones. Word order is flexible/poetic (adverb + verb + subject), and the vocative introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.s677-g42: ز بهرام چوبین که دارد نشان is a noun phrase with a relative clause: ز here is the poetic form of از (‘of/from, concerning’) introducing the name بهرام چوبین (an anthroponym + epithet). که دارد نشان is a relative clause: که ‘who/that’ + دارد (present-tense ‘has/possesses’) + نشان (direct object ‘mark/token’). In short: ‘of Bahram Chubin, who has a mark.’
segments.s677-g43: بدو گفت گردوی is an inverted transmission clause: بدو ‘to him/unto him’ (indirect object clitic phrase) + گفت ‘said’ + گردوی (the speaker’s name or epithet). کای شهریار is a vocative inserted after the verb: کای is the vocative particle ‘O’ and شهریار ‘sovereign/king’. The structure means ‘Gerdūy said to him, “O sovereign...”’.
segments.s677-g44: نگه کن بر آن مرد ابلق سوار is an imperative sentence: نگه (a noun/command-stem 'look/keep watch') + کن (imperative auxiliary ‘do’) = ‘look!’ or ‘keep watch!’. The prepositional phrase بر آن مرد ابلق سوار ('on/at that piebald rider') is the target of the imperative; ابلق modifies سوار (piebald + rider). The demonstrative آن points to a specific man previously mentioned.
segments.s677-g45: قبایش سپید و حمایل سیاه is a compact descriptive clause: قبایش ‘his robe’ (قبا + possessive -ش) + سپید ‘white’, coordinated with حمایل سیاه ‘(his) حمایل black’. The possessive on قبایش is understood to extend to حمایل (the second noun is coordinated and the owner is shared). حمایل is an archaic garment-term here paired with قبا to describe contrasting colors.
segments.s677-g46: همی راند ابلق میان سپاه describes continuous action: همی is a poetic progressive/continuous marker (‘kept on/was repeatedly’), راند is the past/imperfective form of راندن (‘drove/rode’), and ابلق (piebald) is the noun in focus (here applied to the horse or mount). میان سپاه ‘in the midst of the army’ is an adverbial phrase. So: ‘the piebald went/rushed through the midst of the host.’
segments.s677-g47: جهاندار چون دید بهرام را is a temporal clause: جهاندار ‘the sovereign’ (subject) + چون (here used in the sense ‘when/as’) + دید (past tense ‘saw’) + بهرام را (object marked by را). The word order is subject + temporal conjunction + verb + object, a common poetic pattern for 'when X saw Y.'
segments.s677-g48: بدانستش آغاز و فرجام را is a result clause: بدانستش is a fused past form (بـ + دانست + ـش) meaning ‘he knew/recognized him/it’ (the clitic -ش refers back to Bahram), and آغاز و فرجام را lists the objects 'the beginning and the end' with the object marker را; the whole means ‘he (immediately) knew/understood his beginning and his end.’
segments.s677-g329: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); پیغمبر آمد is a simple past clause ('a prophet came'). به لهراسپ is a prepositional phrase ('to Lohrasp') marking the indirect object/recipient, and داد is a past transitive verb ('gave') whose direct object is left implicit here (picked up from the previous line/context). The sequence آمد ... داد is a poetic coordination of two past actions.
segments.s677-g330: پذیرفت is a past/perfect verb ('he accepted/received') with an implicit subject (Lohrasp). زان پس is an adverbial phrase meaning 'after that' (زان = 'from that/it', پس = 'after/then'). به گشتاسپ داد is another past clause ('gave to Gushtasp') where به + name marks the recipient and داد is the giving verb; the thing given is again understood from context.
segments.s677-g331: هرآنکس که introduces a free relative ('whoever'). ما را uses the object pronoun with the definite/object marker را ('us' as direct object). نموده‌ست is a perfect (= has done/caused) indicating a completed action, and رنج is the effect/accusative noun ('suffering, hardship') — together meaning 'has caused us suffering'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (object pronoun + auxiliary before the noun).
segments.s677-g332: دگر here marks an antithesis/alternation ('in return/alternatively'); آنک is a demonstrative ('that/which'); ازو is a prepositional-like enclitic meaning 'from him/of him'; یافته‌ستیم is first-person-plural perfect ('we have found/obtained'); and گنج is the direct object ('treasure'). The clause means 'from him we have found treasure' and contrasts with the previous clause.
segments.s677-g333: همه یکسر is an emphatic subject phrase ('all, entirely'); اندر پناه is a prepositional phrase ('in/within the refuge/protection'); منند is an archaic/formal predicative verb meaning 'are like' or 'are as' (here functioning as the copula/predicate: 'they are in my protection'). The order is emphatic and poetic, placing modifiers before the copula.
segments.s677-g334: اگر ... ار is a doubled conditional ('if, even if') adding emphasis; دشمن is the noun 'enemy'; نیکخواه is a compound noun/adjective 'well-wisher' or 'benevolent one'; and منند again means 'are like'—so the clause reads 'if (they are) enemies, (they) are as if my well-wishers', a paradoxical praise. The two conditional particles (اگر and ار) intensify the concessive sense.
segments.s677-g335: همه begins the generalization ('everyone'); the repeated preposition بر introduces a series of categories ('on/with regard to woman and offspring, on king') — a poetic parallelism listing kinds of people (woman, child/offspring, king). The phrase functions as the topic the speaker is negating in the next segment ('we do not call everyone thus'). The doubled بر is stylistic for balance.
segments.s677-g336: نخوانیم is the 1st-person-plural negative present of خواندن used here in the sense 'we do not call/name' (خواندن can mean 'to call' or 'to name'). کس را is the object 'anyone' with the object marker را, and مگر پارسا means 'except the pious' — together: 'we call no one except the pious.' The negative verb governs the restriction expressed by مگر.
