segments.s677-g33: بگفت is a past 3rd‑singular verb 'he said' (classical past), followed by the coordinating و 'and'. برانگیخت is another past 3rd‑singular verb 'roused/raised' and takes its object immediately after it (ابلق, the piebald horse). زجای is the poetic contraction of ز + جای 'from (its) place' and functions as a prepositional complement to برانگیخت. The sentence omits an explicit subject for these verbs because it is clear from discourse.
segments.s677-g34: تو is the 2nd‑person singular 'you' and گفتی is the past 2nd‑person 'you said' introducing reported speech. شد is a past auxiliary/verb here meaning 'happened/took place' and introduces the reported clause. آن باره is a temporal noun phrase 'that occasion/that moment', پران is an adjective 'winged' modifying همای (the mythical bird, Humay). Word order is verb (شد) + temporal phrase + modifier + noun in this poetic register.
segments.s677-g35: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one' / 'someone'. تنگ آورد is a past construction ('آورد' with adjective تنگ) meaning 'brought (someone) into straits' or 'pressed closely'; گاهی is an adverb 'at times' or 'sometimes'; گرفت is past 3rd‑singular 'seized/took'. The second verb has an understood object (him/it) recoverable from context. This is compact, typical epic phrasing with two short actions conjoined.
segments.s677-g36: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him / at him' (here marking the target of the amazement). مانده is a past participle/resultant state 'remained' or 'was left' (stative). بد is an adverbial use meaning 'badly' / 'greatly' (intensifier here). لشکر is the subject 'the army', اندر is the preposition 'in', and شگفت is the noun 'wonder/astonishment' — together 'the army remained greatly in wonder at him.' Word order places the participle/auxiliary before the predicate phrase in this poetic style.
segments.s677-g37: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. آوردگه is a noun 'place of arrival' and functions as the object of ز. شد is a past 3rd‑singular verb here meaning 'moved/went' or 'turned' (classical usage of شد to mark change of place/state). سوی نهروان is a directional phrase 'toward Nahrawan' (سوی + place). The order 'ز آوردگه شد سوی...' is typical of older Persian poetry where the source, verb and direction appear in sequence.
segments.s677-g38: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually' / 'still'. بود is the past copula 'was / remained'. بر پیش is a compound prepositional phrase 'in front of / before'. فرخ جوان is a descriptive noun phrase 'the fortunate (فرخ) young (جوان)' — here a reference to a young man named/epithet Farrokh. The clause says he kept himself continually before (i.e., ahead of) that young man.
segments.s677-g39: تنی چند is a fixed idiom 'a few men' or 'some bodies' (i.e., some companions). با او means 'with him'. ز ایرانیان indicates origin/membership 'from the Iranians' (i.e., these men belong to the Iranian side). The phrase is a compact nominal group listing companions.
segments.s677-g40: همه 'all' is the subject; بسته is a past participle 'bound/tied' used predicatively to mean 'committed' or 'joined together'. بر جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'for the battle' (بر + جنگ), خسرو is the genitive 'of Khosrow', and میان here conveys 'in the midst / together' — overall: 'all committed to Khosrow's battle'. In classical Persian the participle بسته frequently carries the sense 'arrayed/ready/pledged' in military contexts.
segments.s677-g153: The sequence is object + temporal clause + predicate: 'م' + 'را' functions together as the object pronoun 'me' (here given as two tokens), with را marking the definite object. چُون الان is an adverbial 'as/now' ('چون' = 'as', 'الان' = 'now'). خوانی is a present-tense verb from خواندن used here in the sense 'to call/name/summon' and همی is the archaic/poetic present-progressive/emphatic particle (marks ongoing or emphatic present). Word order is topical/object before verb (object – adverbial – predicate).
segments.s677-g154: ز marks provenance/selection 'from (among)'; گوهر is 'gem/jewel' here literal; به introduces the idea 'as/to be' and links to the following noun phrase 'یک سوم' ('one of three' / 'one third') which is the complement of دانی. دانی is 2nd-person present of دانستن used in the evaluative sense 'you deem/consider'. همی again is the emphatic/present particle. The clause reads literally 'from the jewels you consider (me) to be one-third/one of three.'
segments.s677-g155: مگر here introduces a rhetorical negative/exclamatory turn ('is it not...?/surely not...?') rather than a plain exception. ناسزایم is ناسزا + the first‑person possessive ending (my/against me) meaning 'an insult to me'. به شاهنشهی 'به' + شاهنشهی indicates 'to/for (regard to) kingship/imperial dignity' — the whole phrase questions whether being placed in kingship is an insult to the speaker.
segments.s677-g156: نه before the adjective negates the copula clause ('is not'). زیبا is the adjective 'beautiful/fitting'. ست is the copula 'is'. بر من is the prepositional phrase 'upon me' (literally 'on me'). کلاه مهی is a noun phrase 'the moon‑like hat/crown' (کلاه = hat/crown, مهی = moon‑like, an epithet for a certain royal crown). Together: 'No — the moonlike crown is not beautiful upon me.'
segments.s677-g157: چون ... و چو ... is a paired simile/conjunction: 'as/like Khosrow Nia and as/like Hormoz the father.' کسری is the personal name 'Khosrow' (کسری), نیا here functions as an epithet/descriptor (literally 'ancestor' in the sense of a qualified name element), و connects the parallels, چو repeats 'as/like', هرمز is the personal name 'Hormoz' (here the name sense), and پدر is appositional 'father'. The whole phrase lists illustrious figures for comparison.
segments.s677-g158: کرا is an archaic interrogative 'who/whom' (object form). دانی is 2nd‑person present of دانستن used here 'you deem/consider'. از marks the comparative 'than'/'of' in 'more deserving than me' (from/of me). من is the first‑person pronoun 'me'. سزاوارتر is the comparative adjective 'more deserving/fit' (suffixed comparative -تر). The clause is a rhetorical question: 'Whom do you deem more deserving than me?'
segments.s677-g159: ورا is a dative/goal particle 'to him' (marks the indirect object or direction toward someone). گفت here is the verb 'said' (but the provided sense list did not include the verbal 'to say' sense, so a direct sense index is not available). بهرام is the speaker's name 'Bahram'. کای is an archaic vocative particle (like 'O' or the honorific 'Kay') addressing someone. بدنشان is بدن + possessive suffix 'their bodies/persons' meaning 'their persons/them'. The clause sets up direct speech: 'To him Bahram said, O ... their persons ...'.
segments.s677-g160: به here works adverbially 'in/by' introducing the domains 'گفتار و کردار' ('speech and deed', i.e. words and actions). چون is the comparative/simile 'as/like'. بیهشان is an archaic form (بیه = 'without' or an archaic particle) with the 3rd‑person plural suffix; in context it conveys 'as if without them' / 'as if not themselves' (an old poetic idiom). The whole quoted fragment reads: 'their persons, in speech and deed, are as if not themselves.'
segments.s677-g257: ایا here functions as an address/interrogative particle used vocatively (roughly 'O' or 'oh'), introducing the person spoken to; مرد (man) is the head noun modified by the adjective بدبخت (wretched) and linked by و to the adjective-noun بیدادگر (tyrant/cruel). Word order is typical Persian: determiner/particle + noun + modifiers.
segments.s677-g258: به here marks the target/topic of the following supposition ('about'/'towards'); نابودنیها is a plural nominalized adjective 'the perishable (things)'; گمانی is a noun 'a supposition/suspicion'; مبر is the negative imperative (from مِبر 'do not take') meaning 'do not take/harbor'. The clause literally: 'do not take a supposition regarding the perishable.'
segments.s677-g259: که begins the explanatory/causal clause ('for'/'because'); خرچنگ is the subject 'crab' (used metaphorically); را is the definite/object/topic marker attached for emphasis; نیست is the negative copula 'is not/there is not'; پر 'wing/feather' is the object, followed by عقاب 'eagle' in apposition/genitive (i.e. 'the eagle's wing'). Word order: subject — object marker — negated copula — object phrase.
segments.s677-g260: نپرد is a negative present/formal-subjunctive form of پرید/پریدن here meaning 'does not fly' or 'will not fly away'; عقاب is the subject 'the eagle'; the prepositional phrase از بر آفتاب ('from/from beside the sun') uses از ('from') plus بر ('on/upon/near') and آفتاب ('sun'), indicating origin/location. The verb follows its subject (typical of Persian clauses where the verb comes after its noun phrase in classical/poetic word order).
segments.s677-g261: This is an oath-like phrase introduced by به meaning 'by' (here the instrumental/adjuration sense). یزدان پاک 'the pure God' is a nominal phrase; و joins تخت و کلاه ('throne and crown') — conventional grand items invoked together. The preposition به repeated before تخت emphasizes 'by throne and crown' parallel to 'by the pure God.'
segments.s677-g262: که‌گر is the conditional 'if' (archaic/spelled together); من بیابم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of یافتن 'to find' used here in a protasis ('if I should find'); تو را uses را as the definite/object marker with 'you'; بی‌سپاه is a predicative phrase 'without an army/unescorted'. The clause therefore reads 'if I find you unescorted.'
segments.s677-g263: اگر marks a conditional 'if'; برزنم is an archaic/poetic verbal/locative form here: in context it functions as part of a conditional clause meaning roughly 'if I attack/meet you on the plain' (the root بر زدن/زنیدن or noun برزن 'plain' gives this sense in poetry). بر تو 'upon you/on you' follows, and بر باد سرد literally 'by/on cold wind' — 'باد سرد' = 'cold wind' — an idiomatic phrase implying harsh/blasting force. Word-order is affected by poetic compression; the whole chunk sets up the circumstance of an encounter on the field.
segments.s677-g264: ندارمت is the negative present of داشتن with the enclitic object 'ـت' ('I do not/have not you' → here idiomatically 'I will not let you / I will not cause you'); رنجه is a verb/noun meaning 'be distressed' or 'make suffer' (object implied); ز is the preposition 'from'; گرد here means 'dust' (گردِ نبرد = 'dust of battle'); نبرد is 'battle'. Together the clause says 'I will not (let) you be distressed by the dust of battle' — the verb carries the object pronoun in its form.
segments.s677-g505: چو is the conditional/time conjunction ('when' or 'if'). فرمان کنی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive clause where فرمان (order/command) is the object and کنی (کَنی) is the verb 'you do/you issue' (classical subjunctive/optative form used to express a present conditional). هرچ خواهی تو راست is a verbless nominal clause: هرچ 'whatever' + خواهی 'you want' (2nd-person present) + تو explicit subject pronoun + راست predicate 'is right'. Word order is typical classical Persian: condition (چو + verb), then the result clause with subject explicit for emphasis.
segments.s677-g506: This clause is a short, elliptical comment. یکی 'one' introduces a single point; بهر ازین here functions as 'with regard to / in respect of this' (بهر = 'for, with regard to', از + ین = 'of/this'), پادشاهی 'kingship/kingdom' names the domain, and the clause ends with تو راست 'you are right' (تو = you, راست = correct). The clause is verbless except for the final predicate راست; word order puts the topical phrase (one / for this kingship) before the pronoun and predicate for emphasis.
segments.s677-g507: بدین here is the classical fused prepositional phrase 'به + این' meaning 'in/with this' or 'in this (world)'; the editor split it into ب + دین, but in sense it functions as 'in this'. گیتی 'world' is the locative target, اندر 'within/in' is an adverbial reinforcement, and بزی is an archaic 2nd-person imperative/subjunctive of زیستن 'to live' (i.e. 'live'), followed by شادمان 'happily'. The clause is an exhortation: 'Live happily in this world.'
segments.s677-g508: This is a descriptive coordinate phrase: تن آسان 'with an easy/comfortable body' (تن = body, آسان = easy/comfortable). و links the next member, دور از بد بدگمان = 'far from bad / from evil suspicion' where دور از is the prepositional phrase 'distant from / free from', بد intensifies or qualifies, and بدگمان is an adjective/noun 'suspicious, mistrustful (suspicion)'. The whole string modifies the exhortation (how one should live).
segments.s677-g509: وگر is the conditional 'if / but if'. بگذری is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive of گذرِیدن/گذراندن 'you pass / you go past'. زین here is an archaic 'from this / of this' construction (editor split it as زین but some listed senses do not match; functionally 'from this/this particular'), and سرای سپنج is a noun phrase 'the house/abode of ...' (سرای = house/abode; سپنج is a noun whose exact sense here is obscure or archaic: contextually 'this dwelling' or 'this world/abode'). The clause means 'if you pass beyond this abode'.
segments.s677-g510: گه here functions as a temporal adverb 'then / at that time' (classical usage can be 'occasion' or 'sometimes'), بازگشتن is the infinitive 'to return', نباشی is the negative 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'there will not be / you will not be' (here idiomatically expressing '... will not be' or 'it will not be [for you]'), and به رنج 'with hardship/pain'. Together: 'then returning will not be (accompanied) by suffering' — i.e. 'returning will not be painful.'
segments.s677-g511: نشاید is an impersonal present negative 'it is not fitting / ought not to be.' کزین = 'from this / of this' (classical fused form), کم کنیم is 'we lessen / we make less' (کنیم = 1st-person plural present/subjunctive of کردن used with کم 'to lessen'), ار = 'if', and فزون = 'more / excessive'. The clause expresses a normative prohibition: 'We should not diminish this even if it is excessive.'
segments.s677-g512: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause 'for / because / that'. زردشت is the proper name 'Zoroaster'. گوید is the present tense verb 'says' (classical narrative present). به زند اندرون = 'in the Zand (commentary) within' (به = in/according to, زند = Zand, اندرون = inside/within). The clause cites authority: 'for Zoroaster says this in the Zand.'
segments.s677-g121: چنین is an adverb fronting the clause meaning “thus/so.” داد پاسخ is a compound verb phrase (past) meaning “gave an answer”; مر is the archaic oblique pronoun equivalent to modern مرا/مَرا meaning “to me.” ابلق (adjective) precedes سوار (noun) and together form the subject phrase “the piebald rider.” Word order is a poetic fronting of the adverb, then verb+object, then the person to whom the answer was given, then the subject.
segments.s677-g122: که introduces reported speech or a content clause here (“that” / marking the rider’s words). من is the explicit subject pronoun. خرمم is the predicate with the adjectival stem خرم plus the 1st‑person enclitic -م meaning “I am joyful”; شاد is a coordinating adjective “happy.” و links the next phrase; به روزگار is a prepositional/adverbial phrase literal “with/to fortune/at time” describing his state. Tense/mood: this is direct reported speech in the rider’s present/stateive predicate.
segments.s677-g123: تو را places the addressee in a dative/benefactive role using the object marker را (here marking the person concerned). روزگار بزرگی = “an age/days of greatness” (روزگار + noun modifier بزرگی). مباد is the classical negative optative—“may it not be” / “may you not have” —so the whole clause is a wish/curse: “May you not have a time of greatness.”
segments.s677-g124: The correlative negatives نه ... نه ... set up two balanced items. دانی is the 2nd‑person present of دانستن “to know” (here negated): “you do not know / you know not.” بیداد is the first item (injustice/oppression), ز شاهی = “from/of kingship” (ز as poetic ‘from’), and the final داد is the second nominal item (justice). Grammatically the clause says the addressee knows neither injustice of kingship nor justice.
segments.s677-g125: الان is an adverb “now.” چون here is a subordinating conjunction meaning “when/as.” شهریاری is a noun meaning “sovereignty/royal rule,” and کند is the 3rd‑person singular present/habitual of کردن meaning “does / performs” — together شهریاری کند = “acts as sovereign / assumes rulership.” The clause reads temporally: “Now when the king assumes rulership...”
segments.s677-g126: ورا is the poetic form of the prepositional/indirect object meaning “to him” or “for him.” مرد بدبخت is a noun phrase “an unfortunate man.” یاری کند combines the noun یاری (“help/aid”) with the verb کند (“does/makes”) functioning as a light verb to form the verbal idea “gives help / will help.” The clause therefore means “an unfortunate man will help him.”
segments.s677-g127: تو را marks the person affected (you) with the object marker را. روزگاری is an adverbial use of روزگار meaning “for a time / for some days.” سگالیده‌ام is the perfect 1st‑person form of سگالیدن (“to set dogs on someone / to harry with hounds”): “I have set dogs on you.” The tense is perfect/resultative describing a completed hostile action.
segments.s677-g128: بنوی is the imperative of نوشتن here used idiomatically as “write/mark/take note” (a rhetorical ‘mark this’). کمندیت is کمند (“lasso/snare”) with a 2nd‑person possessive/poetic suffix -یت, meaning “your lasso/snare.” مالیده‌ام is the 1st‑person perfect of مالیدن (“to rub/smear/anoint”), so the phrase says “I have smeared/greased your lasso.”
segments.s677-g17: بیاموخت is a past-tense third-person verb (‘he learned’). In poetry Persian the verb often precedes its object, so آیین شاهنشهان (the object) follows the verb. آیین here means ‘custom/practice’ and شاهنشهان is the royal genitive/epithet ‘of the kings’ (literally ‘king-of-kings’).
segments.s677-g18: به زودی is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘soon’ (به functions as an adverbial marker here). سرآرم is a stative/operative verb meaning literally ‘to set/place (one’s) head’; poetically it means ‘to establish oneself / come to prominence’ (here referring back to the same subject). بدو is the dative pronoun ‘to him/for him’, and بر جهان = ‘upon the world’ (بر = on/upon, جهان = world) — together giving the sense ‘he soon set himself up in the world’.
segments.s677-g19: ببینید is an imperative/optative plural ‘look/see’. لشکرش is the possessed noun ‘his army’, and را is the definite object marker attaching to the noun phrase. سر به سر is an idiomatic repeating phrase (‘head to head’ / ‘one by one’ / ‘in succession’), here emphasizing inspection of the army rank by rank.
segments.s677-g20: که introduces a relative clause ‘so that / to see who…’. تا here is a limiting/emphatic particle (‘as far as / exactly’), and کی is the interrogative ‘who’. ست is the copula ‘is’. زیشان = ‘of them / among them’. یکی means ‘one / someone’ and نامور is ‘renowned’. The whole clause asks which one among them is famous.
segments.s677-g21: سواری is ‘rider/horseman’. نبینم is the negative present/subjunctive form ‘I do not see / may I not see’, here functioning as ‘I see no…’. همی is a poetic continuous/emphatic particle (‘ever/still’). رزم + جوی form a compound adjective (رزمجوی) meaning ‘battle-seeking’ or ‘eager for combat’; جوی is the formative element ‘-seeking’.
segments.s677-g22: که introduces the relative clause ‘who…’. بامن = ‘with me / before me’. به روی is a prepositional phrase literally ‘to (one’s) face’ (به = to, روی = face). اندر is the locative ‘into / before’, and آرند is the present-subjunctive/optative plural of آوردن ‘to bring’ (here ‘who would bring/put’). The final روی repeats ‘face’ — the phrase means ‘who would come and bring his face before me’ i.e. ‘who would face me’. Note the archaic word order (object/phrase spread around the verb).
segments.s677-g23: ببیند here is optative/subjunctive third-person (‘let him see / may he see’), used imperatively in poetic narration. کنون means ‘now’. کار = ‘deed/work’, and مردان مرد is a compounded epithet: مردان (men/heroes) plus مرد (man/hero) yields the sense ‘the man among men’ or ‘the hero of heroes’ — i.e. an outstanding warrior whose deeds are being shown.
segments.s677-g24: This is a stock enumeration of war-equipment. تگ modifies اسپ (classically meaning a single/strong/active steed here), اسپ = ‘steed/horse’. Then و شمشیر و گرز lists the sword and the mace. نبرد at the end is a genitive/attributive noun ‘of battle’ specifying that these are implements of war. The word order is a terse, poetic noun string rather than a full clause.
segments.s677-g529: This is a nominal clause with no copula: 'تو بیماری' = 'you [are] sickness' (predicate nominative). The coordination 'و پند داروی' links a second nominal statement: 'پند [is] دارو' (advice is the medicine/remedy). The final token 'تست' is an archaic/poetic fused form attached to the clause (here functioning like an old copular or emphatic particle), rather than a lexical English 'test'.
segments.s677-g530: بگوییم is a 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive 'let us say' / 'we will say'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that' governing the following subjunctive. شوی is 2nd-person singular subjunctive of شدن 'become'. تن درست is a noun + adjective 'a sound/healthy body' (درست here = 'sound/proper'); word order is Subject/Goal (تو) + predicate (شوی تن درست).
segments.s677-g531: وگر is the conditional 'if / but if'. چیزه 'any small thing' or 'anything' is the subject of شد 'becomes/occurs'. بر دلت is a locative phrase 'upon your heart' where ت is the 2nd-person enclitic marking possession ('your'). کام و رشک are two coordinated nouns 'desire and envy' that describe what may come upon the heart.
segments.s677-g532: سخن گوی is an imperative/command 'speak (words)' — گوی here functions as the verbal stem 'say/speak' used imperatively with the object سخن. تا introduces purpose/result 'so that'. دیگر آرم is a short clause with آرم ('I rest' / 'may I rest') in a poetically inflected first-person form; پزشک is nominative apposition 'physician' identifying the speaker. The overall sense: 'Speak, so that I (the physician) may be relieved.'
segments.s677-g533: This is again copula-less predication in classical Persian style: 'پزشک تو پندست' reads as 'your physician is counsel' (پندست = پند است, an archaic contraction of 'پند است'). The coordination 'و دارو خرد' places خرد ('wisdom') as the medicine/remedy: 'and wisdom is the drug/medicine.' Word order is predicate after subject, with the copula implied.
segments.s677-g534: مگر here means 'unless'. آز تاج is a genitive-like phrase 'greed (آز) for the crown (تاج)'. از دلت attaches the preposition 'from / out of' to 'دل' with the 2nd-person enclitic ت ('from your heart'). بسترد is an older poetic verb form meaning 'remove / take away' (optative/jussive sense here: 'should remove' or 'may remove'); it expresses the exceptional condition governed by مگر.
segments.s677-g535: به پیروزی اندر is a prepositional phrase 'into/within victory' (به/اندر both locate the action). چنین 'thus/so' modifies the following verb complex. کش شدی is a compound where کش (from کشیدن 'to pull/draw') combines with شدی (past/perfective of شدن) to mean 'you were drawn/carried away' — an agentive/passive-like formation ('were carried away').
segments.s677-g536: وز is an archaic poetic variant of از meaning 'from' or 'because of' and introduces the cause. اندیشه 'thought/ambition' is the causal noun. گنج سرکش places سرکش ('rebellious/insolent/wild') as a predicate modifier of گنج (treasure) or more idiomatically describes the subject's attitude toward wealth; شدی is the past second-person 'you became.' Together: 'and from ambition you became insolent about treasure.'
segments.s677-g553: تو خواهی که is a 2nd-person present/future desire construction: تو (you) + خواهی (you want/wish) + که (that) introduces the subordinate clause. جنگی سپاهی گران is a noun phrase in classical order: جنگی (warlike, adjectival) modifies سپاهی (army/host), and گران is a postpositive adjective meaning 'formidable/heavy' — together 'a formidable war-host'. The clause sets up what the addressee wants.
segments.s677-g554: همه (all/every) introduces a coordinated noun phrase: نامداران (the renowned, 'famous ones') و (and) کنداوران (a second group/name in the poem). In classical Persian lists like this name two kinds of people who make up the described host; کنداوران is a plural noun here functioning in apposition to نامداران.
segments.s677-g555: شود is a 3rd-person singular present/future auxiliary 'becomes/will be'. بوم ایران is the subject ('the land/realm of Iran'). ازیشان is the contracted prepositional phrase از ایشان ('from/of them') and تهی is a predicate adjective 'empty/devoid (of)'; together: 'the land of Iran will become empty of them.'
segments.s677-g556: شکست (defeat) is a noun subject and آید (comes) is the verb; اندر آید is a common verbal phrase meaning 'will come upon' or 'befall'. به تخت مهی is a locative phrase 'to/upon the fair/moonlike throne' (مهی here an epithet 'beautiful/fair' following تخت). The whole clause: 'Defeat will come upon the fair throne.'
segments.s677-g557: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('that'). بد شاه is an adjective + noun 'ill/ill-omened king' (bad modifying شاه). هنگام آرش is a temporal phrase 'at the time of Arash'. بگوی is a nominal form related to گفت‌وگو (here 'speech/tale/conversation') used after the temporal phrase to mean 'the king's speech at Arash's time.'
segments.s677-g558: سرآید is a 3rd-person singular verb here meaning 'to end/cease' (classical/poetic word order often places verb first). مگر is used as 'unless' or 'except'; بر من ('upon me') marks the experiencer/target; این گفت و گوی ('this talk/conversation') is the topical noun at the end. The clause reads roughly 'it should end — unless this talk should fall upon me.'
segments.s677-g559: بدو گفت is inverted classical word order: بدو ('to him') + گفت (here the verb 'said' / 'spoke') and then the subject follows: بهرام (Bahram) — so 'Bahram said to him.' کان گاه شاه is a temporal/introducing phrase: کان (here 'formerly/then' or 'at that time') + گاه (time) + شاه (king), i.e. 'at that time the king...'.
segments.s677-g560: منوچهر (Manuchehr) is the proper name, followed by بد as an evaluative adjective 'bad/ill' (here 'ill-fated' or 'evil' depending on context). با کلاه و سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with helmet (کلاه functioning as 'helmet' in epic usage) and army' — two instruments/companions of the king. The segment names the king and his accoutrements.
segments.s677-g585: This is a noun phrase introduced by the poetic preposition ز (z) meaning 'from/of' that marks provenance or membership: 'ز خاقانیان' = 'from the Khaghanians.' It is followed by the demonstrative آن plus the counted noun phrase 'سه ترک سترگ' = 'those three Turks (lit. three Turks great)'; word order is preposition → possessor → demonstrative → numeral → noun → adjective. 'سترگ' here is an adjective modifying 'ترک' and follows it (classical Persian allows adjective after noun).
segments.s677-g586: Relative clause introduced by که 'who/which': ارغنده بودند is a past-tense copular clause (بودند = 'they were') with ارغنده as a predicate adjective (here 'bloodstained/reddened'). بر سان is a fixed simile 'upon/like' + سان 'like/as', so بر سان گرگ = 'like a wolf' or 'like wolves.' The clause modifies 'آن سه ترک' before it.
segments.s677-g587: An interrogative or rhetorical main clause beginning with کجا 'where' followed by the perfect/pluperfect-like compound گفتـه بودند 'they had said' (گفته + بودند) — the past auxiliary marks prior action. بهرام را places Bahram as the (direct) object/topic of the saying (را is the definite/object marker).
segments.s677-g588: Complement clause introduced by که 'that' giving the content of what was said. ما is the subject 'we'; روز جنگ 'on the day of battle' is a temporal noun phrase. از پی here functions together (from + پی) to mean 'in pursuit of' or 'after' and takes نام را 'the name/reputation' as its object (را marking the definite object). Word order: subject → adverbial/time → prepositional pursuit phrase → object.
segments.s677-g589: An if-clause opened by اگر 'if.' The noun مرده‌گر (literally 'dead-worker', i.e. gravedigger/undertaker) is the agent; زنده 'alive' functions as a predicate or participial modifier (here 'who was made/alive' or 'made alive'). بالای شاه is an older locative/phrase meaning 'before/upon the king' (بالا+ـی used to indicate position relative to شاه). This whole phrase sets up a hypothetical condition.
segments.s677-g590: Continuation of the conditional: به نزد تو 'into/into the presence of you' (به + نزد = 'to the presence of'), followed by آریم, an archaic/poetic subjunctive/hortative form meaning 'we may bring' or 'we were to bring' (here marking the hypothetical action). پیش سپاه 'before the army/host' is an adverbial phrase giving the setting. Word order: destination/recipient (به نزد تو) → verb (آریم) → adverbial (پیش سپاه).
segments.s677-g591: The clause begins with از ایشان 'from/of them' indicating one taken out of that group; سواری 'a rider/horseman' is the subject, followed by a relative clause introduced by که 'who' and the predicate ناپاک بود 'was impure/unclean' (ناپاک + past copula). The structure describes a single member of the previously mentioned three.
segments.s677-g592: A coordinate predication describing that horseman: an initial attribute 'دلاور' (valiant) followed by a series of adjectives linked by و 'and' — 'بد' (bad/evil), 'تند' (swift/impetuous) — and ending with 'ناباک بود' (copula in past). Classical Persian allows stacking of qualities without an explicit copula until the final بود, which applies to the whole series.
segments.s677-g545: چومن (چو من) here functions as a temporal/subordinate particle + pronoun meaning 'when I' (archaic compounding of چو + من). گنج خوی(ش) آشکارا کنم is ordered Subject‑Object‑Verb: گنج خوی(ش) 'my/one's treasure' (خوی + possessive ش) + آشکارا 'openly' modifying the verb کنم; کنم is first person singular present/subjunctive used to express a future/intended action 'I will disclose/make (it) apparent.'
segments.s677-g546: دل جنگیان پرمدارا کنم is a result clause with دل 'heart' governed by the genitive/attributive جنگیان 'of the warriors' (here 'warriors' or 'fighting men'); پرمدارا is a predicate adjective 'magnanimous, generous', and کنم again is first person 'I will make' (causative/active) — together: 'I will make the warriors' hearts magnanimous.'
segments.s677-g547: چو + پیروز گشتی تو is a temporal clause 'when you became victorious.' چو = 'when'; پیروز is predicate adjective 'victorious'; گشتی is the second‑person past/perfect form of گشتن used here 'you became' (poetic word order places تو after the verb). بر + ساوه = 'over/against Saveh' (پیروز گشتن برِِ place = 'victor over'); شاه follows as the subject 'the king' (word order is flexible/poetic).
segments.s677-g548: برآن برنهادند یکسر سپاه: برآن 'thereupon' or 'on that (account)'; برنهادند is a past plural verb 'they set up/raised/deployed' (here used of the king's action, formal/plural or impersonal); یکسر modifies سپاه and means 'entire/complete/all at once'; سپاه = 'army/host'. The clause means the army was mustered in full because of that event.
segments.s677-g549: که introduces a result/purpose clause here (meaning 'so that' or 'that'); هرگز 'never' negates; نبینند is present‑subjunctive/optative 'they may not see' (expressing desired/resultant prohibition); زان پس literally 'from that after' = 'after that' (زان = 'from that'; پس = 'after'); شکست = 'defeat.'
segments.s677-g550: چو 'when'; از خواسته سیر گشتند و مست is a circumstantial clause: از خواسته 'from/with respect to their desire/plunder' (خواسته = 'wish/desire'), سیر (here 'satiety') + گشتند 'they became' = 'they became sated'; و مست adds 'and drunk/intoxicated' (مست) as a coordinating predicate describing their state when the result clause applies.
segments.s677-g551: نباید 'it should not / must not' is an impersonal modal negative introducing a clause with که 'that'; بر دست من is an older/informal instrumental phrase 'by my hand' (بر + دست + من = 'by/at the hand of me'); بر + هلاک here combines with شوند to mean 'be brought to destruction' ('بر' expressing direction/result 'to/into').
segments.s677-g552: شوند is the passive/irreversible result verb 'become / be (destroyed)' governing the following nominative phrase; این دلیران 'these brave ones' is the subject of the passive; بی‑بیم و باک is a compound description 'without fear and without dread' (poetic stock phrase meaning 'fearless, undismayed').
segments.s677-g169: به شاهی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/for kingship' (به + noun). مرا را here is the object 'me' (mَ + را: object marker) and خواندند is a past‑tense 3rd‑person plural verb 'they called/summoned'. آفرین is an interjection placed after the clause ('Bravo!' / an acclamation). Word order is typical for older/poetic Persian: prepositional phrase + object + verb, with the exclamation appended.
segments.s677-g170: نمانم is first‑person negative of ماندن here meaning 'I did not remain / I did not stay'. که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. پی is an adverb 'after; following' or 'in pursuit (of)', linking to the following phrase. برنهی is an adjectival/verb form 'become bare / be stripped' (poetic form) and is followed by the preposition بر + زمین 'on the ground'. Together the clause gives the result/purpose that the speaker did not remain in order that someone be left stripped on the ground.
segments.s677-g171: دگر آنک گفتی is a topicalizing phrase meaning 'the other/another thing that you said' (دگر = 'other/another'; آنک = 'that/which'; گفتی = 'you said' — the verb is the content verb introducing reported speech). که introduces the quoted content: بداختری 'ill‑fatedness / an ill‑fated person'. The clause is elliptical in poetic style: the copula is omitted and the content follows.
segments.s677-g172: نزیبد is an impersonal/predicative form meaning 'does not befit / is not fitting'. تو را places 'you' in the oblique/object role ('for you' — the thing that befits or not). شاهی و مهتری are the two roles/qualities listed ('kingship' and 'preeminence' / 'sovereignty and high rank'). Word order (predicate + object + complements) is characteristic of Persian poetic diction.
segments.s677-g173: ازآن 'from that / therefore' links this utterance to the prior remark. گفتم is the speaker's verb in past tense 'I said'. ای ناسزاوار شاه is a vocative: ای + adjective + noun = 'O unworthy / insulting king' (ناسزاوار = 'deserving insult / unworthy'). The sequence is a conventional formula introducing a direct address in poetry.
segments.s677-g174: که introduces the content clause of what was said: 'that ...'. هرگز is an adverb 'never'. مبادی is plural of مبادِی meaning 'entrances/approaches' (here 'comings/approaches to court'). تو is the possessor/subject 'your'. در پیشگاه is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the presence (of the throne / court)'. The clause therefore asserts that the addressee's approaches will never be (welcome/allowed) before the court.
segments.s677-g175: که continues the reported content 'that ...'. ایرانیان is the plural subject 'the Iranians'. بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'against you'. بر + دشمنند is a predicative construction: دشمن + اند (copula) meaning 'are enemies / are hostile'. In older/poetic Persian the copula may be split (دشمن + ند here as tokenization); overall: 'that the Iranians are against you / are your enemies.'
segments.s677-g176: بکوشند is a 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive form 'they strive / they will (try to)'. و is the conjunction 'and'. بیخت زبن برکنند is a compacted/poetic phrase: syntactically it behaves as object + oblique + verb — '(your) بخت (fortune) (from its) root (ز بن) they will uproot/clear away' — برکنند is 3rd‑person plural 'they uproot/remove'. The phrase is archaic/poetic in spacing and morphology (energia of older Persian); the intended sense is 'they will try and tear up your luck by the root.'
segments.s677-g281: تو is the poetic/reserved 'thou' (addressing the person). فرزند اویی literally 'son of him' — اویی is an older/poetic possessive formation meaning 'of him' (so 'your being his son' is expressed). نباشد here is an archaic impersonal/optative negation meaning 'it is not fitting/it should not be' (not an imperative). سزا is used in the sense 'that which is deserved / fitting'; together 'نباشد سزا' = 'it is not right/fitting'.
segments.s677-g282: به ایران و توران is a prepositional phrase ('to/over Iran and Turan') placed before the verb for poetic word order. شده is the participial/past form 'become' (here used like 'has become / become'), and پادشا is the predicate noun 'king/monarch' — literally 'has become king of Iran and Turan.'
segments.s677-g283: تو را uses را as the definite/object/topic marker placed after the pronoun to topicalize/emphasize 'you'. زندگانی نباید uses the impersonal/modal نباید ('one ought not / should not') with زندگانی as the thing denied — 'you ought not to have life'; نه تخت coordinates a negation 'nor a throne' (نه = 'not / nor').
segments.s677-g284: یکی دخمه‌ای بس is a compact clause: یکی 'one/a single' with دخمه‌ای (indefinite -ای) 'tomb/sepulchre', and بس 'enough' as the predicate ('one tomb is enough'). که introduces an explanatory clause ('for / because / that') and دوری ز بخت is a prepositional phrase using poetic ز (from) + بخت 'fortune/fate' to mean 'being far from fortune'; together giving the reason why one tomb suffices.
segments.s677-g285: هم 'also / too' marks addition. ان کین 'that vengeance' (ان = 'that', کین = 'vengeance/hatred') is the object. هرمز here is the name 'Hormoz(d)' (proper noun). کنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive form 'I do / I will do' used here to state the speaker's intention, and خواستار functions as a predicative adjective/noun 'desirous / seeker' — together conveying 'I too desire/seek that vengeance.'
segments.s677-g286: دگر here means 'else/other' (sense 'elsewhere' or 'another place'). کاندر is the fused phrase که اندر ('in / within'), so کاندر ایران = 'in Iran'. منم is the emphatic 'I am' (من + enclitic م), and شهریار is the predicate noun 'king' — 'elsewhere in Iran, I am king.' The sentence uses poetic inversion: locative phrase precedes the subject-predicate.
segments.s677-g287: کنون 'now' sets temporal frame. تازه کن is an imperative: تازه 'new/fresh' + کن (imperative 'do') = 'renew / tell anew'. برمن (بر + من) means 'to me' ('upon me' idiomatically = 'for me'). این داستان is the direct object 'this tale/story' placed after the imperative as is common in Persian.
segments.s677-g288: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which / so that'. از راستان literally 'from the righteous/upright' (از = 'from/of', راستان = 'the upright/true ones'). گشت is the past/preterite 'became / turned', and همداستان is an adjective/noun meaning 'of one mind / in agreement' (or 'accomplice' in some contexts) — here 'so that it becomes consonant/agreeing with the righteous.'
segments.s677-g417: بران here functions as a prepositional/adverbial particle meaning 'upon/at that' and introduces the location; تخت سیمین is a noun + adjective phrase 'silver throne' (تخت = throne, سیمین = silver). The coordinating و links that noun phrase to آن مهرشاه ('that Mehrshah'), so the whole segment names two loci/figures ('upon that silver throne and that Mehrshah').
segments.s677-g418: سرت is the possessed noun 'your head' (object of the following predicate); مست شد is the verb phrase 'became intoxicated' (مست = intoxicated/adjective, شد = became). بازگشتی ز راه is a separate clause 'you turned/returned from the road' where بازگشتی is a perfective/past participial notion 'you turned back/returned' and ز راه literally 'from the road/path'; the two clauses are consecutive (resultive/explanatory).
segments.s677-g419: کنون is an adverb 'now/presently' setting time. نام چوبینه گشت is a pseudo-passive phrasing meaning 'was named / came to be called "Chubineh"' (نام = name; چوبینه functions as the name/nickname 'Chubineh' derived from چوب 'club'); بهرام is the person affected, so the whole order 'کنون نام چوبینه بهرام گشت' means 'now Bahram acquired the name Chubineh.'
segments.s677-g420: همان تخت سیمین is 'that same silver throne' (همان = that/the same; تخت سیمین = silver throne). تو را دام گشت is a verbal clause with گشت 'became' and دام 'a snare/trap' as predicate: تو را (object-marking phrase) indicates 'for you' (تو را = you as affected); thus 'that same silver throne became a snare for you.'
segments.s677-g421: برآن تخت 'on that throne' places the scene (برآن = 'on/at that' + تخت = 'throne'). بر ماه خواهی شدن is a predicative infinitival construction where خواهی (here functioning as a future/modal auxiliary 'you will') + شدن 'become' yields 'you will become'; بر ماه (literally 'on the moon') idiomatically means 'like the moon' (i.e. shining/beautiful/illustrious) — so 'on that throne you will become like the moon.'
segments.s677-g422: This segment lists offices/roles as a series of results: سپهبد ... شاه خواهی شدن uses خواهی شدن again as future 'you will become.' سپهبد names one high military office (spahbed), and شاه 'king' is the later outcome; the line reads as coordinated predicates: 'you will be spahbed, (and) you will become king.'
segments.s677-g423: سخن زین نشان مرد دانا نگفت is a negated clause: سخن 'speech/words' is the theme, زین نشان literal components here mean 'about this token/sign' (زین = 'of/from this' in classical usage, نشان = 'sign/token'). مرد دانا 'a wise man' is the subject; نگفت is a past negated verb 'did not say' (classical word order places the verb at the end). Overall: 'A wise man would not speak words concerning this sign.'
segments.s677-g424: بر آنم که is a conventional classical phrase meaning 'I am of that opinion / I hold that'; که introduces the content clause. با دیو گشتی تو جفت is a predicative statement 'you have turned into/been matched with a demon' where با دیو = 'with a demon' and جفت = 'paired/mated' (تو is the affected person). The overall sense: 'I think that you have become paired with a demon.'
segments.s677-g321: پذیرفتم is a first-person singular past tense verb ('I accepted'). In this line the verb appears before its object (پذیرفتم این), a poetic inversion of the usual Persian SOV order; این is the direct object 'this'. از introduces the source/agent phrase ('from'), and خدای جهان is a noun phrase 'God of the world' (noun + postposed modifier 'جهان' forming a genitive/possessive relationship).
segments.s677-g322: شناسنده is an active participle 'knower' used as an epithet qualifying the preceding noun phrase (i.e. God). آشکار و نهان are two coordinated adjectives 'manifest/clear and hidden', joined by و; together they form the descriptive phrase 'knower of the manifest and the hidden'. The adjective phrase follows the noun it modifies, a common order in Persian poetry.
segments.s677-g323: به دستوری is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by the command' (به used here in the sense 'by/through' and دستوری is 'command/order'). هرمز شهریار is an appositional or titulary sequence: هرمز is the name (here the deity Ahura Mazda) followed by شهریار 'king' as a title; together they form the genitive/agent of the command.
segments.s677-g324: کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where' used rhetorically. داشت is the simple past third-person singular 'he had / he kept' and functions as the main verb. تاج پدر is a noun phrase 'the father's crown' (تاج + postposed modifier پدر), and یادگار is an appositive/adverbial noun 'as a memento' describing the state or purpose of the crown.
segments.s677-g325: ازان is a fused demonstrative/prepositional particle 'from that / of that' linking back to something mentioned earlier. نامور 'renowned' and پر هنر 'full of skill/art' are adjectival phrases describing the thing in question; note پر precedes the noun (پر + noun = 'full of X'). بخردان is the plural form meaning 'to the wise' (بخرد + -ان), functioning as an indirect-object-like recipient phrase.
segments.s677-g326: This is a coordinated list of recipients: بزرگان 'the great ones' (nominative/oblique plural), و is the conjunction 'and', and کارآزموده ردان is a compound describing the last class of people — کارآزموده 'experienced/proven' modifies ردان. The whole phrase names categories of people who receive or are concerned with the object just mentioned.
segments.s677-g327: بدان دین is a prepositional-demonstrative phrase 'to/in that religion' (ب here marks direction/relation; دان functions historically as part of the demonstrative but does not correspond to the provided verbal senses, so it is treated as a demonstrative element). که introduces a relative clause modifying دین. آورده بود is a pluperfect construction: آورده (past participle 'brought') + بود (past auxiliary) = 'had brought'. از بهشت 'from paradise' is the source phrase of that action.
segments.s677-g328: خرد یافته is a compound adjectival expression: خرد (wisdom) + یافته (acquired/found) = 'having acquired wisdom' or 'wise'. It modifies پیر 'elder' (here 'پیر' functions as 'elder' or 'sage'), and سر here is 'chief/head' followed by زردهشت (Zarathushtra / Zoroaster) as a proper name in apposition. The whole cluster names Zoroaster with epithets: 'the wisdom-acquired elder, head (i.e. chief) Zoroaster.'
segments.s677-g233: پرستنده فرخ is a poetic juxtaposition (no ezafe) meaning 'worshipper of (good) fortune'; آتش کنم is a volitional/periphrastic clause with کنم (1st person singular) expressing 'I will set/ kindle/ make into fire'. Word order places the object/phrase (پرستنده فرخ آتش) before the verb کنم, typical in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s677-g234: دل موبد و هیربد is an adjectival/noun cluster meaning 'the heart of the mobed and the hirbad' (the nouns are juxtaposed without an explicit ezafe); خوش کنم uses the same 1st-person کنم to mean 'I will make (it) happy/pleasant'. دل is the direct object and خوش is the predicate adjective formed before the verb.
segments.s677-g235: بگفت is a past-tense reporting verb 'he said'; این picks out 'this' (the foregoing words); وز here functions as an archaic/poetic prepositional element ('from' / 'out of') governing خاک, so وز خاک means 'from the dust'. بر پای خاست is a compound verbal phrase 'rose to his feet' with خاست (past) as the main verb.
segments.s677-g236: ستمدیده (an adjective) modifies گوینده 'the speaker' and means 'oppressed/afflicted'; بود is the past copula 'was' and راست is a predicate adjective meaning 'true' or 'truthful'. The structure is [adjective + noun] + بود + predicate adjective: 'the speaker was oppressed and true.'
segments.s677-g237: ز جای نیایش 'from the place of prayer' uses ز as the poetic preposition 'from' followed by the noun phrase جای نیایش (place of prayer). بیامد is the past 'he came'. چو گرد is a simile/adverbial phrase 'like a dust-whirl' (چو = 'like/as', گرد used as 'dust/whirl').
segments.s677-g238: به بهرام is the indirect object 'to/at Bahram'; چوبینه here indicates instrument/attribute ('with a club' / 'club-like') attached to the action; آواز کرد is a verbal phrase 'he made a cry / he called' with کرد as the past auxiliary/verb 'did/made'. The sequence thus says he cried out to Bahram, with a wooden club (or club in hand).
segments.s677-g239: که‌ای is an archaic/vocative particle here (addressing someone: 'O you'); the following adjectives/nouns (دوزخی، بنده، دیو، نر) are a string of epithets in apposition: 'hellish, slave, demon, male' — common in heroic/epic vocatives where Ezafe is omitted and epithets pile up.
segments.s677-g240: خرد is the subject 'wisdom'; دور و دور is a repetitive intensifier 'far and far' functioning as predicate/adverbial complement; از تو ('from you') links the next nouns (آیین و فر) as being absent/removed from the addressee. The line therefore asserts that wisdom is remote and that ritual (آیین) and royal glory (فر) are distant from him.
segments.s677-g361: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از + noun expresses origin/parentage ('of/from Babak's daughter'). بزاد is an archaic past form 'was born' (third-person singular) with the verb after the object; اردشیر is the subject placed after the verb, a common poetic word order.
segments.s677-g362: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' referring back to 'Ardashir'. اشکانیان را places 'the Parthians' as the definite object (را). بدی is the noun 'evil/harm' used predicatively ('being a harm/scourge'); دار و گیر form a paired phrase meaning roughly 'holder/seizer' or 'one who seizes' (poetic epithets).
segments.s677-g363: نه چون is a negative comparative opening ('not as/like when...'). اردشیر is the agent; اردوان را marks Ardavan as the definite object; بکشت is an archaic past form meaning 'he killed' — here the old prefix ب‑ appears before the past stem (poetic morphology).
segments.s677-g364: به نیرو شد is 'he became (into) power/strong' — به + noun + شد indicates becoming endowed with that quality. و connects the clauses. تختش آمد به مشت is an idiomatic sequence: تختش ('his throne') + آمد ('came') + به مشت ('into the fist') meaning the throne came into his grasp; مشت is literal 'fist' used metaphorically for 'grasp'.
segments.s677-g365: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. سال چون پانصد combines 'year' + چون + a numeral to mean 'when/about five hundred years' (چون here functions in a temporal/comparative sense). برگذشت is the past verb 'passed by' (third-person singular).
segments.s677-g366: سر تاج is a genitival/compound sequence 'the head/top of the crown'; ساسانیان qualifies which crown ('of the Sasanians'). سرد گشت is 'became cold' (سرد + the past-of-become گشت), here used metaphorically to mean decline or loss of vigor.
segments.s677-g367: کنون 'now' again opens the clause. تخت و دیهیم را places 'throne and diḥīm' (rural/peasant domain or countryside) as the topical/definite objects with را. روز ماست is a copular phrase 'it is our day' = 'the time belongs to us' (ما + ست = 'is ours').
segments.s677-g368: سرو کار is a nominal pairing ('cypress/lofty things' + 'affairs') that together evokes prosperous/high affairs; با introduces the instrumental/associative 'with'. بخت پیروز ماست is 'our victorious fate is with (us)' or 'our affairs are aligned with our victorious fortune' (پیروز as adjective 'victorious'; ست = copula 'is').
segments.s677-g481: The first word is the conditional particle and demonstrative contracted (گر + این = «گر این») meaning 'if this'. The phrase 'پادشاهی ز تخم کیان' is a genitival phrase: پادشاهی (kingship/kingdom) governed by ز + تخم کیان ('from the seed/origin of the Kayanids'). Word order is poetic but literal: 'if this kingship from the seed of the Kayanids...'.
segments.s677-g482: بخواهد is a 3rd-person present/future/desiderative form 'wishes/ would like'; it takes the infinitive شدن 'to become' as its complement. تو here is best read as the possessor ('become yours') and چه introduces the interrogative 'what'; بندی is a noun derived from the verb 'بستن' (a bond/tie) and میان 'between' completes the locative relation. The whole segment asks 'what bond is there between you?'
segments.s677-g483: چو here functions as 'when' (or 'if, when it is the case that'); اسکندری is used generically 'an Alexander' (i.e. someone like Alexander). باید is the impersonal/modal 'is necessary / is needed', and اندر جهان sets the domain 'in the world'. The clause says 'when an Alexander is needed in the world...'.
segments.s677-g484: که here introduces a relative/result clause referring back to 'اسکندری' ('who'); تیره کند is a light verb construction where تیره (dark/ominous) is predicated and کند 'makes/does' is the verb: 'who will make (it) dark'. بخت شاهنشهان 'the fortune/destiny of the shahanshahs' is the object affected. Note tense/mood: کند gives a general/future sense ('will cause').
segments.s677-g485: توبا is orthographic compression of تو با ('you with'); the line lists attributes: چهره دیو 'a demon's face' (چهره = face/appearance, دیو = demon), then با رنگ و خاک 'with color/complexion and dust' — two coordinating prepositional phrases introduced by با. The adjectives/nominals describe the person's appearance.
segments.s677-g486: مبادی is plural and here means 'places/standing/origins' (i.e. any place or standing) in the world; به گیتی 'in the world' sets the domain. جز 'except' negates all other places, and اندر مغاک 'in the abyss' names the only place left. Syntactically this says there is no place for you in the world except the abyss.
segments.s677-g487: ز here means 'because of'; بی + راهی forms the compound بی‌راهی 'straying/misguidance' (بی negates راهی). کارکرد تو is 'your deeds/performance' (کارکرد as 'actions' or 'effect of work'), and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause gives the causal premise: 'It was because of your misguidance and deeds.'
segments.s677-g488: که introduces the result clause 'that ...'. شد is the past of 'become/happen' ('became'), روز here can mean 'day' or metaphorically 'time/fortune' and is used with برشاه ایران 'for/upon the King of Iran'. کبود is an adjective 'dusky/dark' describing the resulting state. Together: 'that the King's day/fortune became dark.'
segments.s677-g353: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative past copula meaning 'there was not' or 'there was no'; it comes first and governs the following noun phrase هیچ بد 'any/any kind of evil' (هیچ after the verb emphasizes 'none'). جز introduces an exception and takes the prepositional phrase به فرمان تو 'by/through your command' (به here functions as 'by').
segments.s677-g354: وگر is a conditional/concessive conjunction 'and if / but if' introducing an implied clause (the verb 'بود' or similar is omitted). What follows is a coordinated list of causes connected by و: 'تنبل و مکر و دستان تو' — 'laziness and deceit and your hands' — where ودستان should be read as و دستان (and the hands) with تو as the possessive 'your'. The verb is omitted and understood from the previous clause.
segments.s677-g355: گر begins a conditional 'if'. ایزد بخواهد is a subordinate clause 'if God wills' where بخواهد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive of خواستن ('wills/permits'). The sequence 'من از کین شاه' starts the speaker's clause: من 'I' followed by the ablative/causal phrase از کین 'out of/for vengeance' and شاه which will serve as the object/complement of the verb کنَم in the next segment ('to make king').
segments.s677-g356: کنم is first-person present/subjunctive (used here with a future/intent sense) 'I will make / I will do'. بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'upon/against you' (بر = 'on/upon' or 'against/over' depending on context). خورشید روشن سیاه is the object phrase: 'the bright sun black' — poetically 'I will blacken the bright sun' (روشن modifies خورشید; سیاه is the result applied to it).
segments.s677-g357: کنون is an adverb 'now' placed sentence-initially. The rest is a cleft-like question: 'تاج را درخور کار کیست' literally 'who is the crown's fitting one according to deed?' را marks تاج as the definite/accusative object/topic, درخور کار means 'worthy according to work/deeds' and کیست is the interrogative 'who is'.
segments.s677-g358: چو here is conditional/subordinating and functions 'if/when'; the clause reads 'چو من ناسزایم' = 'if I am undeserving' or 'since I am unworthy' with ناسزایم describing the speaker's lack of merit (the copula 'am' is understood). سزاوار کیست then asks 'who is deserving?' — a rhetorical contrast dependent on the preceding subordinate clause.
segments.s677-g359: بدو گفت بهرام is inverted word order common in classical Persian: بدو 'to him' + گفت 'said' + بهرام (the speaker) = 'Bahram said to him'. کای is the vocative particle 'O', and مرد 'man' is the vocative address. گرد is ambiguous here: it can be an epithet or an imperative/archaic form; grammatically it follows the vocative and either modifies 'مرد' or is an imperative-like admonition. The phrase functions as direct speech introduction.
segments.s677-g360: سزا آن بود is a fixed expression 'it was deserved/fitting' (سزا = 'desert'/'just penalty'; آن a demonstrative). کز introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'that/from which' and governs 'کز تو شاهی ببرد' = 'that kingship be taken from you.' ببرد is a 3rd-person form of برد (with the prefix ب-), here used as a jussive/subjunctive sense 'should/would take' or 'be taken' in reported/desiderative construction.
segments.s677-g225: A short noun list introduced by the demonstrative hemen/این: 'همین' (this) determinative precedes the first noun; items are linked by و (and). Word order is noun phrases in apposition (this X and Y and this Z). 'یاره' here appears as an ornamental item in a list (poetic vocabulary), while طوق and گوشوار are ordinary nouns meaning necklace and earring.
segments.s677-g226: Another noun phrase with the demonstrative 'همین' (this) modifying 'جامه' (robe/garment). The sequence 'زر گوهرنگار' is a poetic compound: زر (gold) qualifies the robe and گوهرنگار is an adjectival/participial descriptor meaning 'gem‑adorned' (literally 'written/painted with gems'). Adjectival qualifiers follow or cluster after the head noun in such classical lines.
segments.s677-g227: 'همان' is the demonstrative 'the same/that' referring back to the previously named item. 'نیزده' is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'also give' (imperative/optative sense 'give too'). 'بدره' is a poetic epithet 'like the full moon' used here as a simile for shining coins; 'دینار زرد' = 'yellow/golden dinars.'
segments.s677-g228: 'فشانم' is first‑person singular present/subjunctive ('I scatter' / 'I will scatter') with the -م ending. 'برین' is the bound form of 'بر این' (on/upon this) written together for metrical reasons. 'گنبد لاژورد' is the object/place: 'dome of lapis (lazuli)' — 'لاژورد' qualifies the گنبد.
segments.s677-g229: Object phrase + verb order typical of poetry: 'پرستندگان را' (the worshippers, with را marking a definite object) followed by the verb 'دهم' (I give). The numeral 'ده هزار' (ten thousand) is the amount given and is understood as the object of 'دهم'.
segments.s677-g230: 'درم' names the coin (dirham). 'چون شوم' is a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if I become' (چون here functions as 'when/as' and شوم is 1st‑person 'may/become'). 'بر جهان شهریار' = 'lord over the world' (بر = over/on, جهان = world, شهریار = king/lord); the clause as a whole gives the time/condition for the gift.
segments.s677-g231: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/among'; 'بهرامیان' names the group (the Bahramians). 'هرک' is an indefinite relative 'whoever/anyone who'; 'گردد اسیر' is a passive/result clause 'becomes captive' (گردد = becomes/happens; اسیر = captive). The segment sets the conditional protasis.
segments.s677-g232: 'به پیش من آرد' is an instructive clause: 'bring to my presence' where آرد (brings) is the present/subjunctive 3rd‑person form. 'کسی دستگیر' places an indefinite 'کسی' (someone/anyone) before 'دستگیر' (the seized/captive) — poetic word order putting the verb before the noun phrase; together: 'let someone bring the captured person to me.'
segments.s677-g73: This is an indirect interrogative introduced by the initial که meaning “who” (an interrogative). داندکه is the verb “knows” fused with the subordinating که that introduces the reported question; the clause در جنگ پیروز کیست is the content of that question: در جنگ “in battle,” پیروز “victorious” (predicate adjective) and کی + ست form the question “who is (victorious)?” — literally “who knows who in battle is victorious.”
segments.s677-g74: بدان is here to be read as the administration of دان with a preceding particle (بِ/بـ) meaning roughly “regarding/as to that” (i.e. “and as to that”); سر دگر is “another head/chief” (سر = leader), لشکر افروز is a compound epithet “army-illuminating” (افروز as adjective modifying لشکر) and کیست again asks “who is it?” — together: “and as to that, who is the other army‑illuminator?”
segments.s677-g75: برین گونه is the adverbial phrase بر + این (written as ین here) meaning “in this manner / thus”; آراسته is a postpositive adjective “adorned/arrayed,” and لشکری is the noun “army/troops.” Word order places the manner-phrase first, then the adjectival description and the noun it qualifies.
segments.s677-g76: به here marks accompaniment/instrumental sense (“with / for the assault”), پرخاش is “assault/aggression.” بهرام is the proper name; یل is an epithet “hero/brave man,” and مهتری is an epithet “chief/ foremost.” Together they form an appositive: “with the assault (led by) Bahram, a hero and foremost (among men).”
segments.s677-g77: دژاگاه مردی is a genitive construction: دژاگاه “stronghold/fortress” + مردی “of a man” (possessive). چو introduces a simile “like/as,” followed by دیو (demon). سترگ is an adjectival epithet attached to دیو (here describing the demon’s quality).
segments.s677-g78: سپاهی “a troop/warrior(s)” is followed by the manner phrase به کردار “in the conduct/behavior of” and درنده گرگ is descriptive: درنده “predatory/ferocious” modifying گرگ “wolf.” The whole phrase means the soldiers act in the manner of a predatory wolf.
segments.s677-g79: گر is the conditional “if.” ایدون (here functioning adverbially) conveys a sense of ‘henceforth/from now on’ or ‘to come,’ که is the complementizer “that/that (we)…” and باشیم is the present-subjunctive first-person-plural “we be/are” (expressing the protasis). همداستان is a predicate/adjective meaning “like‑minded / accomplices.”
segments.s677-g80: نباشد is the negative existential/capability copula “there will not be / may not be.” مرا is the dative/accusative “to me” (here split into tokens m + را in the word list), ننگ is the noun “shame/disgrace,” and زین داستان is a prepositional/genitive phrase “from this tale” (زین = ز + این, “from this,” modifying داستان “story/tale”). The whole clause: “there will be no shame for me from this tale.”
segments.s677-g369: چو here is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” بینیم is a 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive form meaning “we see” or “when we see.” The nouns چهر (face) and بخت (fate/fortune) follow the verb as its objects, and تو (your) is repeated as the possessive pronoun; the clause lists possessions/qualities seen.
segments.s677-g370: This is a simple noun series connected by و (and): سپاه (army) + کلاه (helmet/covering) + تخت (throne), each followed by the possessive تو (your). The repeated تو emphasizes that all these items belong to the addressee; و is the coordinating conjunction “and.”
segments.s677-g371: بیازم is a 1st‑person singular (optative/intentional) form meaning “I will spring/attack” or “I will break forth.” بدین کار = به + این کار “upon/with this deed” (a prepositional phrase) and ساسانیان is the target/indirect object “the Sasanians.” Together the clause expresses the speaker’s intended action against the Sasanians.
segments.s677-g372: چو begins a simile here and means “like/as.” آشفته qualifies شیری: آشفته “frantic/disturbed” modifies شیری (leonine, i.e. “lion‑like” or simply “lion”). که introduces the subordinate clause ‘that becomes…’ and گردد is the 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of گردیدن “become.” ژیان means “life” (i.e. “comes to life” or “is roused”).
segments.s677-g373: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” دفتر here means “register/book” and همه نامشان = “all their names” (همه = all, نام = name, شان = their). بسترم is used here as a verb in 1st person singular idiomatically meaning “I will efface/blot out” (an archaic or poetic verbal form), so the whole clause says the speaker will remove their names from the register.
segments.s677-g374: سر تخت means “the head upon the throne” (سر = head, تخت = throne) and ساسانیان marks whose throne it is (Sasanian throne). بسپرم is a 1st‑person singular present/optative form of سپردن “to place/commit/lay (upon),” so the clause says the speaker will place their heads on the Sasanian throne.
segments.s677-g375: بزرگی (greatness) is the nominal topic; مر is the dative/benefactive pronoun “to me” (or “for me”). اشکانیان را marks the Parthians (را as the topical/accusative marker). سزاست is a fixed 3rd‑person predicate meaning “it is fitting/it is deserved.” The clause literally means “The greatness of the Parthians is fitting to me.”
segments.s677-g376: اگر is the conditional “if.” بشنود is a 3rd‑person singular subjunctive/optative of شنیدن “to hear” (here: “should hear”/“hears”). مرد داننده راست is a three‑word noun phrase modifying the hypothetical listener: مرد (man/person), داننده (knowing/knowledgeable), راست (right/true/upright). So: “if a true, knowing man hears (it).”
segments.s677-g313: 'جهاندار' (subject) comes first as the agent 'the sovereign'; 'شاهی' is the thing created ('kingship') and appears before the oblique phrase 'ز داد' ('from justice'). The preposition 'ز' (poetic 'from') introduces the source 'داد' ('justice'), and the verb 'آفرید' is final (past 3rd sg), following the SOV order typical of classical Persian.
segments.s677-g314: 'دگر' here means 'again' / 'another' and signals a second act; 'از هنر' = 'from skill/merit' with 'از' (from) before the noun. The item 'وز نژاد' is a poetic fusion of 'و ز' (= 'and from') linking 'هنر' and 'نژاد', and the verb 'آفرید' repeats at the end (past 3rd sg). Note: 'وز' is a fused poetic form (='و ز') meaning 'and from'.
segments.s677-g315: 'بدان کس' = 'to that person' (ب = to; 'دان' here is an old/demonstrative element merged with the preposition), with 'کس' 'person' as the referent. The verb 'دهد' (3rd sg) follows the object. 'کو' is an archaic relative/interrogative particle here meaning 'who/that', and 'سزاوارتر' is a comparative adjective 'more deserving' modifying 'کس'.
segments.s677-g316: A triad of comparative qualities describes that person: 'خرددارتر' ('more prudent/wiser') followed by 'هم' ('also/even') linking the next quality, and 'بی آزارتر' literally 'more without-harm' — i.e. 'less harmful'. 'بی' is the negating prefix 'without' and the comparative is formed with the suffix '-تر'.
segments.s677-g317: 'الان' = 'now'. The sequence 'شاه ما را پدر کرده بود' uses 'شاه' as subject, 'ما را' ('us' with the definite/object marker 'را') as the beneficiary/affected party, and the compound past 'پدر کرده بود' ('had made [father]') — 'کرده' is the perfect participle and 'بود' is the past auxiliary, so the whole phrase is past perfect: 'had made (father) for us'.
segments.s677-g318: 'کجا' is a rhetorical/archaic interrogative here ('how/where' in the sense 'how could it be that'), 'برمن' = 'against/upon me' (بر + من), 'از کارت' = 'because of your deed' where 'از' marks cause, and 'آزرده بود' is past perfect/passive-stative 'was offended/distressed' (with 'بود' as auxiliary). The clause asks why he would be displeased with the speaker on account of the other's action.
segments.s677-g319: 'کنون' = 'now'; 'ایزدم' is 'ایزد' ('god') plus the possessive suffix '-م' ('my'), serving as the subject. 'داد' here is the verbal past 'gave/granted' and 'شاهنشهی' is the direct object 'imperial kingship' (compound noun). The clause follows subject–object–verb order.
segments.s677-g320: A simple enumerative noun phrase of gifts: 'بزرگی' ('greatness'), 'تخت' ('throne'), and 'کلاه مهی' ('a crown described by the adjective مهی'). The coordinating conjunction 'و' links the items, and 'مهی' is a postpositive adjective meaning 'moonlike' or 'beautiful', modifying 'کلاه'.
segments.s677-g561: بدو (bedu) is a prepositional clitic meaning 'to him / unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' with the subject following (typical older Persian word order: 'to him said Khosrow'). خسرو is the subject/name. که‌ای بدنهان is a vocative / address: که‌ای (archaic/poetic 'که ای') functions like 'O (who/you)', and بدنهان is an epithet (poetic/compound) modifying the addressee ('ill‑hidden' / treacherous); together they form the direct address.
segments.s677-g562: چو دانی is a concessive/causal phrase 'as/since you know' (چو = 'as/since/when', دانی = 2nd person present of 'to know'). که introduces the content clause 'that he was king of the world' (او = 'he', بود = past copula 'was', شاه جهان = 'king of the world').
segments.s677-g563: ندانی is negative 2nd‑person form 'you do not know' (ن + دانی). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that Arash was his servant' (آرش = proper name, ورا = 'to him'/'of him' functioning as an indirect object/possessive reference here, بنده = 'servant/slave', بود = past copula 'was').
segments.s677-g564: به فرمان و رایش forms an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/through his command and his counsel' (به + فرمان = 'by command', و = 'and', رایش = 'his counsel/opinion'). سرافکنده بود is a compound predication: سرافکنده = 'ashamed/abashed' (adjective/participle) and بود = past copula 'was' (resultant state).
segments.s677-g565: بدو گفت بهرام = 'Bahram said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = past 'said', بهرام = subject). کز is the contracted form of 'که از' or a relative/prepositional 'from which / from whom'; here it introduces the idea 'from whom / by whose (means)'. راه داد is a verbal phrase 'gave way' / 'gave passage' / 'allowed' (راه دادن = 'to give way/allow passage').
segments.s677-g566: تو از تخم ساسانی‌ای بد نژاد is an accusatory statement: تو = 'you', از تخم = 'from the seed/of the stock', ساسانی‌ای = 'a Sasanian' (here adjectival/ethnic), بد = 'bad', نژاد = 'lineage/race'. The order places the origin phrase (از تخم ساسانی) before the quality 'bad‑nurtured/ill‑born' (بد نژاد).
segments.s677-g567: که introduces a reported/explanatory clause 'that Sasan...' (که = 'that/that is'), ساسان is the proper name subject, شبان = 'shepherd' is predicative, و شبان زاده = 'and (was) shepherd‑born' (شبان زاده = 'born of a shepherd' — a noun + زاده as a born/offspring adjective), and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s677-g568: نه is a negative/contrastive particle ('not / it was not'), followed by بابک شبانی in apposition 'Babak the shepherd'. بدو is the indirect object 'to him'/'for him'. داده بود is the pluperfect (past‑perfect) 'had given' (داده = past participle 'given', بود = auxiliary 'had'), so the clause means 'it was not Babak the shepherd who had given him [that origin/position]'.
segments.s677-g105: This is a nominal predicate with an omitted copula: تو (addressing 'you') + درگاه را (the court marked by را for topical/emphatic reference) + همچو پیرایه‌ای (a simile phrase 'like an ornament'). In classical Persian poetry the verb 'to be' is often omitted, so this reads as 'You are to the court like an ornament' or 'You make the court like an ornament.' Word order is Subject (تو) — topicalized NP (درگاه را) — predicate/simile (همچو پیرایه‌ای).
segments.s677-g106: همان precedes the definite NP تخت ودیهیم and identifies it ('that/the same throne and (our) valley'); را again marks that NP as topic/target. مایه‌ای is a predicate formed from the noun مایه with the second-person ending -ی (a common poetic predicative/declaring form), so the clause means 'you are a mây-e (support/resource) of that throne and valley' with the copula omitted. Grammatically the object/topic marked NP (همان تخت ودیهیم را) is linked to the predicative مایه‌ای referring back to تو from the previous clause.
segments.s677-g107: ستون سپاهی is a noun phrase meaning 'pillar/column of the army' (ستون + سپاهی in a genitive/attributive relation). به هنگام رزم is an adverbial temporal phrase ('at the time of battle'), with به هنگام functioning as the prepositional expression. There is no finite verb here; this is a descriptive nominal clause (common in poetry) apposed to the person praised in surrounding lines.
segments.s677-g108: چو introduces a simile ('like/as'), so چو شمع درخشنده is 'like a shining candle'. هنگام بزم is another temporal adverbial ('at the time of the feast/banquet'), completing the image. Again the copula is omitted; the whole phrase functions adjectivally to the praised person (i.e. in festive gatherings you shine like a candle).
segments.s677-g109: جهانجوی is an agent-noun 'seeker of the world' and گردی here functions as a predicative/characterizing element (the -ی/-گردی form commonly indicates 'you are such' in poetic usage), so the phrase reads 'you are world-seeking' or 'you seek the world.' The conjunction و links to یزدان‌پرست ('God-worshipper' / 'pious one'), yielding 'you are world-seeking and pious.'
segments.s677-g110: This is an address and short imperative-like appeal with omitted verbs: مداراد ('forbearance/tolerance' used as a call to be patient or show lenience) + دارنده باز ('holder of the falcon' — a vocative compound: دارنده = holder/possessor, باز = 'falcon') + از تو دست ('from your hand'), where از introduces the source ('from') and دست is 'hand'. Many words and the verb are omitted in poetry, so the sense is 'Show forbearance, holder of the falcon; let your hand (be) from (me/from action),' i.e. 'hold back your hand.'
segments.s677-g111: سگالیده‌ام is a perfect/past form 'I have set (dogs) on' or more generally 'I have harried/assailed' (first-person singular suffix -ام). روزگار تو را is the object (روزگار = fate/fortune; تو = your; را marks the object). The clause is a straightforward past-perfect active clause: 'I have harried your fortunes.'
segments.s677-g112: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'well' or 'with goodness/skill'; بسیجیده is a past participle/adjectival verb (from بسیجیدن, 'to marshal/assemble') meaning 'well marshalled/arrayed'; کار تو را ('your affairs/work' marked by را) is the object. The clause lacks an overt copula in poetry but reads as a coordinated continuation of the previous verb: '(I have) well marshalled your affairs.'
segments.s677-g217: ندارم is the first-person present negative of داشتن (’I do not have’). The phrase به گنج اندرون is a locative prepositional phrase ('in/into the inner treasure-house') that scopes the following direct objects زر و سیم ('gold and silver'). Word order places the negated verb before the locative phrase and objects for a compact, poetic statement.
segments.s677-g218: به گاه پرستش is a temporal adverbial ('at the time/occasion of worship'); به here introduces the time phrase. بپوشم is first-person present/subjunctive form of پوشیدن with the prefixed ب- used in classical Persian for the simple present/subjunctive sense ('I put on / I wear'). گلیم is the direct object ('a kilim').
segments.s677-g219: گر introduces a conditional ('if'). ایدون is an archaic/poetic particle meaning roughly 'it comes to pass / it happens' (used here to mark the hypothetical). که introduces the subordinate clause. این پادشاهی is the noun phrase 'this kingship/kingdom'. مراست is an older fused form meaning 'is mine' (مرا + است); it is the predicate asserting possession. The whole segment sets up a conditional: 'If it happens that this kingship is mine...'.
segments.s677-g220: This is a string of predicative nouns/adjectives joined by و describing the speaker's state if the condition holds. پرستنده functions as 'devout / a worshipper' used predicatively, ایمن as 'safe/secure', داد as 'justice' used adjectivally (i.e. 'just'), and راست as 'right/upright' or 'true'. The conjunctions simply list these qualities.
segments.s677-g221: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. پیروز گردان is a causative/imperative-like sequence: پیروز ('victorious') plus گردان (from گرداندن/گردانیدن) yields 'make (someone) victorious' — a poetic command/request. سپاه مرا is the object 'my army' with the possessive suffix and the object marker ra left implicit in the poetic line order.
segments.s677-g222: به بنده مده is a negative imperative ('do not give to a servant'), where مده is the negative command form of دادن. تاج وگاه مرا is the direct object 'my crown and rank/office' (تاج = crown; وگاه here denotes dignity/position/time as a paired item with تاج). The sequence says: 'Do not give my crown and office to a servant.'
segments.s677-g223: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). کام دل is a genitive phrase 'the desire/wish of the heart' (کام = wish/desire, دل = heart). یابم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of یابیدن ('I obtain / I attain'); in classical usage the subjunctive here expresses the conditional result ('if I obtain ...'). این تاج و اسپ is the direct object 'this crown and steed' (اسپ = horse/steed).
segments.s677-g224: بیارم is the first-person form of 'bring' (I will bring / I bring). دمان پیش is a prepositional phrase where پیش = 'before / in the presence of' and dمان (here an older locative/relational word) marks the act of bringing into presence. آذرگشسپ is a proper name (Azargushasp); the whole phrase means 'I will bring (them) into the presence of Azargushasp.'
segments.s677-g177: بدرند is a third-person plural verb (they tear) in an older/poetic present/present-future form; it precedes the prepositional phrases that mark the affected parts. تنت is تن + the possessive suffix -ت (“your body”); the repeated بر introduces two closely linked local phrases (بر پوست و رگ = on/at the skin and the veins) that specify what is torn.
segments.s677-g178: سپارند is a third-person plural verb (they entrust/leave/deliver); پس is an adverb “then/afterwards.” استخوانت is استخوان + possessive -ت (“your bones/remains”), and به سگ is a dative phrase “to/for the dog(s).” The clause says the action (leaving/delivering) that follows tearing: they leave your bones to the dogs.
segments.s677-g179: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (بـ + او) and is placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت here functions as the speech verb 'said' (past), while خسرو is the subject/name following the verb (inverted order: 'to him said Khosrow'). که‌ای + بدکنش is a vocative/exclamation addressing the addressee: که‌ای (a poetic form of 'که ای'/'o, you who') + بدکنش 'ill‑doer/evildoer'.
segments.s677-g180: چرا begins the question 'why'. گشته‌ای is the second-person perfect/perfect‑result form 'you have become' (گشته + ی). The adjectives تند و برتر modify منش and function as predicate complements: 'tend (fierce/hasty) and baratar (superior/proud) in disposition.' منش ('character/disposition') completes the clause: 'why have you become fierce and of superior (proud) disposition?'
segments.s677-g181: که introduces the content/reason clause. آهوست is the noun 'gazelle' used here as a metaphoric reference or descriptive label placed before the prepositional phrase; بر مرد is 'toward/at the man' (بر = toward), and گفتار زشت is a noun phrase 'ugly/vile speech' that describes the behavior. Taken together the phrase describes speaking an ugly/insulting speech toward a man, with آهوست functioning as a descriptive element (a simile or epithet) within the clause.
segments.s677-g182: تو را uses the pronoun تو ('you') with the object/definite marker را (marking 'you' as the topic/object of comment). اندر آغاز بود is a idiomatic past statement 'was in the beginning' (بود here as past 'was'), and این سرشت is 'this nature/disposition.' The whole segment states that this bad habit/temperament has been with you from the very beginning.
segments.s677-g183: ز مغز تو is a prepositional phrase 'from your brain' (ز = from; تو is a possessive suffix on مغز). بگسست is a past-tense verb 'broke off/was severed' (third person singular), and روشن خرد ('bright/clear wisdom/intellect') is the thing that was severed. Word order is poetic: the source + verb + the separated quality.
segments.s677-g184: خنک نامور is a two‑word descriptive phrase ('cool, famed' — adjectives describing a person), و کو (here only کو) is the interrogative/relative 'where is' (poetic 코/کو). خرد پرورد is a deverbal noun/agent phrase 'one who raised/nurtured wisdom' (پرورد used as a past participle/agent). Together the line asks rhetorically where the cool, renowned person is who fostered that intellect.
