segments.s677-g273: چنو مرد را ارج نشناختی is an object-centered clause with a poetic word order: چنو مرد ('such a man') is a definite/known noun phrase marked by را (the direct-object/definiteness marker), followed by ارج ('esteem, worth') as the thing not recognized, and the verb نشناختی (past, 2nd-person singular, negative prefix n- attached) at the end. چنو is an archaic/poetic equivalent of چنان/چنین ('such'), and نشناختی literally means 'you did not recognize/know'.
segments.s677-g274: به خواری ز تخت اندر انداختی is a sequence of adverbial/prepositional phrases followed by the past verb انداختی (2nd-person singular 'you threw/cast'). به خواری ('into humiliation') is an accusative/adverbial phrase indicating result or direction, ز تخت ('from the throne') gives the origin, اندر is a poetic preposition meaning 'into/within', and the verb انداختی completes the action 'you cast (him)'. Word order places these adverbials before the verb in classical style.
segments.s677-g275: پس او جهاندار خواهی بدن opens with پس ('then/therefore') and the pronoun او ('he'). جهاندار is a predicate noun 'world-ruler/sovereign.' خواهی here functions as an archaic modal/auxiliary meaning roughly 'will be / may be' (classical sense of desire/optative), and بدن ('in body; in person') is a postposed complement—together 'he will be a sovereign in person.' The clause reflects poetic inversion (verb before some complements).
segments.s677-g276: خردمند و بیدار خواهی بدن repeats the modal construction for two coordinated predicates: خردمند ('wise') and بیدار ('awake/alert') are linked by و, then خواهی بدن again gives the modal sense 'will be / may be in person.' The duplication of خواهی بدن after the coordinated adjectives is a poetic way to apply the same modal/complement to both predicates.
segments.s677-g277: تو ناپاکی و دشمن ایزدی is a verbless (nominal) clause typical of Persian: تو ('you') is the subject, followed by two predicate nouns joined by و. ناپاکی ('impurity; moral uncleanness') predicates 'you are impure,' and دشمن ایزدی ('enemy of the divine') is a second predicate in apposition. The copula 'to be' is omitted (present implied).
segments.s677-g278: نبینی ز نیکی دهش جز بدی has the negative present/potential verb نبینی (2nd-person singular 'you do not/will not see') followed by an ablative phrase ز نیکی دهش ('from the giving of goodness' — نیکی = goodness, دهش = the act of giving) and the expression جز بدی ('except evil'). The construction نبینی ... جز ... means 'you see nothing but ...' or 'you will see nothing except ...'.
segments.s677-g279: گر ایدون که هرمزد بیداد بود begins with the conditional particle گر ('if'). ایدون is an archaic/poetic temporal/conditional marker here ('ever/at any time' / 'if ever'), که introduces the clause, هرمزد is the proper name 'Ahura Mazda', and بیداد بود is a nominal predicate 'was injustice/unjust' with the past copula بود. The clause therefore reads 'if ever Ahura Mazda were unjust' (counterfactual/conditional).
segments.s677-g280: زمان و زمین زو به فریاد بود: زمان و زمین ('time and earth') are the compound subject; زو is a contracted poetic form meaning 'از او' ('because of him' / 'on his account') formed from ز (from/because of) + او (him); به فریاد بود literally 'were in an outcry' — به marks the state ('in/at an outcry') and بود is the past copula. The whole means 'time and earth would have been in outcry because of him.'
segments.s677-g81: به پرسش یکی پیش‌دستی کنم is a small clause whose literal parts are: به پرسش (with / in a question), یکی (one / a single), پیش‌دستی (initiative / preemption) followed by the first‑person verb کنم. Here کنَم is the present/subjunctive form used narratively for 'I do / I will do.' Word order is slightly emphatic: the prepositional phrase 'به پرسش' and the numeral 'یکی' precede the noun 'پیش‌دستی' and then the verb 'کنم'. The whole means 'I will (preemptively) take the initiative by asking one question.'
segments.s677-g82: از آن به که ... سستی کنم is a comparative/subordinating clause: the fixed idiom 'از آن به که' functions as 'rather than / instead of' and introduces the clause 'در جنگ سستی کنم' ('that I be lax in battle'). در جنگ ('in battle') is a simple locative phrase; سستی (laxity, weakness) is the nominal predicate and کنَم matches the earlier verb form (first‑person).
segments.s677-g83: اگر زو بر اندازه یابم سخن is conditional: اگر introduces the condition 'if'. ز + و here function together as the poetic contraction 'زو' (= زِ او = 'of/from him' or 'his'). بر اندازه means 'in measure / measured'; یابم is the first‑person present/subjunctive of یابیدن 'to find' ("I find / judge"); سخن (speech/words) is the thing judged and is placed after the verb for poetic emphasis. Literally: 'If I find his words to be measured.'
segments.s677-g84: نوآیین بدیهاش گردد کهن: نوآیین (new, novel) modifies بدیهاش ('his evils' = بدی‌ها + ش). گردد is the third‑person present/subjunctive/optative of گردیدن 'to become', and کهن is the adjective 'old/ancient'. The clause means 'his new (recently‑fashioned) evils will become old.'
segments.s677-g85: ز گیتی یکی گوشه او را دهم: ز گیتی ('from the world') gives the source; یکی گوشه ('one corner') is the thing given; او را is the marked object ('to him' with the accusative/definite marker را placed after the pronoun), and دهم is the first‑person present/subjunctive of دادن 'I give'. Poetic word order places the source and the quantified noun before the verb: 'From the world I give him one corner.'
segments.s677-g86: سپاسی ز دادن بدو برنهم: سپاسی ('a thanks / gratitude') is the noun; ز دادن بدو ('for / from the giving to him') uses the gerund/infinitive دادن with the preposition ز to mark reason/source and بدو ('to him') as the indirect object. برنهم is the first‑person present form (بر + نهم = 'I put / I attach / I accept')—here idiomatically rendered 'I will accept (no) thanks' in context. The whole clause says that no thanks will be taken for that gift (i.e. 'I will take no thanks for giving it to him').
segments.s677-g87: همه آشتی گردد این جنگ ما places the noun phrase 'همه آشتی' ('all reconciliation' or 'complete reconciliation') before the verb گردد ('become' / optative 'may become') for emphasis; the following 'این جنگ ما' ('this war of ours') is the theme/subject. The structure is a wish or optative: 'May this our war become altogether reconciliation.'
segments.s677-g88: برین رزمگه جستن آهنگ ما: برین = بر + این ('on/at this'); رزمگه is 'battlefield'; جستن is the infinitive 'to seek' (used as a verbal noun); آهنگ ما ('our intention/aim') is the nominal phrase that governs the infinitive. Literally: 'Our aim (آهنگ ما) is the seeking of this battlefield,' but context and prosody render it as 'it is not our aim to seek this battlefield' (i.e. our intention is not to pursue battle here). Note that آهنگ here means 'intention/aim.'
segments.s677-g425: This is a short reporting clause with VSO order: بدو (to him) is fronted indirect object, گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here placed before the subject), and بهرام is the subject 'Bahram'. کای بدکنش is a vocative phrase ('O, ill‑doer') addressing the addressee; کای is the archaic vocative marker.
segments.s677-g426: نزیبد is a negative form meaning 'is not fitting' or 'does not befit' (ن‑ negates زیبد). همی is the old continuous/iterative particle marking a habitual or emphatic present. بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'upon you', and جز سرزنش is a restrictive phrase 'except reproach' (جز = 'except', سرزنش = 'reproach').
segments.s677-g427: تو is the 2nd‑person subject 'you'. پیمان یزدان is a genitive phrase 'the covenant of God'. نداری here functions as the 2nd‑person verb 'you do not have/keep' (negative). نگاه is used as a noun 'regard/heed' (literally 'look') so the whole phrase means 'you do not heed/keep God's covenant.'
segments.s677-g428: همی again marks continuous/habitual action. ناسزا خوانی is a light‑verb construction: ناسزا (insult) + خوانی (you call/utter) = 'you utter insults' or 'you insult'. این پیشگاه is the object 'this presence/this court' (the place/person before whom the insult is offered).
segments.s677-g429: نهی here is an exclamatory/negative particle ('nay' / 'not so') introducing the nominal phrase. داغ is 'a mark/brand' (metonymically shame/spot), followed by the locative phrase بر چشم شاه جهان 'upon the eye of the king of the world' where شاه جهان is a fixed genitive/title 'king of the world.'
segments.s677-g430: سخن is the subject 'speech/words'. زین is an archaic prepositional/relational element here (literally 'of/from this' in classical usage) — not the literal 'saddle' sense — linking to نشان 'this token/mark'. کی بود در نهان is a rhetorical question 'when was (it) in hiding?' with بود as the past copula/auxiliary.
segments.s677-g431: همه دوستان is the plural subject 'all the friends.' The double use of بر introduces attitude/direction: بر تو ... بر دشمنند = 'toward you ... they are hostile.' دشمنند is the predicate 'they are enemies' (‑ند is the 3rd‑person plural ending + copula).
segments.s677-g432: This is a parallel antithesis: به گفتار با تو 'in speech/words with you' (i.e. they show you verbal support), به دل با منند 'in heart with me' (i.e. in their hearts they side with me). منند (manand) is the comparative/copular form 'are like/are with' indicating their actual inner allegiance.
segments.s677-g113: تو را ... مهمان کنم is literally 'I make you guest': تو (you) is the direct object marked by را, and مهمان کنم is a first‑person verb phrase (کنم here = 'I will make/host'). The prepositional phrase با سپاه تو ('with your army') is inserted between the object marker and the verb, so the whole line means 'I will host you together with your army.' The verb کنم is in the literary/poetic present-subjunctive sense often used for a future or volitional sense ('I will/may make').
segments.s677-g114: ز دیدار تو is an adverbial phrase 'from/by the seeing of you' (ز = 'from, by' in poetic register; دیدار = 'meeting/seeing'), and رامش جان کنم is a transitive verbal phrase 'I will make (render) (my) soul's repose/solace.' جان ('soul') is the logical possessor of رامش ('solace, tranquility'), and کنَم is again the 1st-person volitional/subjunctive 'I will/may make.' The clause thus means 'by seeing you I will make my soul calm/solaced.'
segments.s677-g115: سپهدار ایرانت خوانم = 'I shall call/name (you) the سپهدار (spahbed/commander) of your Iran.' خوانم is the 1st‑person present/optative of خواندن used in the sense 'to name/call' here (a common poetic use). به داد is an adverbial phrase 'with justice/rightly' (به + داد = 'in justice' / 'justly'), qualifying the naming.
segments.s677-g116: کنم آفریننده را بر تو یاد is a causative construction: کنم ('I will cause') takes آفریننده را ('the Creator' marked by را) as the object that is caused to do/feel something; بر تو یاد literally 'have remembrance toward you' = 'remember you.' So the whole means 'I will cause the Creator to remember you' or 'I will put you into the Creator's remembrance.' Note the literary ordering (verb before its complement) and the use of را to mark the affected entity in the causative.
segments.s677-g117: سخنهاش بشنید بهرام گرد is best read as 'Bahram Gerd heard his words.' سخنهاش = 'his words/speeches' (suffixed possessive), بشنید is the past of شنیدن 'he heard', and بهرام گرد here functions as the subject/name (Bahram Gerd). In classical verse the subject often comes after the verb for metrical/poetic ordering.
segments.s677-g118: عنان باره تیزتگ را سپرد: سپرد is the simple past 'he entrusted/handed over.' The marked noun phrase تیزتگ را (a proper name, the horse's name) is the direct object, and عنان (the reins) together with باره ('load/burden' or 'laden') describe what is being handed; taken naturally, this yields 'he entrusted/handed over the reins of Tiztag.' Note the poetic compaction: an explicit possessive particle may be absent and ownership is read from context.
segments.s677-g119: هم از پشت آن باره بردش نماز: هم 'also' opens the clause; از پشت آن باره = 'from behind that load/baggage'; نماز بردش is an idiomatic phrasing meaning 'prayer (نماز) took/had him' — i.e., he began to pray or was seized by prayer. بردش here is best understood as 'it took him' (impersonal/experiential use) rather than the literal meanings in the modern lemma list.
segments.s677-g120: همی بود پیشش زمانی دراز: همی بود is the archaic/poetic imperfect/past continuous 'he was (kept)'; پیشش 'in front of him / before him / in his presence'; زمانی دراز = 'for a long time.' Together: 'he remained (and) prayed before him for a long while' (so the second line continues the description of the prolonged prayer).
segments.s677-g65: چو introduces a temporal clause here: 'when'. بفریفت is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person form with the prefixed بِ-; in context it functions as the main past action 'swung/brandished' (the provided lemma senses don't match this exact sense, see senses). چوبینه را is the direct object marked by را. نره دیو is a descriptive noun phrase ('nareh' + noun), with نره qualifying دیو ('the virile/mighty demon') using a somewhat inverted, poetic order.
segments.s677-g66: کجا is the interrogative 'where/how'. بیند is a 3rd-person singular verb 'sees/will see' placed before its subject (poetic V–S order). او is the pronoun 'he'. راه گیهان is a genitive phrase 'the path/way of the world' (راه + modifier گیهان). خدیو is an address/title ('ruler, khedive') appended here in apostrophe, addressing a sovereign.
segments.s677-g67: هرآن ... که is a correlative temporal/conditional introducer meaning roughly 'whenever' or 'any heart that'. از + آز indicates cause/agent ('from/through greed'). شد is the copular/becoming verb 'became', and دردمند is the predicate adjective 'sorrowful/in pain' following شد (verb + adjective construction common in classical Persian).
segments.s677-g68: نیایدش is negative impersonal/3rd-person form 'does not befit/does not come to him' with the enclitic pronoun ش ('to him'); here it means 'it will not befit him' or 'he will not have'. کار بزرگان is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the deeds/works of the great', and پسند functions as the nominal predicate 'pleasing/approved'. The whole phrase: 'he will not have deeds pleasing to the great.'
segments.s677-g69: جز از جنگ establishes an exception/limitation 'except in/for battle'. چوبینه را places 'the cudgel/wooden club' as a topical object with را. رای نیست is a negated existential/predicative clause 'there is no counsel/plan' (i.e., 'no other counsel exists'). The overall sense: 'there is no counsel for the cudgel except in battle' (i.e. the cudgel's use/counsel is only in war).
segments.s677-g70: به دل‌ش اندرون is a locative phrase 'in his inner heart' (دل with the enclitic ش = 'his'). داد را: داد is here a noun 'justice' (object marked by را). جای نیست is a negative existential 'there is no place'. Together: 'there is no place for justice in his inner heart.'
segments.s677-g71: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بر جنگ رفتن is an infinitival/gerundial phrase 'to go to war' or 'the going to war'. بسی شد سخن is an impersonal/resultative phrase: بسی 'much' + شد 'became/occurred' + سخن 'speech/talk' = 'much talk occurred' or 'there was much discussion.'
segments.s677-g72: نگه کرد باید is an idiomatic obligation: نگه کرد (literally 'kept watch/considered') with باید 'one must' — meaning 'one must be careful/attentive.' ز سر تا به بن is a fixed-range phrase 'from the head down to the root/base' i.e. 'from top to bottom' or 'thoroughly.' Word order (verb before باید) is typical of classical poetic inversion.
segments.s677-g473: نبُد (here spelled نبد) is the archaic/poetic negative copula 'was not' and begins the clause; خواست یزدان literally 'the will/desire of God' follows, so the phrase as a whole means 'it was not the will of God'. که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content clause that follows; ایران‌زمین is the topic of that subordinate clause ('the land/realm of Iran').
segments.s677-g474: به ویرانی is a prepositional phrase 'into/unto destruction' (به + noun) placed before the verb for emphasis. آرند is a classical-form present/subjunctive/optative 'bring' (3rd person plural here; 'they bring/they be made to bring'), and it governs the agent phrase ترکان چین, where ترکان is the agent 'the Turks' and چین identifies them as 'of China' (postpositive modifier). Word order (prepositional phrase + verb + agent) is typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s677-g475: تو بودی is simple past 'you were'. بدین is the fused prepositional expression 'in/with this' (Poetic بدین = به + این) and attaches to جنگشان 'their battle' (جنگ + possessive suffix). یارمند is a predicate adjective/noun 'an ally, helpful' describing the subject 'you'. The adjective follows the noun as is normal in Persian.
segments.s677-g476: کلاهت 'your hat/helmet' is the possessed subject (کلاه + ـت). برآمد is the past intransitive 'rose / went up'. به introduces the destination/comparison phrase; ابر بلند 'a high/lofty cloud' is the goal/figure of speech — literally 'your hat rose to a lofty cloud', a poetic way to say it rose very high.
segments.s677-g477: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دارنده چرخ گردان is a compound epithet: دارنده 'holder/possessor' + چرخ 'the sky / cosmic wheel' + گردان 'turning/revolving', an epic title (literally 'holder of the turning wheel' = Fate/God/heaven). بخواست is the simple past 'willed/desired' and introduces the following subordinate clause.
segments.s677-g478: که introduces the content/result clause 'that...'. آن پادشا را uses آن 'that/the' plus پادشا 'king' with the object/topic marker را emphasizing the affected person. شود is the present/subjunctive of 'to become' used in the sense 'be made' or 'come about'; کار راست means 'the matter/affairs (کار) [be] right/straight' — together 'that the king's affairs be set right'. Note the poetic word order (object/topic marker before the verb).
segments.s677-g479: تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you'. زان مایه is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'from that source / because of that' (زان = از آن / for that reason; مایه = source/cause/resource). مر خویشتن را is an emphatic/reflexive construction: مر is an archaic/clitic form meaning 'me/to me' here embedded in the expression 'مر خویشتن' (poetic reflexive), and را is the object marker attached to the reflexive phrase. نهی is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'you withhold / you refrain (from showing/using)'; the verb is used here in the sense 'keep yourself back/withdraw yourself'.
segments.s677-g480: که here is causal/concessive 'for/that', introducing the reason. هرگز is the negator 'never'. ندیدی is the past negative 2nd-person 'you did not see' (negated with هرگز). بهی and مهی are descriptive nouns/adjectives used nominally: بهی 'excellent/good' and مهی 'moonlike/beautiful' — together 'excellent and moonlike beauty', the things the addressee 'never saw'.
segments.s677-g129: به زودی is an adverbial phrase 'soon' (به + زودی); به here functions adverbially. یکی دار سازم = 'I will make/build one gallows': یکی (= one/a certain) precedes the noun دار (here 'gallows'), and سازم is first-person singular ('I will make') — the prefix بـ often omitted in poetic/classical Persian. بلند is an adjective 'tall/high' placed after the noun for emphasis (poetic word order).
segments.s677-g130: دو دستت ببندم = 'I will bind your two hands': دو (numeral) precedes دست (hand) and the second-person suffix -ت is attached to دست to mark the object ('your hand(s)'). ببندم is first-person verb 'I will bind' (classical present/future form). به خم کمند is a prepositional phrase 'to/in the bend of the noose' (به = 'to/in', خم = 'bend/curve', کمند = 'lasso/noose') giving the place/way of binding.
segments.s677-g131: بیاویزمت is an archaic/poetic compound meaning 'I will hang you' (verb with speaker and object clitics included). The form shows classical morphology (the verb with enclitics marking person/object). زان = 'from that' (pointing to the gallows just mentioned). سزاوار دار = 'a gallows befitting/deserved' (سزاوار = 'deserving/fitting', دار = 'gallows'), so the whole phrase = 'I will hang you from that fitting gallows.'
segments.s677-g132: ببینی is second-person present/future 'you will see' (in reported speech here it has future sense). ز من = 'from me' (indicating source/agent of what follows). تلخی روزگار is a noun phrase 'the bitterness of fate/life' (تلخی = bitterness, روزگار = fate/the times), i.e. 'you will experience from me the bitterness of fate.'
segments.s677-g133: چو at the start of the clause = 'when'. خسرو is the subject (name). ز بهرام = 'from/of Bahram' (ز used poetically to mark source or agent of the utterance). پاسخ شنید is the verb phrase 'heard an answer' — شنید is past tense 'he heard'.
segments.s677-g134: به رخساره شد = 'became upon his face' (به here marks the locus/target of the change). شد is past 'became'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', گل شنبلید is the image of comparison ('the shenbalid flower' — a specific plant); together: 'his face became like a shenbalid flower.'
segments.s677-g135: چنین داد پاسخ = 'he gave such an answer' (چنین = 'thus/so', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). که‌ای introduces the quoted address: که (here introducing reported speech 'that'/'that which') together with the vocative ای ('O'), so که‌ای ناسپاس is the opening of the direct quote: 'O ungrateful one'.
segments.s677-g136: نگوید here is a third-person negative/potential sense 'would not say' (in reported speech after که). چنین is 'such' placed after the verb for emphasis, and مرد یزدان‌شناس is the noun phrase 'a god‑knowing / pious man' (یزدان‑شناس = one who knows/reveres God). The whole clause means 'a pious man would not say such (a thing).' Note: the lexical senses list provided for نگوید did not include the verb reading, so its sense index is marked -1.
segments.s677-g289: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'). تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. داغ here means an afflicting mark or sorrow (a figurative 'brand' or 'blot') and is the object; بر + چشم ('on the eye[s]') is the usual Persian spatial phrase 'upon the eyes of'. شاهان is the plural 'kings'. نهی here is a 2nd-person verb form meaning 'you place / you put' (an archaic/poetic form of نهادن); syntactically it completes the clause introduced by که.
segments.s677-g290: کسی functions as 'whoever / anyone'; کو here is a classical relative/particle linking 'کسی' to the verb (literary use, 'who/that'), and نهد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive of نهادن 'places/sets'. نیز 'also' links this action to the previous one. فرمان is 'command, order' and دهی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive/imperative 'you give' — together: 'if anyone places (one) as well, you give the command.'
segments.s677-g291: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / thereupon'. بیابی is a 2nd/3rd-person classical form meaning 'you will find' / 'you find'. که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. شاهی here means 'kingship' and مراست is a fused form (مرا + است) meaning 'is mine' (poetic contraction indicating possession).
segments.s677-g292: ز خورشید تا is a poetic range 'from the Sun to ...'; تا here means 'as far as / up to'. برج ماهی is a horoscope/astrological phrase: برج is the zodiac/sign and ماهی 'the Fish' names that sign (Pisces). مراست again is the contracted possessive clause 'is mine' (مرا + است).
segments.s677-g293: بدو = 'to him' ('said to him'). گفت here is the past tense 'said' (classical narrative past), and خسرو is the speaker's name/title. که introduces the quoted wish/clause. هرگز مباد is an idiomatic jussive/optative: مباد ('may it not be') with هرگز 'never' gives 'may it never be'.
segments.s677-g294: که باشد is a jussive/subjunctive 'that it be / may it be'; به درد = 'in/into pain' (به + درد), پدر = 'father'. بنده is the speaker's humble self-reference 'your servant / your slave' (classical courtly usage) and شاد = 'happy, glad.' The clause expresses a wish that the father not be afflicted while the speaker is joyful.
segments.s677-g295: نوشته 'the writing / what was written' (here 'the account/text'). چنین بود 'was thus' — وبود is a stylistic repetition 'and it was'. آنچ (آن‌چه) = 'that which' or 'what'; بود is the past 'was.' This formulaic line announces that the preceding account is what was written.
segments.s677-g296: سخن بر سخن 'word upon word / speech upon speech' is a poetic binomial. چند here is interrogative/indefinite 'how many / how much', باید = 'must / it is necessary', and فزود is the infinitive/present 'to add / to increase'. The line questions how many more words one should add.
segments.s677-g193: 'یکی' is an indefinite introducer meaning 'a certain' placed before the noun; 'کوه' is the noun 'mountain'. 'داری' here functions as a present/predicative 'having' (poetic/archaic use of داشتن) and attaches to the noun phrase to mean 'a mountain that has...'. The prepositional chunk 'به زیر اندورن' ('به' + 'زیر' + 'اندورن') specifies location 'beneath, under' and 'اندورن' is a noun 'inner chamber/within'.
segments.s677-g194: 'که‌گر' is an archaic/poetic inversion of 'گر که' meaning 'if' (conditional). 'بنگری' is the subjunctive/potential form 'you/one look' (if one were to look). The clause ends with a comparative predicate 'برتر از بیستون' — 'brtar (higher/superior) + از (than) + بیستون (Bisotun)'.
segments.s677-g195: 'گر' is the conditional particle 'if'. 'از تو' means 'from you / of your line' (source phrase). 'یکی شهریار' = 'one/ a certain king' (subject). 'آمدی' is a poetic/archaic form of 'آمد' used in conditional clauses to express a hypothetical 'should come / were to come'.
segments.s677-g196: The noun 'مغیلان' (a people/place name) is the topic of the clause. 'بی‌بر' is an adjective 'without offspring/ill‑fated' describing that topic. The verbal idiom 'به بار آمدی' (here with poetic agreement) conveys 'would come into being / be produced / come forth' — together giving the sense 'Maghilan would come forth childless/ill-fated'. The verb form is again the conditional/hypothetical 'آمدی'.
segments.s677-g197: 'تو را' is topicalizing/emphatic (the 'را' marks 'you' as the topic or beneficiary): literally 'to you'. The head noun 'دل' ('heart') is the real subject, described by the adjective 'پراندیشه' ('full of thought/ anxious'). 'مهتریست' is a contracted/poetic copula combining 'مهتر' (greater/important) + 'است' (is), meaning 'is greater/weighty'.
segments.s677-g198: 'ببینیم' is the hortatory/first-person-plural present subjunctive 'let us see' or 'we shall see'. 'تا' here introduces the content of what we will see (roughly 'to/what/so that we may know'), followed by 'رای یزدان' = 'the counsel/plan of God'. The final copula 'ست' ('is') closes the clause 'what the will of God is.'
segments.s677-g199: 'ندانم' is the negative present 'I do not know' (classical/poetic word order). 'که' introduces the subordinate content ('whether/that'). 'آمختت' is an archaic perfect/past form of آموختن 'to teach/ to make learn' with the enclitic object '-ت' (you): 'taught you' or 'has taught you'. 'این بد تنی' is the object/theme 'this bad bodily-nature / this ill constitution' (بد + تنی = 'bad body / ill temperament').
segments.s677-g200: 'تو را' again topicalizes/emphasizes 'you' as the one affected. 'با' is the preposition 'with', 'چنین' is the demonstrative 'such', and 'کیش' means 'creed/sect/way'. 'آهرمنی' is an adjective formed from Ahriman (the evil spirit) meaning 'demonic' or 'devilish'. Together the phrase says 'with such a demonic creed' — i.e. 'taught you such demonic ways'.
segments.s677-g377: چنین is an adverb ('thus/in this way') that opens the utterance; پاسخ آورد is a verbal phrase literally 'brought an answer' (آورد used here as 'replied' — past narrative) with the verb after its object (typical poetic order). خسرو is the subject (proper name) following the verb, and بدوی is an adverbial/epithet (archaic) meaning 'at once' or 'immediately' modifying آورد.
segments.s677-g378: که‌ای here introduces direct speech and functions like a vocative/exclamatory particle ('O'/'hey'); بیهده مرد پیکار جوی is a noun phrase in vocative: بیهده 'idle/foolish', مرد 'man', and the compound پیکار جوی is noun (پیکار) + جوی ('seeker/searcher') producing 'seeker of battle'. The sequence is compact and typical of poetic address.
segments.s677-g379: اگر begins a conditional clause ('if'); پادشاهی is the noun 'kingship/kingdom'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; تخم here means 'seed/stock' in the sense of lineage or origin; کیان names the Kayan royal house ('the Kayanids' / 'the royal house of Kayan'). Together they form the conditional's protasis 'if kingship from the Kayan seed...'.
segments.s677-g380: بخواهد (3rd sing. of خواستن) + شدن (infinitive) form a verb cluster meaning 'wants / would wish to become'; تو is the direct address 'you'; کیی is an interrogative/colloquial/poetic 'who are you' (here a rhetorical 'what sort of person are you'); در جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' placed at the end for emphasis. The whole segment asks, in effect, 'who are you in the world (to deserve that)?'.
segments.s677-g381: همه رازیان is the noun phrase 'all the confidants/secret‑keepers' (همه quantifies رازیان). از + بنه خود is a prepositional phrase 'from their own ...' (بنه is an archaic noun meaning a band/stock/retinue here), and کیند is an archaic/plural verbal form (poetic variant of کردند/کنند) meaning 'they did/caused/removed' used narratively. The order (object → prepositional phrase → verb) reflects poetic word order.
segments.s677-g382: دو (literally 'two') with رویند produces the idea 'two‑faced' (دو‑رو) — رویند is used here as a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'they showed/assumed (faces)' or 'they became' (poetic use). وز is an archaic connective (here functioning like 'and' / 'from' in older poetry). مردمی 'of the people' or 'from among the people' is the phrase that is the object of برچیند, and برچیند (poetic 3rd‑person plural of برچیدن) means 'they plucked/removed' (i.e., took away).
segments.s677-g383: نخست is an adverb 'first/at first'; از ری آمد places origin before the verb in poetic order ('came from Ray'); سپاه اندکی is the noun phrase 'a small army' (سپاه 'army' modified by اندکی 'a small/few'). آمد is the simple past 'came'.
segments.s677-g384: که introduces a relative clause ('which/that'); شد is the past verb 'became'; با سپاه سکندر یکی is a prepositional phrase + predicate 'one with Alexander's army' — با 'with', سپاه سکندر 'Alexander's host', and یکی 'one/united' as the predicate complement of شد ('became one').
segments.s677-g161: 'نخستین' is an adverb/ordinal 'first' that sets sequence. 'ز' here is the archaic/poetic preposition marking the addressee ('to/from the guest'), so 'ز مهمان' = 'to the guest'. 'گشادی سخن' is a nominal phrase 'the opening of speech' (a noun phrase meaning 'you began to speak'); Persian often uses such a noun-phrase to express 'you opened/began speech'.
segments.s677-g162: 'سرشتت' is a past transitive verb stem 'سرشت' with the 2nd-person enclitic '-ت' = 'you made/you fashioned' (the subject is expressed by the enclitic). 'بدو' is the dative 'to him/unto him'. 'داستانت کهن' is the direct object 'your ancient tale' (داستان + possessive -ت; کهن = 'ancient'). The verb precedes its indirect object here, which is a common poetic word order.
segments.s677-g163: 'تو را' is a topicalized/object-marked 'you' (را marks the definite/direct object or topic). 'با سخنهای شاهان' is a prepositional phrase 'with the speeches/sayings of kings'. 'چه کار' is the predicate 'what business/what concern' (the copula 'است' is omitted, as is common in Persian questions). The whole reads 'What concern is yours with the sayings of kings?'.
segments.s677-g164: The paired negatives 'نه ... نه ...' form a 'neither... nor...' construction. 'فرزانه مردی' is a noun phrase 'a wise man' (فرزانه modifying مردی), and 'جنگی سوار' is another noun phrase 'a warlike horseman' (جنگی = 'warlike/fighter', سوار = 'rider/horseman'). There's no copula; both predicates describe the addressee by negation.
segments.s677-g165: 'الان' here functions in a temporal contrast with 'کنون' (older/poetic sense 'then' or 'at that time') and begins the clause 'you were king'. 'شاه بودی' = 'you were king' (بودی is the past form 'you were'). 'کنون کهتری' then contrasts with 'now' ('کنون') + predicate 'کهتری' = 'you are inferior/lesser' (copula omitted). The line sets a past status followed by a present decline.
segments.s677-g166: 'هم از ... کمتری' is a comparative construction: 'هم' (= 'even/also') + 'از' introduces the standard of comparison 'than'. 'بنده بندگان' is a doubled/intensified expression meaning '(the) servants' or 'a servant among servants' (poetic plural), and 'کمتری' is the comparative/predicative 'you are fewer/less'. The predicate has an implied subject 'تو' (you).
segments.s677-g167: گنه‌کارتر is a comparative adjective (گنه‌کار + -تر) meaning “more sinful / most sinful” and here modifies کس (“person/one”), producing “the more sinful person.” The locative phrase توی در جهان is a poetic doubling of location (توی + در) meaning “in the world,” with the modifier-before-noun order (adjective → noun) and the indefinite کس ‘person’ as the target of comparison.
segments.s677-g168: نه ... نه ... is correlative negation “neither ... nor ...” linking two predicates. شاهی and زیباسری are qualities applied to the addressee (شاهی = kingly/regal; زیباسری = ‘fair‑headed’/fair‑faced, a compound of زیبا + سر). از مهان (از = “of/among”) locates these qualities with respect to the guests — literally “neither kingly nor fair‑faced of/among the guests.”
segments.s677-g433: بدین کار is an adverbial/prepositional phrase meaning 'in/for this affair' (ب- as the old/prepositional prefix + دین = 'this' in an archaic spelling). خاقان is the subject 'the Khaghan'; مرا is the object-clitic 'me' (marked here before the predicate); یاورست is a predicative form 'is a helper/ally' (یاور + -ست 'is'). The phrase literally reads 'For this affair the Khaghan is my ally.'
segments.s677-g434: همان is the demonstrative 'the same/that very'; کاندر is an archaic fused preposition/relative meaning 'which/that in' (کـ + اندر = 'which in/wherein'); ایران و چین is the coordinated place phrase 'Iran and China'; لشکرست (لشکر + -ست) is a predicate 'is a host/there is a host' — i.e. 'he is one who has hosts in Iran and China.'
segments.s677-g435: بزرگی من is a possessive NP 'my greatness'. از پارس is the source phrase 'from Pars'. آرم is an archaic first‑person verbal form here meaning something like 'I derive / I bring' (classical usage differs from modern forms). بری here is an adjective/adverb ('free, apart') linked to the verb phrase; together the segment says 'My greatness I take from Pars.'
segments.s677-g436: نمانم is the first‑person negative form 'I shall not remain / I will not be left'. کزین پس is the temporal phrase 'from this on / henceforth'. بود is the copula 'be' (here used in a subordinate construction), نام is 'name' (or fame), and کی here is a particle/name (in the Shahnameh context it often reads as the dynastic element 'Kay' rather than the interrogative 'who'). The clause roughly means 'I will not remain so that henceforth the name "Kay" (would be)...' (a rhetorical question/effect).
segments.s677-g437: برافرازم is a first‑person verbal form 'I raise up / I exalt' (برافراختن stem). اندر جهان is the locative 'in the world'. داد را — داد (justice) with the object marker را — is the object of the verb: 'I exalt justice in the world.'
segments.s677-g438: کنم is the first‑person singular of کردن used to form 'I will/do' (here future/intentional). تازه is 'fresh/new', آیین میلاد is 'the rite/ceremony of birth' (آیین = ritual/custom; میلاد = birth/anniversary), and را is the object marker. Together: 'I will renew the ritual of birth.'
segments.s677-g439: من is the subject 'I'. از تخمه نامور is a genitive/source phrase 'of/from the famed seed/lineage' (تخمه = offspring/lineage, نامور = renowned). آرشم is a name/reference (here functioning as the ancestral name 'Arash' or an eponym); the whole phrase says 'I am of the renowned stock of Arash.'
segments.s677-g440: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. جنگ آورم is a compound verbal idea 'I bring/bring about battle' — idiomatically 'when I go to war' or 'when I wage battle' (جنگ + آورم). آتش سرکشم is a verbal phrase meaning 'I draw/unleash fire' (آتش = fire; سرکشم derives from a classical verb meaning to rush forth or be unruly, here 'to let loose / to blaze'). Together: 'When I take up battle, I unleash a blazing fire.'
segments.s677-g201: هران (هر آن) is an indefinite relative meaning 'whoever' or 'each one who' and introduces the clause; کاین is the fused relative 'که این' linking to سخن 'this speech/these words'. با + تو (با تو) here means 'to you' (i.e., 'speak to you'), and گوید is the classical 3rd-person present of گفتن 'he says'; همی is the archaic/perfective-aspect particle placed after the verb to indicate continuous or habitual action ('keeps saying').
segments.s677-g202: به here functions instrumentally/causally 'by/with', گفتار = 'word(s), utterance', مرگ = 'death'; تو is the object 'your(…)'. جوید (from جستن/جستن‌-جو = جوید) is present tense 'seeks' or 'strives for', and the final همی again marks ongoing action — together: 'by speech of death he seeks (your) death'.
segments.s677-g203: بگفت is a past narrative 'he said/said it'; و simply links clauses 'and'. فرود آمد is a compound verb meaning 'descended' or 'came down' (فرود + آمد). از marks origin 'from', and عاج 'ivory' is the noun; خنگ modifies عاج — in older/poetic usage this adjective is placed before the noun and here describes that ivory/seat from which he descended.
segments.s677-g204: ز (poetic variant of از) + سر = 'from the head'. بر گرفت is an inverted verbal phrase where گرفت (past 'took/seized') governs the object; آن بهاگیر تاج is the direct object with آن 'that' and بهاگیر functioning as a descriptive epithet 'tribute‑/toll‑taking' modifying تاج 'crown'. The clause means he removed that tribute‑taking crown from the head.
segments.s677-g205: بنالید is a past-tense narrative verb 'he moaned/wailed'. و links the next action. سر سوی خورشید کرد is a classical word-order where سر 'head' is the thing moved, سوی 'toward' indicates direction, خورشید 'the sun' is the goal, and کرد (past of کردن) functions as 'he made/turned' — together 'he turned his head toward the sun.'
segments.s677-g206: ز یزدان here means 'by/from God' (an agentive/benefactive phrase). دلش = دل + enclitic ش 'his heart' is the affected thing; پر ز امید is a predicative/adjectival phrase 'full of hope' (پر = 'full (of)'). کرد is the past verb 'he made/fill(ed)' — literally 'by God he made his heart full of hope'.
segments.s677-g207: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus/so' + گفت 'said'). کای is the archaic/vocative particle 'O' used to address a high person (here followed directly by titles). روشن دادگر is a compound epithet: روشن 'bright/illuminated' and دادگر 'just (ruler)' — together a formal, laudatory address 'O bright, just one'.
segments.s677-g208: درخت امید is a metaphorical noun phrase 'the tree of hope' (درخت 'tree' + امید 'hope'). از تو 'from you' indicates origin/source, آید is 3rd-person present/future 'will come' (classical present often has future sense), and به بر is an idiomatic locative/benefactive 'to your side/into your bosom' — literally 'comes from you to (your) embrace' meaning hope will spring up for/near you.
segments.s677-g57: به دیده is an instrumental phrase meaning "with the eye/with your eyes" (به here = by/with). ندیدی is 2nd-person singular past (negative) 'you did not see'. مر is an archaic/clitic form meaning 'to me' (مرا), placed before the object (a poetic/older word order). او را is the marked definite object 'him'; به دست is a prepositional phrase 'into/into the hand' meaning 'in my grasp' or 'in hand'. The whole segment literally: 'with (your) eye you did not see to me him in (the) hand.'
segments.s677-g58: کجا در جهان is a locative interrogative phrase 'where in the world'. دشمن is 'enemy'. ایزدست is an archaic fused form (ایزد است); here it functions predicatively to assert existence/character — 'is (an) enemy of God' — forming the question 'Where in the world is there an enemy of God?'.
segments.s677-g59: نبینم is 1st‑person singular negative present 'I do not see' (classical/poetic word order). همی is an adverb here intensifying the negation — 'at all/ever'. در سرش literally 'in his head' but in poetry means 'in his mind/heart' (سرش = his inner thought). کهتری is a noun 'inferiority/lowliness' (i.e. humility); so the clause means 'I do not at all see humility in his mind.'
segments.s677-g60: نیابد is a 3rd‑person present/future-type negative form of یافتن 'to find/obtain' (here 'will not obtain' / 'will not find'). کس 'anyone' is the subject; او را is the definite object 'him' and به فرمانبری is an adverbial phrase 'by/through obedience'. The clause thus says 'no one will obtain him through obedience' (i.e. no one will make him submit).
segments.s677-g61: ازآن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / thereafter'. به بندوی و گستهم is an indirect‑object phrase introduced by به 'to' naming the addressees (proper names). گفت is the simple past 'he said', introducing the reported clause (marked by که). Note: بندوی and گستهم are names here (not literal dictionary senses).
segments.s677-g62: که introduces the subordinate (reported) clause 'that...'. بگشایم is a 1st‑person present/subjunctive/jussive form meaning 'that I may / I should open or reveal' (ب‑ prefix + گشایم). این داستان 'this tale'. از نهفت literally 'from concealment' (نهفت = hidden/hiddenness), i.e. 'reveal this tale from hiding.'
segments.s677-g63: که‌گر is a conditional conjunction 'if'. خر 'the donkey' is the subject. نیاید is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive negative 'does not come / should not come' (classical form). به نزدیک بار is a locative phrase 'to/near the load' (به + نزدیک + بار), so the clause reads 'if the donkey does not come near the load.'
segments.s677-g64: This is an imperative instruction. تو ('you') is the vocative/agent; بار گران را 'the heavy load' is the definite object (بار = load, گران = heavy). به نزد خر is a dative/locative phrase 'to/near the donkey' (نزد = in the presence of / close to). آر is an archaic imperative 'bring' (poetic form), so the clause means 'You, bring the heavy load to the donkey.'
segments.s677-g537: شنیدی is the past (perfective) 2nd‑person form meaning “have you heard / did you hear”; که is the complementizer “that” introducing the reported clause. ضحاک is the proper name (subject) and شد is the past of شدن used as the copular/linking verb “became.” ناسپاس is a predicate adjective “ungrateful/thankless” that completes the clause (“Zahhak became ungrateful”).
segments.s677-g538: The prepositional phrase begins with ز (here = “because of / from”), marking cause. دیو و ز جادو lists the causes (“by demons and by magic”); جهان is the subject of the verbless predicate and پرهراس is an adjectival predicate meaning “full of dread / terrified.” In classical/poetic Persian the copula can be omitted, so this reads “the world (was) terrified because of demons and magic.”
segments.s677-g539: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” زو is the contracted combination of ز + او (= “because of him / from him”); here the word‑list separates it but it functions as a single causal phrase. شد is the past “became,” دل مهتران “the hearts of the chiefs/important men” is the subject phrase, پر ز درد = “full of pain/sorrow” (پر = “full (of)”, ز = “of/because of”, درد = “pain/sorrow”). The whole clause: “When, because of him, the chiefs' hearts became full of sorrow…”
segments.s677-g540: فریدون فرخنده is the name with an epithet (فرخنده “auspicious/fortunate”) placed after the name as a descriptive title. با او is a prepositional phrase “with/to him,” and چه کرد is the interrogative past construction “what did (he) do?” —کرد is past of کردن. The verb asks how Fereydun acted toward that person/situation.
segments.s677-g541: سپاهت is سپاه + enclitic ـت “your army/host.” همه modifies بندگان (“all servants/subjects”). منند is an archaic/formal predicate (related to مانَد/مانند) used here as a copular/predicative element meaning roughly “are (for) me / belong to me” or “are like (my) …” — syntactically the clause says “Your host — all (are) my servants.”
segments.s677-g542: به دل is a locative phrase “in (the) heart” (here: in my heart). زنده و مردگان sets up a contrast “the living and the dead”; the predicate منند is repeated (elliptically) from the previous clause, so the sense is “(they are) alive in my heart, and (even) the dead are (mine).” Word order places the locative first for emphasis.
segments.s677-g543: ز تو = “from you” (source). لختکی is an adverb/adjectival phrase meaning “for a little while / briefly” (poetic). روشنی is the noun “light/brightness/relief,” and یافتند is the past plural “they found/obtained.” The clause means “They found a little/brief light because of you.”
segments.s677-g544: بدین سان is an adverbial phrase “in this manner / thus” (here the element بدین is formed from the prefix ب- + demonstrative and سان “way”). سر از + noun is a concrete phrase “(their) heads from …,” and برتافتند is a past plural verb meaning “they turned/removed/averted” (an older/poetic form). The whole line: “Thus they turned their heads away from داد,” i.e. they lifted/averted their heads from their previous suffering/affliction — the phrase is compact and poetic with some archaic verbs and fixed collocations.
segments.s677-g457: همی is a classical/poetic continual/emphatic marker meaning ‘always/ever/already’ and here modifies the whole verbal clause. The nominal phrase بوی تاج (literally “the scent of the crown”) is fronted as the semantic subject/phenomenon, followed by the verb آید (‘comes’). از introduces the source and مغفرم is مغفر + -م (1st‑person possessive) meaning ‘my cap/covering’ (i.e. a simple head‑covering or helmet).
segments.s677-g458: The clause mirrors the previous one. همی again provides habitual/emphatic sense. تخت عاج is a compound where تخت (throne) is specified by عاج (ivory) → “ivory throne.” آید is the verb ‘comes’; از + خنجرم (‘from my dagger’) marks the source, with the possessive suffix -م.
segments.s677-g459: اگر introduces a conditional protasis. با تو here marks the relation ‘toward/with respect to you.’ یک پشه is the (small) subject ‘a single gnat/gnat‑like insect,’ followed by کین as the thing brought (object: ‘hatred/vengeance’) and the verb آورد (classical past form used for potential/conditional: ‘would bring’ / ‘brought’ in sense of causing).
segments.s677-g460: ز is the poetic variant of از ‘from.’ تختت is تخت + ‑ت (2nd‑person possessive ‘your throne’). به روی زمین is a locative phrase ‘onto the surface of the earth’ (به = onto/toward, روی = surface/face, زمین = earth/ground). The verb آورد is repeated at the end and completes the motion sense ‘(it) would bring/drag (it).’
segments.s677-g461: بدو is the prepositional phrase ‘to him’ placed before the verb. گفت is the speech verb ‘said’ (note: the provided senses list did not include the lexical sense ‘to say’). خسرو is the subject name. که‌ای here functions as a vocative/archaic address (‘O …’) and شوم پی is a nominal phrase meaning ‘ill‑omened follower’ (شوم = ill‑omened, پی = one following / pursuer); the adjective–noun order is poetically inverted for force.
segments.s677-g462: چرا is the interrogative ‘why’ starting the question that follows. یاد گرگین is the noun phrase ‘the memory/remembrance of Gorgin’ (گرگین here is the proper name). نگیری is the negative present/subjunctive form of گرفتن (‘do not take / do you not take/keep’); it expresses reproach ‘do you not take/remember…’. بری is lexically unclear in the provided senses for this context (it does not cleanly match ‘to you’ or an adverbial here), so it is best treated as a textual/poetic particle or variant that the glossary senses do not cover.
segments.s677-g463: که here is the relative/conjunctive particle introducing the clause (‘who/that’). اندر is the older/pre‑poetic form of از/در meaning ‘in/within.’ جهان is ‘the world.’ بود is the past form ‘was,’ and the coordinating و links to تختش نبود — تختش is تخت + ‑ش (‘his throne’) and نبود is the negative past ‘was not.’
segments.s677-g464: A simple coordinate list of the lacks: بزرگی ‘greatness’ (noun), و links, اورنگ is a word for royal glory / the seat/throne (here the sense ‘worldly glory’ is appropriate), و links again, بختش is بخت + ‑ش (‘his fortune’). The final نبود again negates the whole list: ‘he had no greatness, nor glory, nor fortune.’
segments.s677-g449: تو is the 2nd-person singular pronoun 'you'. خودکامه is the noun/adj 'autocrat/tyrant' (here object). را‌گر is a fused sequence of the object marker را plus the conditional گر, so the phrase marks 'the autocrat' and immediately introduces a conditional: 'if'. ندانی is the present (here negative) form 'you do not know'. شمار in this position is a noun meaning 'number' or 'reckoning' ('if you do not know the number/reckoning of tyrants').
segments.s677-g450: برو is an imperative 'go'. چارصد is the numeral 'four hundred'. بار is used as 'times/occasions' (i.e. 'times over'). بشمر is the present-subjunctive/imperative form 'count' (b- prefix + شمر), governing the following numeral; هزار is the object 'a thousand'. Together چارصد بار بشمر هزار literally orders: 'count a thousand four hundred times' (a hyperbolic way of saying 'count them many times over').
segments.s677-g451: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from'. پیلان is the plural of پیل 'elephant' (here 'of/among the elephants'). جنگی is an adjective 'warlike' or 'military' describing those elephants (i.e. 'war‑elephants' or 'a war force'). هزار و دویست is the numeral phrase 'one thousand and two hundred' (1,200) quantifying them; the clause is a nominal (ellipsis of a verb is typical in poetry).
segments.s677-g452: The first که is a relative/complementizer 'which/that'. گفتی here functions as 'you said' (introducing what was previously asserted). The second که introduces the content of that remark: بر 'on/upon', راه 'the road', برجای 'in place / holding position' (برجای = be in position), and نیست is the negation 'is not'. So the clause says 'which, as you said, does not hold its place on the road.'
segments.s677-g453: هزیمت is the noun 'rout/defeat'. گرفت is the past tense verb 'took/received' (here 'suffered' in combination with هزیمت → 'was routed'). آن سپاه بزرگ is the noun phrase 'that great army' (آن determiner + سپاه 'army' + بزرگ 'great'), placed after the verb as is common in poetic word order.
segments.s677-g454: من is 'I'. از پس is a prepositional phrase 'from after / in pursuit of' (از + پس). خروشان is a participial/adverbial adjective 'roaring/tumultuous' describing the pursuit or the speaker's advance. چو is the simile particle 'like/as'. دیو is 'demon/monster' used figuratively; سترگ is an adjective 'great/huge' modifying دیو. The whole gives a simile: 'I, following in the roar, like a mighty demon.'
segments.s677-g455: چنان دان is an idiomatic imperative 'consider/know it thus' (چنان 'so/thus' + دان imperative of 'to know/consider'). که introduces the content clause. کس بی‌هنر در جهان is an indefinite noun phrase 'any person unskilled in the world' (کس 'anyone/person' + بی‌هنر 'unskilled' + در جهان 'in the world'); together introducing the general statement that follows.
segments.s677-g456: به خیره is an adverbial phrase 'by/with a stare' (به + خیره 'staring/astounded look'). نجوید is the present negative 3rd‑person singular of جستن/جَوِيْدن 'to seek/desire' → 'does not seek' or generically 'does not attain/seeks not'. نشست is a noun 'seat/sitting/placing' and مهان 'guests' modifies it ('the guests' seat' or 'place among guests'). So the clause states that such an unskilled person does not, by staring, claim the guests' seat.
segments.s677-g345: بیاییم is a hortative first-person plural ("let us come"), used to invite joint action; و simply links clauses. دل را puts the definite object (دل) before the verb with the object marker را. ترازو کردن is a compound verb meaning "to weigh," and کنیم is the present-subjunctive / hortatory first-person plural of کردَن used here to form the verbal phrase "let us weigh."
segments.s677-g346: بسنجیم is the present-subjunctive/hortative form of سنجیدن ("let us measure/try/measuring") parallel to کنیم in the previous clause; ونیرو is actually و نیرو ("and strength") with و fused in writing; به marks the beneficiary/target "to/into," بازو is the recipient noun "arm," and کنیم (کردن) again is first-person-plural subjunctive "let us make/put." The two verbs (بسنجیم and کنیم) form a balanced pair: "let us measure and let us put strength into the arm."
segments.s677-g347: چو here introduces a temporal clause meaning "when." هرمز جهاندار is a titled noun phrase "Hormoz, the world‑ruler." و links the next predicate; باداد is an archaic verbal/adjectival form related to دادن ("gave, bestowed") and with بود (past of بودن) forms a past/state comment — effectively "and had bestowed/was bestowing/was the giver." بود is the past copula "was."
segments.s677-g348: زمین و زمانه is the compound subject "earth and the age/the times." بدو is the contracted prepositional pronoun (به + او) meaning "to him/for him." شاد بود is the predicate "was glad/merry." The word order (subject — indirect object — predicate) and the use of بدو (rather than separate به او) are poetic/archaic.
segments.s677-g349: پسر is the subject "the son." بی‌گمان is an adverbial insertion meaning "undoubtedly/without doubt." از پدر is a prepositional phrase "from (his) father." تخت is the direct object "throne," and یافت is the past tense "he obtained/received." The verb appears after its object, a common poetic order.
segments.s677-g350: کلاه و کمر are coordinated direct objects "helmet (or crown) and belt/girdle." یافت repeats the past-tense verb "he obtained"; و هم introduces an additional coordinated clause with هم emphasizing "also"; بخت یافت is an idiom meaning "he found fortune/was fortunate." The repetition of یافت for each coordinate is a stylistic, emphatic choice.
segments.s677-g351: تو‌ای is a poetic/vocative address "O you." The following adjectives پرگناه ("sinful") and فریبنده ("deceptive") modify مرد ("man"), forming a vocative phrase "O sinful, deceitful man." The order places the epithets before the head noun, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s677-g352: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding "man" ("who..."). جستی is second-person past tense of جَستن here meaning "you leapt/rose/advanced" (i.e., "you rose/attacked"); نخستین is a postverbal modifier "first." ز is the poetic preposition "against/from" and precedes the agent/target هرمز; نبرد is the noun "battle," so the clause literally reads "who first rose to/for battle from/against Hormoz." The construction is archaic/poetic but means "who first rose in battle against Hormoz."
segments.s677-g297: This is a simple clause meaning “you make yourself king.” تو is the 2nd-person pronoun (poetic address). شاهی is a substantive meaning “kingship / kingly status” placed before the verb as the thing being made. همی‌سازی is a poetic present-tense verbal form (همی‑ + سازی) meaning “you fashion/make” or “you set up,” and از خویشتن is a prepositional phrase (‘from/for yourself’) with خویشتن the reflexive “yourself.”
segments.s677-g298: که‌گر introduces a conditional clause (که + گر = “if”); مرگت آید is ‘your death comes’ with آید the present/poetic form of ‘come/happen.’ نیابی is the negative present/near‑future 2nd‑person form of یافتن/یابیدن (“you will not / do not find”), and کفن is the direct object “shroud.” The clause therefore gives the result: if death comes, you will not find a shroud.
segments.s677-g299: بدین (here written as ب + دین) functions as a locative/prepositional phrase meaning ‘with/among these’ or ‘in these,’ followed by a coordinated list: اسپ ‘steeds/horse’ and برگستوان کسان ‘strong‑armed people/men’ (برگستوان modifies کسان). The whole segment names the group in which the individual appears.
segments.s677-g300: یکی is an indefinite ‘one / a certain (one).’ خسروی is an adjective/nominative describing a royal or kingly person; برزو is the proper name (in apposition), and نارسان is an epithet ‘fiery/ardent’ following the name. Together they present ‘one kingly figure, Borzu, the fiery.’
segments.s677-g301: A string of negated nouns introduced by نه … و نه …: نه خان و نه مان و نه بوم و نژاد lists things being denied. Each noun is linked by و; the overall meaning is ‘neither X nor Y nor Z nor W.’ These items are being rejected as true attributes or supports of the person/situation described.
segments.s677-g302: یکی شهریاری = ‘one sovereignty/one rulership’ (یکی as ‘one/a single’, شهریاری = ‘sovereignty’). میان پر ز باد is a prepositional/locative modifier: میان ‘in the middle/among’, پر ز = ‘full of’ and باد ‘wind’ — idiomatically ‘blown by the wind’ or ‘insubstantial.’ The phrase qualifies the single sovereignty as being in the middle and full of wind (i.e. unstable or unsubstantial).
segments.s677-g303: بدین begins a instrumental/prepositional phrase ‘with these/by these’ followed by a coordinated list: لشکر ‘army/host’, چیز ‘thing/possessions’, and نامی دروغ ‘a fame/name that is false’ (نامی ‘fame/noted’, دروغ ‘falsehood’ modifies it). The segment lists the resources or pretensions that are being criticized as insufficient.
segments.s677-g304: نگیری is the negative 2nd‑person present/subjunctive form of گرفتن (ن + گیری), meaning ‘you do not / will not take or seize.’ بر تخت شاهی is the prepositional phrase ‘upon the throne of kingship’ (بر = upon, تخت = throne, شاهی = kingship/throne), and فروغ ‘radiance/splendor’ is the quality being aimed at. The clause says that by those means you will not seize the throne’s splendor.
segments.s677-g601: کمان را is the definite object (کمان + object marker را) fronted; به زه is an instrumental/prepositional phrase (به + زه ‘bowstring’) indicating the means; کرد is the simple past 'did/made' used as the main verb; بندوی گرد here functions as a verbal/participial phrase meaning 'made fast/taut' (an archaic compound: بند + وی + گرد) describing the result of his action. Word order is somewhat archaic/poetic: object first, then the instrumental phrase, then the verb and a resultant participial phrase.
segments.s677-g602: به تیر uses به instrumentally ('with/by the arrow'); از هوا روشنایی is a source phrase ('brightness/light from the air/sky') where از marks the source; ببرد is a third‑person singular verbal form of بردن ('take/carry off')—in epic style it functions as the eventive verb complementing the preceding clause, giving 'the arrow carried away brightness from the air.'
segments.s677-g603: بدان is an archaic fused locution (be + an/that) meaning 'to that' or 'to him'; ترک بدساز is a noun phrase 'the ill‑made/ill‑tempered Turk' with بدساز post‑modifying ترک; بهرام (the speaker) is the subject and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'—word order here places the indirect object/recipient (بدان ترک) before the subject and verb, typical of poetic syntax.
segments.s677-g604: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that'); جز is 'except'; خاک تیره is a noun + adjective 'dark earth/dark soil'; مبادت is an optative/negative jussive form (مباد + ت) meaning 'may there not be' or 'should not be'; نهفت is a past/participial form meaning 'hidden' or 'concealed'—together giving the sense 'that nothing except dark earth be/is hidden.'
segments.s677-g605: که گفتت begins reported speech: که (that) + گفتت ('he said to you', where -ت marks 'to you'); the following كه is a complementizer introducing what was said; با شاه is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'with the king'; رزم آزمای is a compound verb/verb‑phrase ('test yourself in battle' or 'try battle')—the whole segment reports 'he told you to test yourself in battle with the king.'
segments.s677-g606: ندیدی is the negative past second person form 'did you not see / you did not see'; مرا (here split in the tokenization as م + را) is the object 'me' (م as the clitic/short form + را object marker); پیش او is a locative phrase 'in his presence / before him'; بر به پای is a periphrastic expression 'on/standing on (one's) feet' (بر = on/upon, به پای = on the foot/feet) describing his posture. The whole segment is a question: 'Did you not see me before him, on my feet?'
segments.s677-g607: پس آمد is a simple past sequence adverb + verb 'then came/arrived'; به لشکرگه is the locative/directional phrase 'to the army‑camp'; خویش + ش is the reflexive/possessive 'his own' (خویش 'own' + clitic ش 'his'); باز is an adverb meaning 'again' or 'back'—together 'then he returned to his own camp.'
segments.s677-g608: روانش is 'his spirit/soul' (روان + possessive ش); پر از is an adjectival construction 'full of' taking a following noun; درد is 'pain' (here emotional/physical suffering); وتن is a fused item meaning 'and the body' (و + تن); پرگداز is a compound adjective 'full of burning / searing' describing the body—so: 'his spirit full of pain and his body searing/aflame.'
segments.s677-g185: هرآن is a temporal adverb here meaning 'whenever' or 'at any moment.' دیو is the noun 'demon' and کاید functions as the verb 'plots/schemes' in a relative clause ('the demon that plots'). زمانش فراز is an elliptic clause: زمانش = 'its time' (possessive -ش) and فراز (literally 'on high / upward') indicates rising or coming at that time.
segments.s677-g186: زبانش is the subject with the enclitic -ش 'its' ('its tongue/speech'). به گفتار is a prepositional phrase 'into/into speech' (به + گفتار) marking the result or medium. گردد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to become' (here: 'becomes'), and دراز is the predicate adjective 'long' — together 'its tongue becomes long in speech' i.e. it speaks at length.
segments.s677-g187: نخواهم is the first‑person negative present of 'خواستن' meaning 'I do not want / I will not wish' and introduces a subordinate clause with که. چون تو means 'like you' (چون = 'like, as'). یکی پهلوان is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/one champion' (یکی = 'one/a certain' modifying پهلوان).
segments.s677-g188: به تندی is an adverbial expression 'by/through haste' (به marking the manner). تبه is a predicate adjective 'wicked/evil' and گردد is 'becomes' (present/subjunctive). The conjunction و links a second predicative adjective, ناتوان 'powerless', understood as also being what the champion 'becomes.'
segments.s677-g189: سزدگر is an impersonal/factive adjective/noun meaning 'fitting/appropriate' (literally 'one to be fitting' → 'it is fitting that'). ز دل marks origin 'from the heart' and خشم بیرون کنی is a verb phrase 'you drive out anger' where کنی is the second‑person present/subjunctive (or jussive) of 'کردن' used here in the protasis/consequence introduced by سزدگر.
segments.s677-g190: نجوشی is the negative/prohibitive or jussive form meaning 'you will not boil/you will not seethe' (from جوشیدن, here emotional boiling). و links the next clause. بر تیزی indicates the means/medium ('with/on sharpness/keenness'), افسون is the object 'charm/enchantment', and کنی is again the second‑person verb 'you do' (subjunctive), so 'you will (or should) not seethe, and with keenness you will work a charm.'
segments.s677-g191: ز here is the poetic preposition 'of/from' used with یاد کن to mark the person to be remembered: 'remember the ...'. دارنده is 'holder/possessor' and دادگر follows as a postpositive adjective/title meaning 'just' or 'just ruler' — altogether 'the just possessor' (i.e. a just ruler). یاد کن is the imperative 'remember' (یاد + کن).
segments.s677-g192: خرد را is the direct object 'wisdom' with the definite/object marker را. بدین یاد is the prepositional/phrastic 'by/with this remembrance' (بدین = به + این 'by this'), where بدین functions adverbially to link to بنیاد کن 'found/establish' (imperative). بنیاد کن is a verbal phrase 'establish/found' with کن the imperative 'do!' — 'establish wisdom on/with this remembrance.'
segments.s677-g209: تو is the 2nd-person singular pronoun 'you'. دانی is the present 2nd-person form of دانستن 'know' (you know). که introduces a subordinate interrogative clause ('who ... is'). بر پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before' (بر + پیش), followed by the demonstrative این + noun بنده 'this servant'; کیست is the interrogative 'who is' formed by کی 'who' + ست (copula) placed at the clause end in typical Persian word order.
segments.s677-g210: کزین is an archaic/poetic contraction meaning 'from this' (از + این). ننگ 'shame' is the subject/cause; بر تاج is a prepositional phrase 'upon/on the crown' (تاج here referring to the crown/sovereignty). باید + verbal form (گریست) expresses necessity/impersonality: 'one must weep' or 'it is necessary to weep' — thus باید گریست = 'one must weep'.
segments.s677-g211: وز is an archaic/poetic preposition here meaning 'from' (similar to از); آنجا 'there' is the locative adverb. سبک شد is a compound of the adjective سبک 'light' + شد 'became' = 'became light / felt relieved' (شد as the auxiliary verb). به جای نماز is a prepositional phrase 'in place of / instead of prayer' (به جای = 'instead of', نماز = 'prayer').
segments.s677-g212: همی‌گفت is an archaic imperfective/past-continuous form meaning 'he kept saying' or 'he would say' (همی indicating continuous aspect). با introduces the addressee/companion 'to/with the Judge' (داور); پاک is an adjective 'pure' modifying داور (postpositive order is common in Persian poetry); راز 'secret' is the direct object. Overall: 'he kept telling the secret to the pure Judge.'
segments.s677-g213: گر is the conditional 'if'. این پادشاهی 'this kingship' is the subject. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; تخم 'seed' = lineage/origin; کیان refers to the Kayan royal house (the Kians). The phrase z-e + noun (ز تخم کیان) postmodifies پادشاهی as 'kingship from the seed of the Kians.'
segments.s677-g214: بخواهد شدن is a verbal phrase: بخواهد (3rd-person singular of خواستن used here as 'wishes/will') + شدن 'to become' = 'wishes to become / desires to be established'. تا introduces a result/purpose-like clause ('so that / (such that)') and is followed by نبندم, the negative present/subjunctive 1st-person form of بستن 'to bind/close' (ن + بندم = 'I will not bind/close'). میان 'between' functions as an adverbial/relational complement. Together: 'If ... wishes to become, I will not put/close myself between (them).' (i.e. I will not interpose.)
segments.s677-g215: پرستنده is a noun 'worshipper / devotee' used predicatively. باشم is the 1st-person subjunctive/optative form of بودن 'to be' here expressing the speaker's resolve or determination ('I shall be / I will be'). به آتشکده is a locative phrase 'at/in the fire-temple' (به + آتشکده). Word order places the predicate noun before the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s677-g216: نخواهم is the negative 1st-person form of خواستن/خواه (here functioning as a modal/future-like auxiliary: 'I will not / I do not wish to'). خورش 'cooked dish / stew' is the object. جز ز introduces the exception 'except from', with ز (poetic از) meaning 'from'. شیر here is 'milk' (not 'lion'), and دده is 'the elder / grandfather' (a provider or named elder). Literally: 'I will take/eat no stew except from the elder's milk.'
segments.s677-g305: ز تو پیش بودند کنداوران is a poetic word order: the preposition ز + pronoun تو ('from/of you' used to mark relation) comes first, then پیش بودند (compound meaning 'were before / stood ahead'), and کنداوران is the plural noun (subject) placed after the verb — a common inversion in epic Persian. بودند is past plural 'were'.
segments.s677-g306: جهانجوی و با گرزهای گران is a phrase modifying کنداوران. جهانجوی is an adjectival/epithet 'world-seeking' attached to the preceding noun; و is the copulative 'and'; با introduces the instrument/attendant phrase 'with'; گرزهای is the plural (indefinite/construct) of 'mace/club' and گران is an adjective 'heavy' describing them.
segments.s677-g307: نجستند شاهی که کهتر بدند: نجستند is the past plural of جستن used here as 'sought' (they sought). شاهی is the direct object 'kingship'. که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'; کهتر is an adjective 'lesser/inferior'; بدند here is used in a subjunctive/hortative sense 'would be' (poetic/off/archaic modality), completing the relative clause 'that would be lesser.'
segments.s677-g308: نه اندر خور تخت و افسر بدند: نه is the negative 'not'; اندر 'in/within' opens the prepositional phrase. خور here is the literal/figurative 'sun' (used metaphorically as shining splendour), تخت 'throne', و 'and', افسر 'diadem/helmet'. بدند is the verb repeated at the end of the line (here functioning as 'they did [not] do/place' or more literally 'they were not' in older poetic usage). The clause denies that they put/placed the sun on throne and diadem.
segments.s677-g309: همی هرزمان سرفرازی به خشم: همی is a frequentative/adverb 'always/continually'. هرزمان here functions adverbially 'at every time / constantly' (no exact matching sense in the provided list, see senses). سرفرازی is a noun 'pride / loftiness / splendour', and به خشم 'into anger' shows the change or motion of that pride into anger.
segments.s677-g310: همی آب خشم اندر آری به چشم: همی 'continually'; آب خشم is a poetic compound 'the water of anger' (i.e., tears or the fluid of wrath). اندر آری is an archaic/poetic present-tense verb phrase meaning 'comes into / wells up in' (آری here is used as a verbal form of آوردن/آمدن in epic diction). به چشم 'into the eye' is the locative. Thus the clause says the 'water of anger' continually comes into the eye.
segments.s677-g311: بجوشد همی بر تنت بدگمان: بجوشد is a present/imperfect third-person singular (poetic) of جوشیدن 'to boil/seethe' and here means 'seethes/boils up'. همی 'always/continually'. بر تنت 'upon your body' (preposition + possessive noun), and بدگمان is a noun/adjective 'suspicion; distrust' indicating what seethes. The verb precedes its adverb and object in the typical epic inverted order.
segments.s677-g312: زمانه به خشم آردت هر زمان: زمانه 'the times / fortune' is the subject. به خشم 'in anger' (a circumstantial phrase). آردت is آرد + ت: third-person singular present 'brings' with the enclitic object -ت 'you' (so 'brings you'). هر زمان 'at every time / always' closes the clause. Together: 'fortune, in anger, will bring you to (or into) anger/distress at every moment.'
